(4) Manual ↻ button: placed in the panel header; calls the regenerate endpoint and reloads the affected panels on success. Spinner during regeneration (disable button to prevent double-click).

(5) Periodic auto-refresh: fire every N minutes (e.g. 20 min) while the tab is active. Gate on !document.hidden — visibility-change listener pauses/resumes the timer. Avoids background gh API calls when the user has switched tabs.

(6) Data-date label: display the report's generation timestamp ('data as of: YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm') in the header. Staleness is now observable — users know whether to click ↻ without guessing.

Complements cached-report-compute-and-schedule (backend: aggregation correctness + scheduler wiring) — together they cover the full pipeline: KPI computation → scheduled refresh → on-demand refresh → live UI freshness. Red flag: panels show stale data even after manual refresh → regenerate endpoint is re-reading cache, not re-running the generator. Provenance: PR #135 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #135: feat(dashboard): manual ↻ refresh + auto-refresh for Token/Issues/PR panels

run-now-state-rearm.l0: Run-now: re-arm nextRunAt=now in state.json; daemon tick executes ≤30s — no new execution surface in the UI.
run-now-state-rearm.l1: PR #132: ▶ Run button calls POST /api/schedules/run → validates (manifest + isSafeArtifactId + enabled) → markJobDue sets nextRunAt=now (pure fn, clones state). Daemon's 30s tick finds job due and runs it. Same mechanism the heartbeat uses to re-arm overdue jobs. UI stays read-only — writes only a 'due' marker (data, not execution). Disabled → 409; unknown → 404. Path safety: writes only the validated agent's schedule-state.json (isPathInsideRoot). describeJob fallback: description → prompt first line → ''.
run-now-state-rearm.value:
Pattern: when a UI needs to trigger a scheduled job on demand, do NOT add execution capability to the UI layer. Instead:

(1) API endpoint POST /api/schedules/run {agent, id}: validate (a) agent matches manifest; (b) id is a real job in agent's schedule.json; (c) job is enabled (else 409); (d) agentRoot passes isPathInsideRoot. Write only to <agentRoot>/.agent-mesh/schedule-state.json.

(2) Pure helper markJobDue(state, id, now): returns shallow-cloned state with nextRunAt=now.toISOString(), running:false. Creates the entry if absent. No I/O — the route does the I/O.

(3) Response 202 {queued:true, runsWithinMs:30000}. UI shows transient 'queued…'; status updates on next poll. Errors: 409 disabled, 404 unknown, 500 write failure.

(4) Daemon's tick (≤30s) finds job due (enabled && !running && nextRunAt ≤ now) and executes. Dashboard never runs anything itself — preserves the daemon's serialization, path-guard, and auth model.

(5) Idempotent: clicking twice is harmless — both set nextRunAt=now; daemon's one-job-at-a-time lock prevents double-run.

(6) Optional: if dashboard-owned scheduler present (--allow-shell), also call scheduler.runNow for immediacy — but the re-arm is the universal mechanism in daemon-owned setup.

(7) describeJob fallback: description field (trimmed) → first non-empty line of prompt (length-bounded) → ''. Include in list-all so /api/schedules carries the description.

Generalizes: any API that triggers daemon-owned work should write a data marker; the daemon reads and executes — no execution surfaces in the UI. Provenance: PR #132 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #132: feat(dashboard): Schedules panel — Run-now button + per-task descriptions

port-scoped-pidfile-single-instance.l0: Single-instance CLI server: port-scoped pidfile, SIGTERM→grace→SIGKILL on relaunch, fail-open I/O, --no-replace opt-out.
port-scoped-pidfile-single-instance.l1: PR #136: dashboard processes piled up on restart — pkill-by-name misses lingering instances sharing the same port. Fix: `<os.tmpdir()>/agent-mesh-dashboard-<port>.pid` records the owner PID. On relaunch: read pidfile → SIGTERM → poll ≤3 s → SIGKILL if still alive → write new pidfile → bind. All I/O is best-effort; a pidfile error never blocks launch. `--no-replace` lets scripts refuse an already-running instance. Remove pidfile on clean exit.
port-scoped-pidfile-single-instance.value:
Apply to any long-running CLI server that users repeatedly restart (dashboard, dev-server, proxy).

(1) Pidfile location: `<os.tmpdir()>/<name>-<port>.pid`. Port-scoped so two processes on different ports coexist safely.

(2) Reap sequence on launch: read pidfile → check `isAlive(pid)` → SIGTERM → poll every 100 ms for ≤3 s → SIGKILL if process still alive → write new pidfile → bind.

(3) Never kill an untracked holder: only kill a PID recorded in this tool's own pidfile. An unrecorded port-holder surfaces as `EADDRINUSE` — actionable error, not a silent kill.

(4) Fail-open I/O: wrap every pidfile read/write and signal send in try/catch. A missing, stale, or unwritable pidfile must never prevent launch — only `--no-replace` may refuse.

(5) `--no-replace` flag: read pidfile, if a live PID exists → exit 1 with a message naming the PID and port. Use in scripted/CI contexts where a double-launch is always an error.

(6) Remove pidfile on clean exit and uncaught fatal (process.on('exit', …)).

(7) Inject I/O and signal deps for hermetic tests — mock the process table to exercise stale/graceful/SIGKILL/best-effort branches without spawning real processes. PR #136 ships 8 tests in test/dashboard-single-instance.test.js covering all branches. Implementation: src/dashboard/single-instance.js. Provenance: PR #136 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #136: feat(dashboard): single-instance — reap prior instance on launch

git-remote-envvar-fallback.l0: Env var fallback: parse owner/repo from git remote when daemon-set var is absent in manual/interactive runs.
git-remote-envvar-fallback.l1: PR #138: POST /api/daily/refresh failed (exit 2) from a manually-launched dashboard — DEV_SOCIETY_REPO is set only by the launchd service, not interactive processes. Fix: repoSlugFromRemote() shells `git config --get remote.origin.url` and parses both https (https://github.com/o/r.git) and ssh (git@github.com:o/r) forms, stripping .git and whitespace, throwing on empty. No behavior change when the var is already set. Tests: https/ssh/.git-suffix/trailing-newline/empty — 4 new cases.
git-remote-envvar-fallback.value: Pattern for any env var typically set by a service launcher (launchd/systemd): (1) try process.env.VAR first; (2) if absent, run `git config --get remote.origin.url`; (3) parse both https (https://github.com/owner/repo.git) and ssh (git@github.com:owner/repo) URL forms; (4) strip trailing .git suffix and whitespace; (5) throw on empty/unparseable. Export the parser as a named helper (e.g. repoSlugFromRemote) for unit testing. Test cases: https URL / ssh URL / .git suffix / trailing newline / empty string. Ensures the feature works in both daemon-managed and manual sessions without requiring the operator to set extra env vars. Provenance: PR #138 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #138: fix(dashboard): daily/refresh derives DEV_SOCIETY_REPO from git remote

terminal-close-escalate-sweep.l0: Daemon closes terminal-labelled issues; escalation sweep converts stuck PRs to deduped self-closing needs-triage issues.
terminal-close-escalate-sweep.l1: PR #158 plugged two silent-hang holes. (1) isTerminalState: daemon sweep top-of-tick closes any open issue with a terminal label (done/rejected/wontfix/duplicate/invalid) — previously required manual close. (2) dev-mesh-escalate sweep: PR stuck past staleMs (3h default) in DIRTY/UNKNOWN/UNSTABLE/CHANGES_REQUESTED, or CLEAN-without-APPROVED → open a deduped needs-triage issue that flows into the Triager pipeline; self-close on recovery. Excludes drafts, forks, memory:promote, BLOCKED/BEHIND, CLEAN+APPROVED. Cron 12,42; gated by AUTOMERGE_ENABLED; issues:write only.
terminal-close-escalate-sweep.value:
Two-part self-healing pattern for no-silent-hangs:

**Part 1 — auto-close terminal issues**: Add isTerminalState(issue) as the FIRST check in the daemon's sweep tick. Terminal labels: done, rejected, wontfix, duplicate, invalid. Call `gh issue close N` without removing the label. Run before any routing logic so closed issues never consume processing budget.

**Part 2 — PR escalation sweep**: For each non-excluded PR stuck past staleMs: (a) classify stuck state (mergeability ∈ {DIRTY,UNKNOWN,UNSTABLE}; reviewDecision=CHANGES_REQUESTED; CLEAN-without-APPROVED); (b) title-dedup before creating a needs-triage issue ('stuck (PR #N — class)'); (c) self-close when PR recovers (MERGEABLE+APPROVED or PR merged/closed).

Exclusions (each prevents a false-positive): draft PRs; fork PRs (isCrossRepository=true); memory:promote label; BLOCKED/blocked-by-* label; BEHIND mergeability (mergefix owns); CLEAN+APPROVED (auto-merge owns).

Config: DEV_MESH_PR_STALE_MS (default 3h); AUTOMERGE_ENABLED gate (see default-off-three-layer-kill-switch); cron 12,42 offset from other sweeps. Permissions: issues:write only.

Tests: truth table for isEscalatable() covering all mergeState × reviewDecision × exclusion-label combos; open/dedup/self-close/--dry-run with injected gh shim; YAML lint for exclusion-list completeness.

Related: pr-janitor-gap-classes (complements — janitor handles scope-drift, UNKNOWN nudge, unlabelled-bot PRs). Provenance: PR #158 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #158: feat(self-healing): close terminal issues + escalate stuck PRs (no silent hangs)

agent-prompt-no-user-refs.l0: User-controlled refs must not be in agent prompt text — only YAML env:/run:; agent pushes HEAD, YAML resolves the ref.
agent-prompt-no-user-refs.l1: PR #160: (1) `${{ inputs.task }}` in run: — dispatch caller controls shell; (2) embedding literal `$PR_BRANCH` in prompt text — git allows `$(cmd)` in branch names, so model-echoed refs are injection. Fix: attacker-controlled data lives ONLY in YAML env:/run:. For repair-loop agents: YAML pre-step checks out branch; agent edits/commits; YAML post-step pushes `git push origin HEAD:$PR_BRANCH` — agent never sees the ref. Extends gh-actions-ref-injection and bash-tool-simple-expansion-hoist.
agent-prompt-no-user-refs.value:
Two injection surfaces closed by PR #160 — same root cause:

(1) workflow_dispatch inputs in run: (`${{ inputs.task }}` inline → shell injection). Fix: `env: TASK: ${{ inputs.task }}` then `"$TASK"` in shell. Applies to inputs.* and vars.* used as positional args (`--model ${{ vars.X }}` is unsafe; `--model "$VAR"` after env: is safe). Extends gh-actions-ref-injection (which covers head.ref from PRs — same fix, different source).

(2) Branch refs in agent prompt text. When simple_expansion blocks `$PR_BRANCH` in Bash tool calls, the naive fix is embedding the literal branch name in the agent prompt. This is CWE-78: git allows `$(cmd)` in branch names, so a crafted ref in prompt text → model echoes it to shell → command injection. Fix: the ref NEVER appears in prompt text.

Canonical pattern for repair-loop agents:
1. YAML run: pre-step — `git checkout "$PR_BRANCH"` (safe: shell var, not template interpolation); `git merge origin/main`.
2. Drive Coder step — agent receives context (conflict files, PR description), edits/resolves, commits. Agent sees no ref string.
3. YAML run: post-step — `git push origin HEAD:"$PR_BRANCH"` (shell var only).

Agent never touches the ref — it pushes HEAD. The YAML step holds the only string copy of the branch name.

Rule: attacker-controlled data (user inputs, branch names, PR titles) must never transit: agent prompt → model output → shell. It must live exclusively in YAML env:/run:. Any workaround that embeds it in prompt text to bypass simple_expansion creates a prompt-injection-to-shell path.

Related: gh-actions-ref-injection (head.ref in run:), bash-tool-simple-expansion-hoist (hoist git ops to YAML env:). Provenance: PR #160 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #160: fix(ci): close mesh-detected workflow holes — shell injection + broken repair loops

sandbox-host-cli-parity.l0: Add project CLI verbs for ops the sandbox agent can't run — shared vocabulary for agent and human, no round-trip paste.
sandbox-host-cli-parity.l1: When a sandboxed agent can't access `gh` (auth/PATH), a round-trip paste loop emerges: agent asks → human runs → pastes output → agent continues. Fix: add project CLI verbs wrapping those ops. Both sandbox agent and human share one command vocabulary — no divergent interfaces. Side benefit: lazy-import verb modules at dispatch so the hot entrypoint (serve-peer-bridge) stays cheap. PR #153 shipped dev-society status/ledger + issue label as Phase 1 (read-mostly); write/restart deferred to Phase 2 to limit blast radius.
sandbox-host-cli-parity.value:
Pattern for closing the sandbox–host interface gap:

(1) Identify the gap: list ops the sandboxed agent frequently needs but can't run directly (e.g. `gh issue list`, `cat .dev-society/ledger.jsonl`, `ls .dev-society/work/`). Any op requiring auth tokens not available in the sandbox, or tools not on the sandbox PATH, is a round-trip candidate.

(2) Add project CLI verbs wrapping them: use the existing project binary (e.g. `agent-mesh`) to add a `<namespace> <verb>` surface. Both the sandbox agent (permitted to run `node ./bin/agent-mesh.js ...` in its Bash tool) and the host human use the same commands. Examples from PR #153:
  - `agent-mesh dev-society status [--repo OWNER/REPO]` — daemon log + ledger + active worktrees + queue
  - `agent-mesh dev-society ledger [--last N]` — pretty-printed ledger with edges and timing
  - `agent-mesh issue label <num> [--add L]... [--remove L]...` — wrapper over `gh issue edit`

(3) Lazy-import the verb module: inside the CLI dispatch (e.g. `src/cli.js`), import the subcommand module lazily inside the case handler. This keeps the hot-path entrypoint cheap on every other invocation (e.g. serve-peer-bridge cost stays at ~7ms).

(4) Phase writes separately: Phase 1 = read-mostly verbs (status, ledger, label flip). Phase 2 = write/restart verbs (daemon restart, pr nudge, workflow run). Starting read-only limits blast radius and validates the architecture before adding write authority.

(5) --repo flag for auth-gated ops: verbs calling `gh` need a `--repo OWNER/REPO` arg (or derive from git remote — see git-remote-envvar-fallback). Read-only paths (daemon log, ledger) work without auth.

Symptom of the gap: human pastes raw `gh issue list` or `cat ledger.jsonl` into chat so the agent can process it. Fix: project CLI verb the agent invokes directly. Provenance: PR #153 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #153: feat(cli): phase-1 unified mesh ops (dev-society + issue verbs)

issue-gate-pr-merge.l0: Auto-merge reads only PR fields — stamp blocked-by-issue via cron sweep when closing issue is blocked/rejected.
issue-gate-pr-merge.l1: isAutoMergeable checks only PR-level fields — so a PR closing a blocked/rejected issue can satisfy CLEAN+APPROVED and auto-merge. Issue labels gated work-start, not merge. Fix (PR #156): a cron sweep ('2,17,32,47 * * * *', 5 min before automerge ticks) resolves closingIssuesReferences per open PR, idempotently stamps/removes blocked-by-issue. isAutoMergeable now includes blocked-by-issue as a hold label. Safety: sweep owns ONLY its label — never touches human-set do-not-merge/hold/wip. PRs with no linked issue are ungated.
issue-gate-pr-merge.value:
Three-component pattern for coupling PR auto-merge to linked-issue state:

(1) Pure gate fn shouldHoldForIssues(closingIssues): check each closingIssuesReference's labels against a blocking set (blocked/rejected/wontfix/duplicate). Returns true if any match. No I/O — pure predicate, unit-testable and extensible without touching the merge path.

(2) Cron sweep (dev-mesh-issue-gate.yml, cron: '2,17,32,47 * * * *' — offset 5 min before automerge's :00/:15/:30/:45 ticks): for each open same-repo PR, call gateDecision → add/remove/noop for blocked-by-issue. Idempotent. Gated by AUTOMERGE_ENABLED (same kill switch as the merge sweep). Least-privilege: pull-requests:write + issues:read + contents:read only.

(3) Hold-label rule in isAutoMergeable extended to include blocked-by-issue — the one new entry in the hold list. No merge-decision logic change beyond this.

Safety invariants:
- Sweep owns ONLY blocked-by-issue — never touches human-set labels (do-not-merge, hold, wip). If a human removes blocked-by-issue, the next sweep re-stamps it (source of truth is the linked issue state, not the label).
- PRs with no closingIssuesReferences are ungated — chore/docs/memory PRs merge normally.
- Manual gh pr merge bypasses label checks (documented trade-off; hard enforcement needs a branch-protection required check — out of scope).

Test surface: test/issue-gate.test.js (pure shouldHoldForIssues/gateDecision), test/issue-gate-sweep.test.js (sweep orchestration), test/issue-gate-workflow.test.js (YAML structure lint), + blocked-by-issue case in test/automerge-eligibility.test.js.

Provenance: PR #156 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #156: feat(automerge): gate PR merge on linked-issue state

swebench-l5-phase-split.l0: L5 external benchmark: ship ask-mode Phase 1 independently; gate do-mode Phase 2 on prerequisite feature.
swebench-l5-phase-split.l1: PR #163 (Issue #98) adds SWE-bench as an L5 eval tier (external comparability vs. OpenHands 72%, SWE-agent 65%). Two-phase split: Phase 1 ships ask-mode with text-match/keyword scoring — no Docker, no external CLI, hermetically testable. Phase 2 (do-mode, Docker swebench-cli) skipped until issue #97 (do-mode peer delegation) lands. Pattern: `phase` field in task descriptors; `do_required` tasks return {pass:false, skipped:true}. Missing `swebench` binary → exit 0 (same skip-guard as L3/L4). L5 reuses eval/harness.mjs — entirely additive, no src/ changes.
swebench-l5-phase-split.value:
Pattern for adding an external benchmark tier (L5) without breaking zero-deps or blocking on harder prerequisites:

(1) Phase-split by mode: define Phase 1 (ask-only, text-match scoring) and Phase 2 (do-mode, Docker/external CLI) as separate named phases. Ship Phase 1 independently; document Phase 2's prerequisite feature in skip messages.

(2) Task descriptor schema: add a `phase` field (1 or 2) and topology marker (`'ask_only'`/`'do_required'`). Check both: `if (task.phase === 2 || task.topology === 'do_required')` → skip with structured `{pass:false, skipped:true, detail:'do_required: Phase 2'}`. Never silently drop tasks.

(3) External CLI skip-guard: check binary on PATH before Phase 2 runs. Absent → exit 0 with warning (same pattern as L3/L4 skip-guards). Docker is a CI runner config, not an in-repo dep — zero-deps ethos preserved.

(4) Reuse eval substrate: `buildMesh`/`driveAgent`/`cleanupMesh` from eval/harness.mjs is the common substrate for all eval tiers (L2–L5). New tier adds only scripts/eval-swebench.mjs + eval/swebench/ — no src/ changes, no new dependencies.

(5) Curate task corpus separately from harness code: ship a placeholder `mesh-bench.json` (empty array) and log 'mesh-bench task list is empty — human curation needed' when dry. Task selection (20–50 tasks requiring multi-peer coordination) is a human-review step — do not block harness merge on curation.

Provenance: PR #163 / Issue #98 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #163: feat(eval): SWE-bench L5 eval harness — Phase 1 ask-mode (issue #98)

credential-forwarding-all-env-sites.l0: OAuth in bridgeEnv: patch ALL independent constructors — buildBridgeEnv fix alone misses doctor's own env builder.
credential-forwarding-all-env-sites.l1: PR #166 added ${CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN} to buildBridgeEnv (demo serve template + delegate-time bridge). PR #169 found doctor.js builds its own bridgeEnv block independently — token absent on the doctor-persisted path caused 'Not logged in' on trio two-hop. A shared-helper fix silently leaves co-constructors unpatched; simpler tests pass while the doctor-wired multi-hop path fails.
credential-forwarding-all-env-sites.value:
When adding a credential placeholder (e.g. ${CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN}) to a bridge or worker env block, locate ALL independent construction sites — not just the shared helper. Three distinct paths build bridgeEnv in the A2A bridge: (1) serve template; (2) delegate-time buildBridgeEnv; (3) doctor's own bridgeEnv block in src/builder/doctor.js. Each must carry the placeholder.

Pattern: (a) add the credential to the shared helper; (b) grep the codebase for the env-variable name AND for 'bridgeEnv' to find co-constructors; (c) update each; (d) add a test asserting the doctor-persisted entry carries the placeholder (test/doctor-bridge.test.js does this).

Diagnostic signal: failure reproduces only on multi-hop scenarios exercising doctor-generated wiring (e.g. trio two-hop) but not on simpler demo-e2e smoke tests — the missing site is whichever path those simpler tests don't exercise.

Provenance: PR #166 (partial — buildBridgeEnv only) + PR #169 (2026-06-20, doctor path — completes all three bridge-env sites, closes #150).

provenance.source: PR #169: fix(doctor): forward OAuth token in bridge entry — fixes trio two-hop (#150)

a2a-http-dual-transport-dispatch.l0: A2A HTTP: type peers in registry, unified client dispatch, recursion threaded as headers — one shared JSON-RPC core.
a2a-http-dual-transport-dispatch.l1: PR #151: adds HTTP/JSON-RPC to the stdio A2A mesh. Three-part pattern: (1) type the peer at registry-normalization time ('http'|'stdio'); (2) a single createA2AClient dispatches to HttpClientSession (fetch, per-request) or StdioClientSession (long-lived spawn) — the caller never selects a transport; (3) AGENT_MESH_PATH/DEPTH thread as X-AgentMesh-Path/X-AgentMesh-Depth headers so recursion-guard/depth-budget work identically on both transports. HTTP servers bind 127.0.0.1 only (auth/TLS deferred); mixed http+stdio registries work. AgentCard reports protocolBinding:'JSONRPC' for HTTP peers.
a2a-http-dual-transport-dispatch.value:
Pattern for adding HTTP transport to a stdio-based JSON-RPC protocol (same core, two framing layers):

(1) Type the peer at registry normalization: normalizeRegistry() inspects the peer record — if it has `url:` and no `command:`, it becomes type:'http'; otherwise type:'stdio'. Mixed registries (http + stdio peers in the same registry.json) work without any caller changes.

(2) Unified client entry point: createA2AClient(registry) returns a single client whose .send(peer, msg) dispatches to HttpClientSession (per-request fetch, stateless) or StdioClientSession (long-lived process, stateful) based on peer.type. The caller never inspects the type — transport is purely a registry-level detail.

(3) Thread recursion state across the HTTP boundary: AGENT_MESH_PATH and AGENT_MESH_DEPTH — normally passed as child process env — become X-AgentMesh-Path and X-AgentMesh-Depth HTTP headers. The receiving server extracts them from headers and injects into the worker env. Cycle-guard and depth-budget logic are transport-agnostic; a multi-hop mesh mixing stdio and HTTP enforces the same invariants end-to-end.

(4) Server binding: HTTP servers bind 127.0.0.1 by default. Auth and TLS are explicitly deferred to v2 — document this limitation in the PR body so reviewers can assess the threat model consciously.

(5) AgentCard: buildAgentCard accepts a transport param; HTTP cards report protocolBinding:'JSONRPC' for A2A interop.

(6) Test split — 4 hermetic files isolating one layer each: http-server (request/response, error handling), http-client (session lifecycle), http-registry (normalizeRegistry typed peers), http-recursion (header propagation + depth enforcement). Clean split means failures point to the exact layer.

Invariant: the JSON-RPC core (message validation, Task construction, agentmesh/* metadata) is NOT duplicated. The transport wraps the shared core — any core change applies to both transports automatically. Provenance: PR #151 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #151: feat(a2a): HTTP/JSON-RPC transport binding (issue #141)

oauth-token-nested-worker-propagation.l0: Nested MCP worker env-token auth: claude doesn't auto-pass CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN — use ${VAR} in peer env.
oauth-token-nested-worker-propagation.l1: PR #166 / Issue #150: claude does NOT forward CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN to MCP-server subprocesses. Keychain auth propagates via HOME so local dev works; env-token auth (CI/headless) fails — nested worker gets 'Not logged in'. Fix: embed literal ${CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN} placeholder in the peer env block (buildBridgeEnv + .mcp.json templates); claude expands from parent env at spawn — secret never written to disk. Unset var → omitted, keychain unaffected.
oauth-token-nested-worker-propagation.value:
Apply to any nested peer mesh agent running under env-token auth (CI, headless, server deployments):

(1) Root cause: claude does NOT auto-forward CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN to spawned MCP-server child processes. Under keychain auth this is masked (all children inherit HOME). Under env-token auth (CI runner, prod server), the nested worker's claude -p starts unauthenticated → all delegations fail with status:error.

(2) Fix: In every peer MCP config entry that may run a claude -p worker, add the placeholder to the env block:
  "CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN": "${CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN}"
claude expands the ${VAR} placeholder from the PARENT worker's process env at spawn time. The literal secret NEVER appears in any file on disk. Unset var → omitted — keychain auth completely unaffected.

(3) Two auth paths, both must work:
- Keychain (local dev): HOME propagates to all children. Fix is a no-op.
- Env-token (CI/headless): propagation is explicit. The ${VAR} placeholder is the bridge.

(4) Scope: applies to buildBridgeEnv in mesh-mcp.js (peer-bridge entry — covers all real mesh A2A delegation) AND .mcp.json.template for demo/eval meshes.

(5) Test: verify buildBridgeEnv forwards the placeholder, never the resolved literal. Assertion: assert(!JSON.stringify(env).includes(process.env.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN)).

Provenance: PR #166 (2026-06-20), Issue #150.

provenance.source: PR #166: fix(mesh): forward OAuth token to nested workers (env-token auth) — #150 root cause

mir-deterministic-backlog-bridge.l0: Assessment→task bridge: pure pipeline converts test/eval artifacts to deduped issues; host files, LLM never does.
mir-deterministic-backlog-bridge.l1: PR #165: the self-evolve loop had no 'assessment→task' bridge — L0–L4 tiers wrote JSON artifacts with no systematic consumer. Fix: pure pipeline (metrics→aggregate→baseline→policy→render→issues) produces a mir.json filing plan; the daemon builtin tester-suite-run or CI executes it as a host action — never an ask-mode MCP call. Hard signals (red tests, I1–I7 failures, error/timeout run-logs) always file; soft metric regressions only file past a direction-aware noise band. finding.id→issueNumber ledger deduplicates across nightly runs.
mir-deterministic-backlog-bridge.value:
Pattern: closing the evidence→backlog gap in an autonomous agent society.

(1) Pure pipeline: separate evidence-reading from issue-filing via pure functions. collect.js gathers artifacts (L0 test JSON, L2–L3 scorecard JSON, run-log JSONL); metrics.js extracts scalars; aggregate.js computes summaries; baseline.js diffs against the last-run baseline (carried in mir.json's ledger); policy.js classifies findings (hard/soft, severity); render.js builds the report; issues.js produces a filing plan — never executes it.

(2) Host-action execution: the daemon builtin (tester-suite-run in schedule.json) or a CI job reads the filing plan and calls gh issue create. Ask-only agents can read mir.json as data; they cannot trigger the filing. This preserves the ask-mode safety invariant: agents inspect findings, host/CI acts on them.

(3) Two-tier gate: hard signals (any failing test, any failed security invariant, any error/timeout run-log) always file. Soft signals (metric regressions) file only when the signed delta exceeds the noise band (AGENT_MESH_MIR_NOISE_BAND_PCT). Prevents alert fatigue while capturing real regressions.

(4) Ledger-based dedup: maintain a finding.id→issueNumber mapping. Before filing, check: if finding.id already maps to an open issue, skip. This ensures each finding appears as exactly one open issue regardless of how many nightly runs have passed.

(5) Additive producer hook: if a tier doesn't persist results as JSON, add an opt-in --json <path> flag (additive, default unchanged). For L0: run-all-tests.mjs --json <path> writes {at, results[], summary}. Existing stdout output is untouched.

(6) Finding-id safety: validate finding.id against MIR_ID_RE (safe characters only) before using it as a GitHub label or issue marker — prevents label injection from crafted scorecard outputs.

Provenance: PR #165 (2026-06-20).

provenance.source: PR #165: feat(mir): Mesh Improvement Report — Tester-owned self-evolve signal

graph-two-layers-capability-vs-observed.l0: Graph: split capability layer (who can) from observed layer (who is) — conflating them hides cross-plane communication.
graph-two-layers-capability-vs-observed.l1: PR #171: graph only rendered peer-wire capability edges, hiding real traffic between agents with no formal wire. Two honest layers: (1) capability — who can delegate (faint dashed, /api/mesh); (2) observed — who is communicating (persistent teal edges, /api/activity), rendered whether or not a wire exists. Symptom: 'links are not accurate — agents can still talk without wires connected.' Reconcile logic is a pure module (reconcileObserved(prevKeys, edges) → {add,update,remove}) unit-tested without DOM; graph-view.js is the thin impure shell.
graph-two-layers-capability-vs-observed.value:
Pattern for multi-agent graph visualizations with layered truth:

(1) Two-layer model: render BOTH capability edges (who has wires — from the mesh registry/peer config) AND observed edges (who is communicating — from actual traffic logs). Never collapse them into one layer. Symptom of collapse: 'the links between agents are not accurate — they can still talk without wires connected.'

(2) Visual distinction: capability edges = faint + dashed (always-present, low-key). Observed edges = solid + dynamic (teal when active, faint when recently-seen, removed when aged out of the activity window). Legend is mandatory: readers must know which layer they are reading.

(3) Observed edges render regardless of whether a capability wire exists: cross-plane communication (daemon-driven delegation, GitHub-mediated handoffs, out-of-band calls) must show up even with no formal wire. Formal wires document intent; observed edges document reality.

(4) Data-driven aging — no client-side fade timers: age is derived from the activity window timestamp in the data source (/api/activity). Client polls data; renderer applies the current state. Avoids drift between visual state and actual signal.

(5) Pure reconcile function: the set-diff logic (reconcileObserved(prevKeys, edges) → {add, update, remove}) belongs in a standalone module with no DOM imports. Unit-testable with node --test and no browser. The impure shell (graph-view.js) applies the diff to the SVG. Matches the pure-core/impure-shell architectural split.

(6) No new graph nodes from observed edges: observed layer adds edges, never nodes. Nodes represent known agents in the registry. An unknown agent appearing as a node would be alarming and should be a separate, explicit concern.

Provenance: PR #171 (2026-06-20), spec: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-19-mesh-graph-both-layers-design.md.

provenance.source: PR #171: feat(graph): both-layers delegation graph — capability wires + observed traffic

agent-reasoning-host-action-split.l0: Agent reasons + emits JSON; host builtin files GitHub issues — cognitive vs. side-effect, not daemon vs. Actions.
agent-reasoning-host-action-split.l1: PR #178 concluded WebSearch/issue-filing required GitHub Actions. PR #180 supersedes: an ask-mode Analyst in the daemon uses WebSearch (manifest webTools:true) + peer delegation, emits structured JSON. A deterministic daemon builtin (analyst-daily-review) parses JSON and calls gh issue create host-side. Agent never touches GitHub. Correct split: agent does cognition (read, search, analyze, emit result JSON); host action performs side effects. Both CAN live in the daemon.
agent-reasoning-host-action-split.value:
Placement decision (supersedes capability-bound-schedule-placement from PR #178):

(1) The split is cognitive-vs-side-effect, NOT daemon-vs-Actions:
- AGENT layer: READ_TOOLS + opt-in WebSearch/WebFetch (manifest webTools:true, ask-only, never on the digest/collection route). May delegate to peers via peer bridge. Emits structured JSON — no GitHub mutations ever.
- HOST layer: deterministic builtin fn (daemon schedule.json kind:builtin) or CI step. Reads agent JSON, deduplicates, calls gh issue create/close. No LLM.

(2) Dedup is host-side only: the builtin checks existing issues by regex match, not the model. Model never reads issue bodies. Files ≤2 genuinely new ideas/run.

(3) Web grant is operator-owned: webTools:true in the agent's manifest (mesh.json), not self-declared in agent.json. Ask-only. Not applied on the digest/collect route.

(4) Peer bridge for compound reasoning: Analyst delegates to Tester peer (ask mode) for eval/test summary, then integrates with web research in one pass. Multi-hop works in the daemon because all hops are ask-mode.

(5) Security invariants preserved: delegate_task surface unchanged ({mode,task}); no Bash any mode; no write tools in ask; registry.json gitignored; doctor(--apply --managedOnly) runs before scheduler starts.

(6) When to use Actions instead: if the agent ITSELF must call gh issue create (not via a host builtin), or needs CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN wired without ${VAR} placeholder complexity → use GitHub Actions cron with claude-code-action.

Provenance: PR #180 (2026-06-20) — supersedes PR #178 capability-bound-schedule-placement.

provenance.source: PR #180: feat(analyst): agent-driven daily performance review (replaces CI #178)

ci-schedules-yaml-section-scope.l0: YAML cron parse: track indentation — top-level `name:` = workflow; crons only under `on.schedule`; skip `#` lines.
ci-schedules-yaml-section-scope.l1: PR #176: surfacing CI cron workflows in the SCHEDULES panel. (1) YAML scanner must be section-aware: top-level `name:` is the workflow display name; crons live only inside `on.schedule:`; `#` comments (full-line or inline) skipped. Naive line-grep matches nested `name:` fields too. (2) Producer→consumer join key must match end-to-end: `latestCiRuns` keys by `ci:<name>`; graph-view joins on the same string — mismatch silently drops the status column. (3) Fetch all sources before the empty-state gate; a missing endpoint collapses the panel incorrectly.
ci-schedules-yaml-section-scope.value:
When adding a dashboard panel that aggregates scheduled jobs from multiple sources (daemon + GitHub Actions CI):

(1) YAML section-aware scanner: do NOT use a naive line-grep to extract workflow names or cron expressions from .github/workflows/*.yml. Top-level `name:` (at indent 0) is the workflow display name; a nested `name:` inside a job or step is NOT. Cron expressions live only inside the `on.schedule:` block. Full-line comments (`# ...`) and inline comments (trailing `# ...`) must be stripped before parsing. Use a three-state machine: scanning-for-name → found-name → in-schedule-block → collecting-crons → back-to-top. The indentation of each line determines which state transition applies.

(2) Producer↔consumer join key — verify end-to-end: the key used to index runs in the cache (e.g. `ci:<workflowDisplayName>`) must be the exact same string the consumer uses to look up status. A capitalisation or prefix mismatch produces a silently missing status column — no error, just `—` everywhere. Write a test that asserts the key from `latestCiRuns` appears verbatim in the rendered group data.

(3) Multi-source pre-empty-state fetch: when a panel aggregates N endpoints, fetch ALL of them before the empty-state decision. If you check `results.length === 0` after only the first endpoint, the panel incorrectly collapses when the second endpoint has data but the first is empty.

(4) Reuse existing caches for read-only CI endpoints: the `gh-activity.json` cache (populated by the scheduler builtin `gh-activity-poll`) already holds workflow run conclusions. A new `/api/ci-schedules` route can serve those without any additional `gh` CLI calls — missing cache → show `—` status (fail-open, not an error).

(5) node --check for syntax validation: when a .js/.mjs file cannot be loaded in a test (e.g. it has smart-quote regressions from copy-paste), run `node --check <file>` as a quick parse-only gate — catches syntax errors before runtime.

Provenance: PR #176 (2026-06-20).

provenance.source: PR #176: feat(dashboard): surface GitHub Actions cron workflows in the SCHEDULES panel

deploy-worktree-daemon-isolation.l0: Daemon sharing dev checkout breaks when agents branch-switch — run it from a deploy worktree pinned to main.
deploy-worktree-daemon-isolation.l1: PR #172: daemon's repoRoot is script-relative — it serves whatever the shared dev checkout has. When another agent branch-switches, the 24/7 daemon runs stale code without warning. Fix: `git worktree add $DEPLOY_ROOT origin/main` (detached, deploy-only). Sync fetches and `reset --hard origin/main` — NOT ff-only (ff-only fails on detached HEAD and skips on dirty files). Restart only when target SHA differs from lastRestartedTarget (persisted in state.json). Reset and restart are independent — a failed restart retries next tick without re-fetching.
deploy-worktree-daemon-isolation.value:
Apply when a daemon resolves its root from its own script location and shares a dev checkout with active agents.

(1) Deploy worktree: `git worktree add "$DEPLOY_ROOT" origin/main`. Detached HEAD is correct — deploy-sync uses reset --hard, not ff-only. Default path: `~/.agent-mesh/deploy`.

(2) OS service config: point launchd ProgramArguments / systemd ExecStart at the deploy worktree's script, not the dev checkout.

(3) Sync (StartInterval=300): `fetch → rev-parse HEAD → rev-parse origin/main → planDeploySync → reset --hard (if head≠target) → restart (if target≠lastRestartedTarget)`. Never ff-only: ff-only returns skip_detached on detached HEAD and skip_dirty on modified files.

(4) Independent decisions: reset = (head ≠ target); restart = (target ≠ lastRestartedTarget). Keep them independent — a failed restart leaves the worktree already advanced; next tick retries only restart.

(5) State file: `.dev-society/deploy-sync-state.json` → `{ "lastRestartedTarget": "<sha>" }`. Write ONLY after successful restart. If restart fails, do not write — next tick retries.

(6) Error handling: wrap in try/catch; a git failure returns `{ action: 'error' }` and skips restart. Never restart after a fetch failure.

(7) Install script: generate .plist/.service from detected absolute paths at install time (see os-service-installer-generate-at-install). Never commit generated unit files.

Related: os-service-installer-generate-at-install, subsystem-promote-and-daemon-guard, doctor-apply-on-framework-upgrade. Provenance: PR #172 (2026-06-19).

provenance.source: PR #172: feat(deploy): run the 24/7 daemon from a deploy worktree pinned to main

git-worktree-reset-not-ffonly.l0: git worktree sync: `reset --hard`, not `--ff-only` — ff-only skips silently on detached HEAD and dirty files.
git-worktree-reset-not-ffonly.l1: Deploy worktrees are created via `git worktree add $PATH origin/main` → detached HEAD. `git merge --ff-only origin/main` silently exits 0 on detached HEAD (skip_detached) and on dirty files (skip_dirty) — the worktree appears current but is stale. `git reset --hard origin/main` always advances, works on detached HEAD, and fails loudly on real fetch errors. PR #172 deploy-sync confirmed: ff-only would silently leave the daemon serving stale code. Promoted via PR #174.
git-worktree-reset-not-ffonly.value:
Pattern: for any automation that advances a git worktree to track a remote ref — deploy sync, CI fixture refresh, scheduled advance:

(1) Always: `git fetch origin && git reset --hard origin/<branch>` — never `git merge --ff-only origin/<branch>`.

(2) Why ff-only fails silently:
  - Detached HEAD (normal after `git worktree add $PATH origin/<branch>`): ff-only returns skip_detached, exits 0, writes nothing.
  - Any dirty file: ff-only returns skip_dirty, exits 0, writes nothing.

(3) reset --hard is safe for deploy worktrees: no local edits expected; works on detached HEAD; fails loudly on bad fetch or missing ref — no silent skips.

(4) Advance verification: `git rev-parse HEAD` should equal `git rev-parse origin/<branch>` after a successful sync; assert equality in automated tests.

Related: deploy-worktree-daemon-isolation. Provenance: PR #172 (2026-06-19), promoted PR #174 (2026-06-20).

provenance.source: PR #174: memory:promote — deploy-worktree-daemon-isolation

label-preflight-before-issue-create.l0: ensureLabels() before every gh issue create loop — label-missing 422 aborts the whole run silently, not per-label.
label-preflight-before-issue-create.l1: PR #191: `gh issue create --label X` 422s when label X is absent — GitHub never auto-creates labels, silently aborting the whole run. All 4 mesh filers had latent 422 risk: analyst (patched manually #182), MIR syncReport, daemon heartbeat, automerge escalation still exposed. Fix: src/gh-labels.js ensureLabels(gh, labels, {repo,color}) — idempotent, best-effort, injected-gh for unit testing. Call once per filer before the issue-create loop. A single-filer manual patch is a systemic-gap signal: audit all filers sharing the same create verb.
label-preflight-before-issue-create.value:
Apply to any autonomous process calling `gh issue create --label X`.

(1) `gh issue create --label X` returns HTTP 422 when label X is absent — GitHub does NOT auto-create labels. The run aborts silently with no issues created. A single manual `gh label create` patch leaves all other filers at risk.

(2) Idempotent label preflight: call `ensureLabels(gh, labels, {repo, color})` once per filer before its issue-create loop. It calls `gh label create` for each label and silently swallows the already-exists error — never throws. Safe to call on every run.

(3) Blast radius audit: when you add label preflight to ONE filer, grep for every other `gh issue create` or `gh issue edit --add-label` in the codebase. Each is a latent 422 if its label set is not pre-ensured.

(4) Inject `gh` for hermetic unit testing. Test cases: dedup (labels deduplicated before calling), idempotent (already-exists swallowed), correct repo/color args, empty/non-string names ignored. Wiring test: assert `gh label create` precedes the first `gh issue create` call for each filer.

(5) Filer inventory (this mesh): analyst-review-run.mjs, src/mesh-improvement/run.js, scripts/dev-society-daemon.mjs, src/automerge/escalation-sweep.js — all wired in PR #191.

Provenance: PR #191 (2026-06-20), closes #188 #182.

provenance.source: PR #191: feat(gh-labels): self-healing ensureLabels at every mesh issue-filer

label-lifecycle-predecessor-clear.l0: Approved+spec:in-review deadlock: planLabelRepair must clear predecessor labels when approval supersedes them.
label-lifecycle-predecessor-clear.l1: routeFor lists spec:in-review as HUMAN_GATED before the code route. Human adding 'approved' without removing 'spec:in-review' permanently strands the issue: planLabelRepair returns null (not blocked), routeFor returns {target:null} (human-gated). Issue #175 sat ~11h in this dead state. Fix: planLabelRepair clears spec:in-review when approved AND code-type AND not already past the gate (blocked/pr:in-review/in-progress). Scoped to code-types: idea→spec:in-review→cleared→idea loops must not happen. Provenance: PR #192 (2026-06-20).
label-lifecycle-predecessor-clear.value:
For any multi-step label lifecycle where a human approval action supersedes a prior automated state:

(1) Identify predecessor labels that can co-exist with 'approved' after a single human action (e.g. adding 'approved' without removing 'spec:in-review'). Co-existence that leaves the issue unrouted AND unrepaired is a deadlock.

(2) Fix in planLabelRepair, not routeFor: planLabelRepair is the repair layer; routeFor is the routing layer. After the repair sweep clears the predecessor, the issue arrives at routeFor in a clean state.

(3) Scope to applicable issue types: code-type (bug/enhancement/documentation). 'idea' must be excluded — idea → spec:draft → spec:in-review → cleared → idea is an infinite loop.

(4) Guard on post-gate conditions: skip when blocked/pr:in-review/in-progress — the issue is already past the spec stage.

(5) Latency: label-repair-sweep runs every 10 min; max 10 min after human approval before drain.

Test matrix: approved+spec:in-review+code-type → removes spec:in-review → routed; approved+spec:in-review+idea → no repair; approved+spec:in-review+in-progress → no repair; spec:in-review without approved → stays human-gated.

Red flag: approved+lifecycle-predecessor-label sitting unchanged >10 min → planLabelRepair is missing a rule. Provenance: PR #192 (2026-06-20), issue #175 stranded ~11h.

provenance.source: PR #192: fix(dev-society): drain the approved+spec:in-review deadlock (unblocks #175)

build-lock-deploy-sync-deferral.l0: Deploy-sync: hold build lock for full task; restart deferred while fresh; reset runs always; stale lock = not-busy.
build-lock-deploy-sync-deferral.l1: PR #197: deploy-sync restarts daemon every 5 min when origin/main advances; frequent memory PR merges caused restarts mid-build, orphaning issues (build finished but commit→push→gh pr create never ran). Fix: hold `.dev-society/build.lock` ({issue,pid,ts}) for the whole `runOneTask`; `planDeploySync` defers restart while lock age < 12 min (past 10-min A2A timeout = not-busy). Reset still runs harmlessly. Lock released in `finally`. Stale/absent/corrupt → not-busy; a crashed build cannot wedge deploy-sync permanently.
build-lock-deploy-sync-deferral.value:
Pattern: decouple "reset" (idempotent, safe) from "restart" (disruptive) in any deploy-sync cycle that may run concurrently with long-duration tasks.

(1) Build-lock file: src/dev-society/build-lock.js exports acquireBuildLock(dir, issue), releaseBuildLock(dir), readBuildBusy(dir, staleMs). Lock payload: {issue, pid, ts}. Acquire writes atomically; release deletes; readBuildBusy reads and checks age: lock.ts + staleMs ≤ Date.now() → not-busy.

(2) Stale TTL = max task timeout + margin. PR #197: 12 min (past the 10-min A2A per-task timeout). A crashed/hung build's lock becomes stale before sync can be permanently blocked.

(3) runOneTask: acquire lock AFTER claiming the issue, BEFORE the build begins. Release in `finally` — a throw, reject, or timeout always frees it.

(4) planDeploySync({..., buildBusy}): returns {restart:false, deferredRestart:true} when buildBusy is true. Reset runs unconditionally (the daemon is already in memory; reset is harmless). Next sync tick re-evaluates; once lock is released → restart proceeds.

(5) Log the deferral: runDeploySyncOnce logs 'restart-deferred(build-busy)' so the operator can see deferral cycles in the daemon log.

(6) Fail-open on lock I/O: absent/corrupt/non-JSON lock → not-busy. Never block restart on an unreadable lock — degraded safety beats a permanently-stuck daemon.

(7) Tests: build-lock.test.js covers acquire/release/fresh-busy/stale-not-busy/absent/corrupt; deploy-sync.test.js covers planDeploySync with buildBusy true/false; daemon source-lint asserts acquire precedes the build and release is in finally.

Applies to: any daemon with a periodic restart step (deploy-sync, rolling-update, config-reload) that may co-run with long-duration owned tasks.

provenance.source: PR #197: fix(deploy-sync): defer daemon restart during in-flight coder builds (build-lock)

deploy-sync-tiered-restart.l0: deploy-sync multi-service: required restarts gate state advancement; best-effort restarts log-only and never block.
deploy-sync-tiered-restart.l1: PR #200: dashboard brought under CD alongside daemon via makeMultiRestart. Required services (daemon): failure rejects, state not advanced, retry next tick. Best-effort services (dashboard): errors caught and logged, never propagated — dashboard failure never wedges daemon CD. Pattern lets new services join CD without raising blast radius on the critical path.
deploy-sync-tiered-restart.value:
When multiple services share a deploy-sync cycle, classify by restart criticality:

(1) Required (daemon): kicked first; failure rejects → state not advanced → retry next tick.

(2) Best-effort (dashboard): kicked after; errors logged, never thrown — failure never blocks critical path.

New services: best-effort by default; wire via env var (DASHBOARD_LABEL). Planner unchanged. PR #200.

provenance.source: PR #200: feat(cd): bring the dashboard under deploy-sync CD (auto-update on push to main)

approved-overrides-review-gates.l0: Split HUMAN_GATED into HARD_GATED and REVIEW_GATED — 'approved' overrides review gates for all issue types.
approved-overrides-review-gates.l1: PR #202: HUMAN_GATED mixed two semantics: pr:in-review/blocked (never auto-build — in progress or failed) vs spec:in-review/discussing (held until human approves). PR #192 rescued code-typed approved issues via planLabelRepair but non-code-typed (typeless, idea) had no code-type label so planLabelRepair skipped them → permanent deadlock. Fix: HARD_GATED=[pr:in-review, blocked]; REVIEW_GATED=[spec:in-review, discussing]. if (approved && REVIEW_GATED present) → coder, for every type. Hard gates + terminal still win.
approved-overrides-review-gates.value:
Pattern for a label-based routing gate with semantically distinct gate classes:

(1) Classify gate labels by WHY they block, not just 'human-controlled':
- HARD_GATED (pr:in-review, blocked): something in-flight or needing human diagnosis. 'approved' does NOT override — a failed build needs diagnosis before re-driving (auto-re-driving loops).
- REVIEW_GATED (spec:in-review, discussing): paused for human sign-off. 'approved' IS the sign-off — overrides unconditionally for all issue types.

(2) routeFor: check 'if (approved && any REVIEW_GATED label)' BEFORE type-dispatch, AFTER terminal + hard gates. Works for code-typed, typeless, AND idea issues equally. This is the right fix point — planLabelRepair is scoped to code-types only to prevent idea→spec→cleared→idea loops.

(3) planLabelRepair still clears spec:in-review for code-typed issues (label cleanup, complements the routing fix). After PR #202, this is redundant for routing but keeps the board tidy for human reviewers.

(4) Invariant to assert: every non-terminal, non-hard-gated, approved issue MUST route to some agent. Test across all type × review-gate combos: approved+spec:in-review+[] → coder; approved+spec:in-review+bug → coder; approved+spec:in-review+idea → coder; approved+discussing → coder. Hard gates still win: approved+blocked → null; approved+pr:in-review → null.

(5) When adding a new 'human-controlled' label: decide first whether it is HARD (in-flight/broken; approved doesn't help) or REVIEW (waiting for a human decision; approved clears it). The split is architectural — conflating the two classes creates deadlocks like #194/#195.

Related: label-lifecycle-predecessor-clear (planLabelRepair complement). Provenance: PR #202 (2026-06-20).

provenance.source: PR #202: fix(dev-society): 'approved' always routes to an agent — overrides the review gates

workflow-run-release-triple-gate.l0: workflow_run CD gate: conclusion==success, head_branch==main, event==push; concurrency cancel-in-progress:false.
workflow-run-release-triple-gate.l1: PR #204: workflow_run fires on every CI completion — PRs, re-runs, dispatch. Three gates, ALL required: (1) conclusion==success: no release from failed CI; (2) head_branch==main: skips fork-PR CI (branch ≠ main); (3) event==push: blocks backward edge rolls from dispatch/PR-event CI. concurrency cancel-in-progress:false is mandatory for releases — opposite of standard CI; mid-run cancel leaves a partial versioned release or an asset-less edge tag. Lint-assert the name coupling: workflows:["CI"] must match ci.yml name: or the release workflow silently never fires.
workflow-run-release-triple-gate.value:
Apply to any workflow_run-triggered CD/release workflow:

(1) Triple gate — ALL three conditions required:
```yaml
if: >-
  github.event.workflow_run.conclusion == 'success' &&
  github.event.workflow_run.head_branch == 'main' &&
  github.event.workflow_run.event == 'push'
```
- conclusion==success: prevents release from failed CI.
- head_branch==main: prevents fork-PR CI runs from triggering (fork head_branch is the feature branch, not main).
- event==push: prevents backward edge rolls from workflow_dispatch re-runs, manually re-triggered CI, and PR-event CI that happened to pass.

(2) concurrency: cancel-in-progress: false — the CORRECT setting for release jobs (opposite of standard CI). Standard CI cancels stale runs to save minutes. A release job canceled mid-flight leaves a partial versioned release (asset never uploaded) or an edge tag with no downloadable file. Never cancel in progress.

(3) Checkout the exact green SHA — not HEAD:
  ref: ${{ github.event.workflow_run.head_sha }}
HEAD may have advanced between CI completion and the release job's checkout. The sha is pinned to the commit that actually went green.

(4) Stable tarball name: `npm pack --ignore-scripts | tail -1` returns the real filename (version/scope-proof). Rename to a fixed name (agent-mesh.tgz) for predictable download URLs. --ignore-scripts prevents pack hooks from running.

(5) Dual-channel: edge (rolling) + v<version> (immutable):
- Edge: delete+recreate each push — gh release delete edge --yes --cleanup-tag 2>/dev/null || true then recreate. Always points to the latest green main.
- Versioned: idempotent skip if gh release view v$VERSION succeeds. Race safety: || echo 'was created concurrently' handles the rare two-runs-same-version race.

(6) Structural dependency lint: the workflows:["CI"] name must match ci.yml's name: CI field exactly. A silent mismatch means the release workflow never fires and no one notices. Add a test asserting the string appears in ci.yml. (PR #204 ships test/release-workflow.test.js with this assertion.)

(7) Permissions: contents: write only. No external secrets — GITHUB_TOKEN suffices for gh release create, tag management, and asset upload.

Provenance: PR #204 (2026-06-20).

provenance.source: PR #204: feat(release): publish tarball as GitHub Release on green main (edge + versioned)

gh-pr-list-sort-before-select.l0: gh pr list returns newest-first; [0] without sort starves older PRs — always sort_by(.updatedAt) first.
gh-pr-list-sort-before-select.l1: PR #220 (fix/review-respond-fair-select): review-respond picked CHANGES_REQUESTED PRs with [select(...)][0] — gh pr list is descending (newest first), so newest PR was always chosen, starving older ones. Fix: sort_by(.updatedAt) | .[0] picks longest-waiting; once a fix commit lands, updatedAt bumps newest and it rotates to back naturally. Same bug class as PR #205 mergefix selector.
gh-pr-list-sort-before-select.value:
Any gh pr list | jq '[select(...)][0]' is a starvation trap — add sort_by(.updatedAt) before indexing:
  gh pr list --json number,updatedAt,reviewDecision \
    | jq '[.[] | select(.reviewDecision=="CHANGES_REQUESTED")] | sort_by(.updatedAt) | .[0]'
Works for any single-item picker (CHANGES_REQUESTED, failing checks, needs-review). On each fix commit, updatedAt bumps to newest so the PR rotates to back — stateless, no extra state file. If you explicitly want newest-first (sprint triage), be explicit: sort_by(.updatedAt) | reverse | .[0]. Never rely on gh default order as meaningful selection. Add sort_by to any [0] selector that must be fair. Provenance: PR #220 (2026-06-20); same class as PR #205.

provenance.source: PR #220 (2026-06-20): fix(dev-mesh-review-respond): fair PR selection (no head-of-line starvation)

bug-bypasses-spec-gate.l0: Bug issues bypass spec:in-review — defect → coder directly; only features/ideas/docs need human-reviewed specs.
bug-bypasses-spec-gate.l1: routeFor already sent bare 'bug' to the coder. But 'bug+spec:in-review' fell into the human-gate path, requiring 'approved' to advance. PR #215 added a bug-specific override symmetric with approved-overrides-review: bug routes to coder even at spec:in-review/discussing. Scoped to bug only — enhancement/documentation/idea at review still return null (human-gated). Hard stops unchanged: blocked (failed build + loop guard), pr:in-review, terminal, in-progress all still win.
bug-bypasses-spec-gate.value:
Pattern for multi-tier issue routing in an autonomous dev-mesh society:

(1) Label-tier override ordering: check issue-type-specific bypasses BEFORE the generic lifecycle gate. spec:in-review/discussing gating is a quality check for FEATURES — it protects humans from shipping underspecified work. A bug is a defect; it has no spec lifecycle. Bypassing the review gate for bugs only keeps the safety net intact for ideas/enhancements/docs.

(2) Override table (symmetric with approved-overrides-review):
  bug (bare)               → coder (unchanged)
  bug + spec:in-review     → coder, reason:'bug-autofix' (NEW)
  bug + discussing         → coder, reason:'bug-autofix' (NEW)
  bug + blocked            → null  (hard stop — failed build + loop guard #98)
  bug + pr:in-review       → null  (already has a PR)
  enhancement/idea/docs + spec:in-review → null (still human-gated, unchanged)

(3) Implementation in routeFor: insert a bug-type check as an early-exit BEFORE the spec:in-review/discussing null-return. Clear the spec review label at routing time so the Coder inherits a clean issue state.

(4) Hard gate invariants never move: blocked, pr:in-review, in-progress, and terminal states short-circuit BEFORE the bug bypass. The bypass targets only the review gate — never safety gates.

(5) Required test matrix: (a) bug bare → coder; (b) bug+spec:in-review → coder, reason:bug-autofix; (c) bug+discussing → coder; (d) bug+blocked → null; (e) bug+pr:in-review → null; (f) enhancement+spec:in-review → null; (g) idea+spec:in-review → null. Document each as an explicit negative case to prevent regression.

Provenance: PR #215 (2026-06-20).

provenance.source: PR #215: feat(dev-society): bug issues auto-fix without human approval (override the review gate)

github-lazy-mergeability-force-view.l0: gh pr list mergeState is lazy — freshly-DIRTY shows UNKNOWN/blank; force via per-PR gh pr view before filtering.
github-lazy-mergeability-force-view.l1: PR #205: mergefix filtered bulk gh pr list by mergeStateStatus=='DIRTY'. GitHub computes mergeability lazily — right after main advances, a freshly-conflicting PR reports UNKNOWN or blank, so the selector silently skipped it (3 runs logged 'no conflicting PR' while #199 was DIRTY). Fix: (1) gather DIRTY|UNKNOWN|blank candidates; (2) gh pr view <n> per candidate forces computation; (3) re-poll once for stragglers. Also: git merge origin/main fails with fatal: empty ident name on stock CI runners — set user.name/user.email before any merge commit.
github-lazy-mergeability-force-view.value:
Apply when selecting PRs by merge state in GitHub Actions workflows:

(1) Lazy mergeability: `gh pr list` does NOT compute mergeStateStatus immediately after main advances. A PR that just became conflicting shows UNKNOWN or blank in bulk list results — it is NOT yet DIRTY. GitHub evaluates mergeability lazily (per-PR read triggers computation).

Two-phase selection:
- Bulk: gather candidates where mergeStateStatus ∈ {DIRTY, UNKNOWN, ''} — never filter DIRTY-only.
- Per-PR force: call `gh pr view <N> --json mergeStateStatus` for each candidate; pick confirmed DIRTY ones. A single re-poll after a short wait catches stragglers.

Symptom: mergefix logs 'no conflicting same-repo PR' across consecutive runs while a known-conflicting PR sits untouched. Root cause is always the bulk selector missing the UNKNOWN/blank window.

(2) Git identity on stock CI runners: `git merge origin/main` produces a merge commit. A fresh GitHub Actions runner has no git identity. Absent → `fatal: empty ident name` (exit 128).

Fix: set before any merge commit:
  git config user.name 'github-actions[bot]'
  git config user.email 'github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com'

Both bugs must be fixed together to drain the first conflicting PR: bug 1 blocks selection; bug 2 blocks resolution. PR #199 was DIRTY across multiple mergefix poll cycles without being caught by either.

Test coverage: selector unit tests must include UNKNOWN and blank as positive cases; assert gh pr view is called per candidate; assert git config runs before git merge. Provenance: PR #205 (2026-06-20).

provenance.source: PR #205: fix(dev-mesh-mergefix): drain freshly-conflicting PRs (lazy-mergeability race + git identity)

github-autoclose-reconciler-backstop.l0: GitHub keyword auto-close is intermittent — poll a post-merge reconciler to close in-flight-labeled issues.
github-autoclose-reconciler-backstop.l1: PR #235: 'Closes #N' intermittently misses on merge. Issues with pr:in-review/in-progress after their closing PR merged stay open forever — planLabelRepair never touches pr:in-review; terminal sweep only acts on done/rejected/etc. Fix: post-merge-reconcile builtin on 10-min cadence. Query OPEN issues with in-flight labels; cross-reference closingIssuesReferences with the merged set; plan to close those whose PR merged. Scoped to in-flight labels only — won't touch human-reopened issues. Pure planPostMergeReconcile({openIssues,mergedPRs})→[{action:'close',number}]; daemon applies the plan.
github-autoclose-reconciler-backstop.value:
Apply when building a pipeline that routes issues based on labels and relies on GitHub keyword auto-close.

(1) GitHub 'Closes #N' auto-close is unreliable: when a PR merges to main, GitHub attempts to close referenced issues — but the mechanism intermittently misses. Issues #183 and #175 (PR #235) remained open with pr:in-review after their closing PRs had already merged.

(2) Standard pipeline gaps:
- planLabelRepair: only targets labels its rules explicitly handle. pr:in-review = 'active work in progress' → no repair rule touches it.
- terminal sweep: only acts on done/rejected/wontfix/duplicate/invalid labels.
- Neither sweep asks: 'did this issue's closing PR actually merge?'

(3) Post-merge reconciler pattern:
  pure planPostMergeReconcile({ openIssues, mergedPRsByNumber }):
    for each open issue:
      if issue has in-flight label (pr:in-review OR in-progress):
        for each closingIssuesReference:
          if reference.number is in mergedPRsByNumber:
            yield { action:'close', number:issue.number, reason:'closing-pr-merged' }
  returns: [{ action:'close', number }]

(4) Daemon builtin registration:
  schedule.json: { kind:'builtin', name:'post-merge-reconcile', intervalMs:600000 }
  daemon: register async fn → call planPostMergeReconcile + gh issue close --reason completed
  See scheduler-builtin-for-native-jobs and scheduler-builtin-return-shape.

(5) Safe scoping:
- Acts ONLY on issues with an in-flight label (pr:in-review/in-progress).
- Acts ONLY when a closing PR is confirmed MERGED — not just closed.
- Idempotent: a re-run over an already-closed issue is a no-op.
- Human-reopened issues: if the human clears pr:in-review on reopen, the reconciler skips them.

(6) Test surface (PR #235 pattern): closes pr:in-review issue with merged closing PR; clears in-progress too; ignores: no in-flight label / unmerged PR / malformed closingIssuesReferences.

Distinct from terminal-close-escalate-sweep (closes terminal-labeled issues) and event-driven-polling-backstop (guards missed webhook events). Provenance: PR #235 (2026-06-20).

provenance.source: PR #235: feat(daemon): post-merge-reconcile — auto-close merged-but-open issues

report-first-sweep-phase.l0: New automation: Phase ① read-only (pure classifiers + allowlist test + dashboard report) before Phase ② mutation.
report-first-sweep-phase.l1: PR #242: merge-sweep ① is structurally read-only — no merges, no label writes. Three pure classifiers extracted from existing mutators (classifyIssueGate, classifyAutomergePr, classifyMemoryPr) re-express each gate decision as data; mutators are untouched. classifyAutomergePr fail-closes: gate-unknown on ambiguous inputs, never overclaims would-merge. A hermetic allowlist test asserts only read-only gh commands in the daemon builtin. State report surfaces on the dashboard (◆ MERGE-SWEEP panel) + /api/merge-sweep. Mutation deferred to Phase ②.
report-first-sweep-phase.value:
Pattern: report-first phase split for any new sweep automation.

(1) Phase ① = read-only only. No gh mutations in the daemon builtin or API endpoint. Prove this via a hermetic allowlist test: iterate every command executed in runMergeSweep (or equivalent) and fail on any write command (pr merge, issue edit, label add/remove). The test is a CI gate — mutation that slips in fails CI before it ships.

(2) Extract pure classifiers from existing mutators. Map:
   runIssueGate()    → classifyIssueGate()    {status, reason}
   isAutoMergeable() → classifyAutomergePr()  {state, reason}
   automerge guard   → classifyMemoryPr()     {state, reason}
The classifier re-expresses the same decision logic as a {state,reason} return. The mutator delegates to the classifier then applies side effects. Behavior is unchanged; the classifier is testable in isolation with no gh calls.

(3) Fail-closed classification. Never overclaim a ready state. Use gate-unknown (not would-merge) when inputs are ambiguous or missing. Overlay semantics (CLEAN+APPROVED PR whose linked issue is gate-held) must produce blocked/pending-issue-gate, not a false positive. The safe default is 'not yet' — a false negative delays a merge; a false positive merges prematurely.

(4) Dashboard + API surfacing in Phase ①. Build the state report, expose it as a GET endpoint with a staleness header, and render a dashboard panel. Operators validate the classifier logic live before any mutation exists. Panel security: HTML-escape all strings; use numeric PR refs in links, never raw branch names.

(5) Phase ② adds mutation atop Phase ①. Classifiers and report survive unchanged. Phase ② only wires execution (call gh pr merge / gh pr edit) gated by the Phase ① classification. No redesign needed.

Apply when any new sweep will eventually mutate GitHub state (auto-merge, auto-label, auto-close, issue routing). The read-only phase ships in days; the mutation phase ships after operators have observed and validated the classification logic on the dashboard.

Related: default-off-three-layer-kill-switch (gate the mutation phase off by default). Provenance: PR #242 (2026-06-20).

provenance.source: PR #242: feat(merge-sweep): report-first state machine ① (read-only, dashboard-visible)

cron-job-no-dedicated-panel.l0: Dashboard cron jobs: Schedules view only, not dedicated panels; report output in a generic job-keyed expand slot.
cron-job-no-dedicated-panel.l1: PR #249: a dedicated MERGE-SWEEP panel was removed because merge-sweep is ONE cron job; the Schedules view (unified daemon-cron + GitHub-Actions view) renders all jobs with description + lastSummary. Report-producing jobs get a generic expand keyed by job id (not job type) — merge-sweep is the first; future jobs reuse the same slot. No backend change: /api/schedules already carries lastSummary. New test: schedules-render.test.js covers the pure render fns.
cron-job-no-dedicated-panel.value:
When a new dashboard feature represents one instance of a known class (cron job, scheduled task, agent), place it in the class's existing view — not as a new top-level panel.

(1) Anti-pattern: a dedicated panel for a specific job (e.g., MERGE-SWEEP) duplicates the Schedules view, adds a special-case rendering path, and establishes a precedent where every future job gets its own panel — dashboard sprawl.

(2) Class-view placement: the Schedules view is the correct home for all cron jobs. Render each job's description + lastSummary inline on its row. /api/schedules already carries lastSummary — no backend change needed.

(3) Generic expand slot for structured output: when a job produces report content, add a generic expand slot keyed by job id (not by job type). New jobs that produce reports populate the existing slot; they do not add new slot types. Implementation: a jobHasReport predicate + a per-job render fn registered by job id (e.g., 'merge-sweep' → renderMergeSweep).

(4) Test coverage: unit-test the pure render functions (jobResultLine escaping, fail/empty states, jobHasReport predicate) rather than the per-job output format — keeps tests stable when job content changes.

Provenance: PR #249 (2026-06-20).

provenance.source: PR #249: refactor(dashboard): cron jobs show description + result; merge-sweep is a Schedules row, not a panel

gha-cron-throttle-use-daemon-builtin.l0: GHA `*/15` cron fires every ~1-2h in practice — use a daemon builtin at 10-min cadence for time-sensitive sweeps.
gha-cron-throttle-use-daemon-builtin.l1: GitHub Actions cron (even `*/15`) is throttled; observed fire rate ~1-2h. dev-mesh-automerge `*/15` left ready PRs (CLEAN+APPROVED) idle for hours. Fix (PR #231): register `runSweep` as a `kind:builtin` (`automerge-sweep`) on maintainer schedule.json at 10-min cadence — same reliable cadence as `issue-sweep`. GHA cron stays as cold-start backstop. Time-critical sweeps (latency target << 1h) belong in daemon builtins, not GHA cron alone.

provenance.source: PR #231: feat(daemon): automerge-sweep builtin — prompt drain of ready PRs (no idle)

daemon-repovar-env-gap.l0: Daemon env ≠ repo variable: fetch feature flags via `gh variable get`; launchd/systemd env doesn't carry repo vars.
daemon-repovar-env-gap.l1: PR #246: automerge-sweep logged 'disabled (AUTOMERGE_ENABLED != true)' even though the operator had set the GitHub repo variable to true. Daemon launchd env is fixed at install time — repo variable changes never reach it. The GitHub-Actions sweep reads `vars.AUTOMERGE_ENABLED` at runtime (correct). Fix: resolveAutomergeEnabled tries `gh variable get AUTOMERGE_ENABLED` first, falls back to process.env, safe-off on read error. 8 CLEAN+APPROVED PRs idled ≥5 cycles before the monitor caught it.
daemon-repovar-env-gap.value:
Pattern for daemon feature flags controlled by GitHub repo variables:

(1) Repo variable is the authoritative source — launchd/systemd env is fixed at install time. `gh variable set AUTOMERGE_ENABLED --body true` updates the repo store immediately but NEVER the daemon's process.env. The GitHub-Actions sweep reads `vars.AUTOMERGE_ENABLED` at call time (correct by design); the daemon must do the same work explicitly.

(2) Two-source resolver: try `gh variable get FLAG` first; fall back to `process.env.FLAG` if gh read errors (local dev, no gh auth); safe-off (false) if both absent.

(3) Dependency injection for hermetic tests: expose a `resolver` param (async fn → bool). Unit-test: gh returns 'true' → enabled; gh returns 'false' → disabled; gh errors + env 'true' → enabled; gh errors + env absent → disabled (safe-off).

(4) Never inline `process.env.FLAG === 'true'` in daemon code paths. The stale-env gap is silent — no error, just permanently disabled, masking the operator's decision for ≥5 cycles.

Related: `default-off-three-layer-kill-switch` (YAML vars + predicate layers are correct; this closes the daemon CLI layer gap). Provenance: PR #246 (2026-06-20), monitor cycle 5.

provenance.source: PR #246: fix(daemon): automerge self-activates from the repo variable (no idle ready PRs)

agent-builtin-input-freshness-guard.l0: Agent builtin with required artifacts: fail-loud inputs_unavailable, never silent delegation on stale/absent inputs.
agent-builtin-input-freshness-guard.l1: PR #224: runAnalystDailyReview silently delegated to the agent when required digests (daily-report.json/gh-activity.json) were absent or stale. Agent received empty context, wasted tokens, and emitted low-quality output with no diagnostic signal. Fix: checkDigestFreshness() stats each REQUIRED_ARTIFACT path, returns {status:'inputs_unavailable', missing:[{path, reason}]} if any artifact is absent or older than today. Delegation only happens when all artifacts are fresh. Replaces silent 'prompt notes unavailability' fallback with a loud, zero-token fail-closed refusal. Closes #195.
agent-builtin-input-freshness-guard.value:
Apply to any agent builtin that reads pre-generated data artifacts (daily digests, nightly reports, cache files) before calling delegateTask.

(1) REQUIRED_ARTIFACTS array: declare the list of file paths the builtin MUST have. Only existence and mtime are checked in the guard — file content is consumed later by the normal buildPrompt path (no behavioral regression).

(2) checkDigestFreshness(artifacts, now): for each path, call stat(). If ENOENT → reason:'absent'; if mtime.toDateString() !== now.toDateString() → reason:'stale'. Returns {fresh:true} or {fresh:false, missing:[{path, reason}]}. Pure function — inject a `stat` stub for hermetic tests.

(3) Return inputs_unavailable: when the guard fails, return {status:'inputs_unavailable', missing:[...]} immediately before calling delegateTask. Include each artifact's path and reason. Zero tokens spent; the scheduler's onJobResult logs it as a structured fail.

(4) Test matrix (all four cases required): (a) both artifacts present + today → proceeds to delegation (regression guard); (b) one absent → inputs_unavailable; (c) both absent → inputs_unavailable; (d) present but yesterday mtime → stale → inputs_unavailable. Absent and stale are distinct failure modes — test them separately.

(5) Behavior change: 'prompt notes unavailability' asks the agent to reason about missing data — it wastes tokens, produces unpredictable output, and buries the root cause inside a model turn. A pre-flight refusal surfaces the failure cleanly in run-logs before the model is ever called.

(6) Related: mir-deterministic-backlog-bridge (MIR builtin reads L0–L4 artifact JSON — same pattern applies); cached-report-compute-and-schedule (ensuring artifacts are generated on schedule so the guard rarely fires).

Provenance: PR #224 (2026-06-20), closes #195.

provenance.source: PR #224: fix(analyst): freshness guard for daily digest inputs (#195)

pr-in-review-abandonment-sweep.l0: Bug issues in `pr:in-review` with no open PR are abandoned — sweep to `blocked` for human re-triage.
pr-in-review-abandonment-sweep.l1: PR #240: an issue labelled `bug + pr:in-review` becomes permanently stuck when its autofix PR closes without merging — no existing sweep covers this gap. `pr:in-review` is HARD_GATED: the daemon won't re-build it, and no other sweep escalates it. Fix: query open issues with both labels; for each, check `gh pr list --search '#N' --state open`. If no open PR found → add `blocked`, remove `pr:in-review`, post comment. Per-item errors are logged and skipped; sweep always exits 0 (cron-safe). Gated on AUTOMERGE_ENABLED.
pr-in-review-abandonment-sweep.value:
Pattern: mid-lifecycle abandonment detection when automation creates PRs that can close without merging.

(1) Detect: query open issues with BOTH in-flight labels (`bug + pr:in-review`). For each, check `gh pr list --search '#N' --state open` — if no open PR exists, the autofix is abandoned.

(2) Mutation plan (pure helper, no gh calls): add `blocked`, remove `pr:in-review`, post comment 'Auto-fix PR was closed without merging — needs re-triage.'

(3) Pure helpers: `isAutofixIssue(issue)` (labels check only) and `abandonedAutofixPlan()` (returns mutation spec) — hermetically testable without mocking gh.

(4) Gate on AUTOMERGE_ENABLED (same kill-switch as other mutation sweeps — see default-off-three-layer-kill-switch). Use `gh variable get` for the runtime value, not process.env (see daemon-repovar-env-gap).

(5) Cron-safe: per-item errors logged and skipped; always exits 0. Offset cron: :27.

(6) Permissions: issues:write + pull-requests:read only — no contents:write needed.

Apply whenever automation produces PRs that can close without merging, leaving the source issue perpetually stuck in an in-flight label state the daemon's HARD_GATED filter prevents from re-building. Provenance: PR #240 (2026-06-20).

provenance.source: PR #240: feat(#218): detect abandoned bug-autofix PRs and escalate to blocked

state-after-mutation.l0: Commit automation state only AFTER the external action succeeds — state-before-mutation silently drops items.
state-after-mutation.l1: PR #254 Codex BLOCKER: runRemediation wrote the escalation state record before calling `gh issue create`. On gh failure, the item was permanently marked 'escalated' with no issue ever filed — next sweeps skipped it silently. Zero diagnostic signal: no error in the run-log, no missing-item alert. The only correct ordering: call gh first, write state only in the success branch. Applies to any automation that pairs a state journal with external side-effect calls.
state-after-mutation.value:
Pattern: action first, state second — for any daemon sweep that pairs a persistent state file with an external mutation call (gh issue create/close/edit, label-add, API POST).

(1) WRONG — state before action:
  state.escalated[id] = { issueNumber: null, ts };
  await writeState(state);   // ← written before gh call
  const n = await gh.createIssue(...); // ← if this throws, state is already committed

(2) CORRECT — action before state:
  const n = await gh.createIssue(...);
  state.escalated[id] = { issueNumber: n, ts };
  await writeState(state);   // ← only written after gh succeeds

(3) On gh failure: catch the error, return a fail-state result ({status:'fail'}), do NOT write the state record. The next sweep tick finds the item unhandled and retries normally.

(4) Test matrix:
  a. gh.create throws → state NOT written, item retried next tick.
  b. gh.create succeeds → state written with issueNumber.
  c. Second run with state written → item correctly skipped (dedup).

Silent failure mode without this: journal says 'escalated', gh never fired, item dropped forever with no log or retry. Provenance: PR #254 (2026-06-20).

provenance.source: PR #254 (2026-06-20): feat(remediation): orchestrator backstop loop ② — Codex BLOCKER state-before-mutation

intake-approved-label-fence.l0: Analyst intake must never mutate labels on `approved` issues — re-triggers strip the review gate and deadlock routing.
intake-approved-label-fence.l1: PR #258: after a human adds `approved`, the intake workflow re-fires via the label event and strips `spec:in-review` (looks redundant). routeFor needs BOTH `approved` AND a review-gate label for approved-overrides-review; without spec:in-review the issue falls through to the advisory triager and stalls permanently. Two fixes: (1) YAML fence — Analyst never edits labels on issues already carrying `approved`; (2) re-arm-approved-route branch in planLabelRepair — the daemon self-heals approved idea-lineage issues that lost their review gate by re-adding spec:in-review.
intake-approved-label-fence.value:
Pattern: gate intake label mutations on the `approved` label; self-heal routing deadlocks in planLabelRepair.

**Root cause:** The Analyst intake workflow fires on EVERY label event, including when a human adds `approved`. At that point, `spec:in-review` is still present (the human gate just passed).
