(5) Test coverage: per-agent enumeration, dot-dir skip, missing meshRoot tolerance, cross-agent aggregation with started/done dedup, missing log file tolerance. PR #346 ships `test/report-per-agent-logs.test.js` (5 tests).

(6) Follow-up gap (not this PR): run records write `route: null` → per-route breakdown groups everything under 'unknown'. A separate delegate.js capture fix is needed; it does not affect total cost/token counts.

Provenance: PR #346 (2026-06-21).

provenance.source: PR #346: fix(report): token panel was always $0 — daily-report read the wrong (empty) log path

per-delegation-cost-budget-guard.l0: Delegation cost cap: AGENT_MESH_COST_BUDGET_USD; per-bridge accumulator; fan-out needs a separate pre-flight.
per-delegation-cost-budget-guard.l1: PR #351 spec: readCostBudget(env) → null|USD in src/config.js; in RESERVED_BRIDGE_ENV (peer.env cannot override). Accumulator: per-bridge-process closure var accumulatedCostUsd=0, resets per claude -p (same scope as depth/path). error_code: cost_budget_exceeded, same structured-refusal shape. fanOutToPeers bypasses delegateToPeer → must get its own pre-flight guard (separate from delegateToPeer's). Accumulate subtree_cost_usd post-settle even on child error (null → skip). Risk: concurrent fan-out may overshoot by one child's cost; timeout paths may under-count.
per-delegation-cost-budget-guard.value:
Implement per-delegation cost governance via a per-bridge accumulator in peer-bridge.js.

(1) CONFIG: AGENT_MESH_COST_BUDGET_USD (string; unset → no guard). Parse in src/config.js:
  readCostBudget(env): const v = Number(env.AGENT_MESH_COST_BUDGET_USD);
  return Number.isFinite(v) && v > 0 ? v : null;
  Add to RESERVED_BRIDGE_ENV so registry.json peer.env cannot override (anti-spoof invariant).

(2) ACCUMULATOR: in createBridge() closure:
  const costBudget = readCostBudget(env);   // null → disabled
  let accumulatedCostUsd = 0;               // resets at process exit (per claude -p span)

(3) PRE-FLIGHT in delegateToPeer (after depth check, before spawn):
  if (costBudget !== null && accumulatedCostUsd >= costBudget)
    return refuseLogged('cost_budget_exceeded', ...);
  Uses >= (hitting the limit also refuses — safe, not permissive).

(4) SEPARATE PRE-FLIGHT in fanOutToPeers: fanOutToPeers dispatches via Promise.allSettled WITHOUT calling delegateToPeer, so the delegateToPeer guard never fires. Add an identical check inside fanOutToPeers after its depth pre-flight.

(5) ACCUMULATION post-settle (in BOTH sites):
  if (typeof mapped.subtree_cost_usd === 'number') accumulatedCostUsd += mapped.subtree_cost_usd;
  Accumulate even on child error/timeout (tokens burned = real cost). null subtree_cost_usd → skip silently.

(6) RISKS:
  - Fan-out overshoot: all fan-out peers dispatched before any settle; pre-flight is a single aggregate gate. A concurrent delegateToPeer completing between fan-out pre-flight and settle can cause slight over-run. Bounded by FAN_OUT_MAX_PEERS; set budget with headroom.
  - Timeout under-count: if child times out before JSON envelope is written, subtree_cost_usd is null → not accumulated. Best-effort heuristic; timeout paths are already structured failures.
  - Per-session scope: multi-turn interactive sessions do NOT accumulate across turns (each claude -p spawns a fresh bridge).

(7) TESTS (test/peer-bridge.test.js): no-budget env → disabled; budget=0.10, accumulated=0 → passes; accumulated=0.10 → refused; peer.env budget → RESERVED_BRIDGE_ENV blocks; child error → cost still accumulated; fan-out refused when exhausted.

Provenance: PR #351 spec (2026-06-21), closes idea #350.

provenance.source: PR #351: spec: idea: per-delegation cost budget guard — prevent unbounded subtree spend via bridge circuit-breaker (#350)

mir-slow-drift-trend-gate.l0: MIR rolling median tracks slow drift — add Q1 two-half trend check as orthogonal signal for gradual deterioration.
mir-slow-drift-trend-gate.l1: PR #352 spec: when a metric degrades gradually across many runs, the rolling-window median baseline degrades in sync — individual runs stay within the noise band and no finding ever fires. Fix: additive trend check — split history into oldest/newest halves, compute Q1 of each, classify direction via metric-direction map. Adverse AND magnitude > noiseBandPct → emit 'trend:<metric>:<cell>' (cluster: trend-regression). Distinct id from per-run finding — both can coexist. Reuses AGENT_MESH_MIR_TREND_N + AGENT_MESH_MIR_NOISE_BAND_PCT; no new env vars.
mir-slow-drift-trend-gate.value:
Apply when adding trend monitoring on top of a rolling-window median baseline: the median solves per-run noise but is susceptible to gradual drift — the baseline degrades with the metric, so individual runs never breach the noise band.

Algorithm (additive — runs alongside per-run check, does not replace it):
1. Require ≥ TREND_N data points (default 10). Short history → skip.
2. Split window into oldest and newest halves.
3. Compute Q1 (first quartile) of each half — one outlier run cannot flip a half's verdict (unlike mean/min).
4. Classify direction using the existing metric-direction map ('higher-is-better' vs 'lower-is-better').
5. Adverse AND |magnitude| > noiseBandPct → emit finding id 'trend:<metric>:<cell>', cluster 'trend-regression'.

Finding lifecycle via existing planIssues path (no new code):
- First appearance → file issue
- Recurrence → update
- Reversal (drift no longer adverse) → close

Id 'trend:<metric>:<cell>' is distinct from the per-run id so both can coexist when a metric has both a spike and a long-term drift.

Config reuse: AGENT_MESH_MIR_TREND_N and AGENT_MESH_MIR_NOISE_BAND_PCT — no new env vars.

Noop cases: history < TREND_N; flat trend (oldest Q1 ≈ newest Q1); favorable drift (metric improving).

Complementary to mir-rolling-window-median-baseline (that lesson solves per-run noise; this lesson closes the blind spot the median creates for slow cumulative regressions that never breach the noise band individually). Provenance: PR #352 / Issue #349 (2026-06-21).

provenance.source: PR #352: spec: idea: MIR slow-drift trend gate — rolling-window median masks gradual metric deterioration (#349)

mobile-tailscale-askonly-concierge.l0: Mobile front-door: 127.0.0.1 bind + Tailscale (*.ts.net auto-allow) + token enforced + ask-only AI + human-tap write.
mobile-tailscale-askonly-concierge.l1: PR #355: bind to 127.0.0.1; proxy tailnet→localhost via tailscale serve (device-identity transport, no shared creds). Host-gate (server.js) auto-accepts *.ts.net MagicDNS + AGENT_MESH_DASHBOARD_ALLOWED_HOSTS — zero Tailscale config needed. Auth token STILL required on every gated route — Host allowlist widens which Host is accepted, never removes auth. Concierge ask-only (read tools only); proposes a fenced concierge-proposal; only write is gh issue create on explicit human Confirm tap with a fixed label allowlist (idea/approved/route:a2a).
mobile-tailscale-askonly-concierge.value:
Apply when exposing a locally-running AI service to a phone or remote device without public internet exposure.

(1) TRANSPORT: bind the HTTP server to 127.0.0.1 (not 0.0.0.0). Run `tailscale serve https / http://127.0.0.1:<port>` to proxy the Tailscale tailnet → localhost. Device-identity transport means no shared credentials in transit.

(2) HOST-GATE: extend the same-origin check to accept remote host headers:
  - Auto-accept *.ts.net (Tailscale MagicDNS suffix — zero owner config for Tailscale deployments).
  - Accept AGENT_MESH_DASHBOARD_ALLOWED_HOSTS (comma-separated extra hostnames for non-Tailscale tunnels).
  - Keep defaults: 127.0.0.1, localhost, ::1.
  - Parse via readDashboardAllowedHosts(env) — testable in isolation.

(3) AUTH INVARIANT: widening the Host allowlist NEVER removes auth. The token check and the Host check are separate layers. Passing one does not imply the other. Test explicitly: allowlisted Host without token → 401.

(4) ASK-ONLY CONCIERGE PATTERN: when exposing AI chat via mobile:
  - Spawn claude -p with read tools only (ask mode). Cannot write the repo.
  - Parse AI output as a fenced concierge-proposal block (structured intent, not raw commands).
  - The only write path is gh issue create — framework-side, not AI-side.
  - That write fires ONLY on explicit human Confirm tap (POST /api/concierge/confirm).
  - Label allowlist is fixed at the framework level — AI cannot inject arbitrary labels.
  - Test: gh must NOT fire on /api/concierge/message, ONLY on /api/concierge/confirm.

(5) ONE-TIME MOBILE SETUP: a helper script (mesh-mobile-serve.mjs) runs tailscale serve and prints the one-time https://<magicdns>/m?t=<token> link. User opens on phone → Add to Home Screen → installed as PWA.

(6) SECURITY INVARIANTS PRESERVED:
  - Ask-only concierge: read tools only, cannot write the repo.
  - Issue creation: framework-side + human-tap-gated + label-allowlisted.
  - No public exposure: 127.0.0.1 bind + Tailscale device identity.
  - Auth token enforced everywhere: Host allowlist ≠ auth bypass.
  - Path-guard, recursion guard, single-writable-root, anti-spoof surface untouched.

Provenance: PR #355 (2026-06-21).

provenance.source: PR #355: feat(dashboard): Mesh Mobile Concierge — private phone front-door

mobile-activity-feed-endpoint-reuse.l0: Mobile live-feed: reuse /api/activity-log (no new backend); extract pure display helpers zero-dep tested.
mobile-activity-feed-endpoint-reuse.l1: PR #357 (follow-up to #355): Recent activity card on /m Status reused /api/activity-log — no new endpoint. summarizeActivity (text truncation/agent label) and relTime (3m/2h/1d compact age) extracted as DOM-free pure helpers; 4 new test cases in test/mobile-pwa.test.js. One CSS property (.metric word-wrap) was the only layout change. L0: 254/254 green.
mobile-activity-feed-endpoint-reuse.value:
When adding a live-feed widget to a mobile or dashboard view:
(1) ENDPOINT AUDIT FIRST: check /api/* for an existing activity or log route before adding backend surface. /api/activity-log was already present — wiring the frontend to it cost zero backend lines.
(2) PURE HELPER EXTRACTION: extract all display/formatting logic (summarize text, format relative age) as DOM-free pure functions co-located with zero-dep unit tests. Never embed formatting logic in the render path.
(3) CSS IS SURGICAL: one property per new widget (e.g. word-wrap on .metric). If more is needed, reconsider the layout strategy.
(4) TEST FIRST, RENDER SECOND: 4 unit tests for the two helpers before any DOM integration. Pattern confirms frontend-extract-dom-free-first for the mobile context.
Provenance: PR #357 (2026-06-21), follow-up to PR #355 mobile-tailscale-askonly-concierge.

provenance.source: PR #357: feat(dashboard): mobile concierge Status — live activity feed

tailscale-serve-http-over-https.l0: tailscale serve: use --http=80 <port> not --https=443 — HTTPS Certs off by default; CLI rejects 127.0.0.1:<port>.
tailscale-serve-http-over-https.l1: Two bugs in mesh-mobile-serve.mjs (PR #359, found live on a real tailnet): (1) --https=443 requires the tailnet's HTTPS Certificates feature, which is OFF by default — tailscale serve exits 'does not support getting TLS certs'; (2) 127.0.0.1:<port> is rejected — the CLI wants a bare port. Both fail silently until runtime on a real tailnet.
tailscale-serve-http-over-https.value: Fix: `tailscale serve --bg --http=80 <port>`. Tailscale's WireGuard layer encrypts all tailnet traffic — HTTP over tailnet is private + encrypted without the HTTPS-cert feature. Set bootstrapUrl to emit `http://`. To opt into real HTTPS: enable 'HTTPS Certificates' + MagicDNS in the Tailscale admin console, then switch to `--https=443 <port>` (bare port, no 127.0.0.1 prefix). Verified live during phone front-door bring-up. Related: mobile-tailscale-askonly-concierge (broader mobile architecture).

provenance.source: PR #359: fix(mesh-mobile-serve): HTTP-over-tailnet serve (no TLS-cert dependency)

es-module-cors-http-origin.l0: ES module scripts always send Origin (CORS mode); stylesheets don't — https-only gate 403s module fetches silently.
es-module-cors-http-origin.l1: PR #368: app.js (<script type='module'>) was 403ing over Tailscale --http=80 while app.css returned 200. ES modules are fetched in CORS mode — browsers always send an Origin header. Stylesheets and classic scripts don't trigger CORS mode and never send Origin. The same-origin gate (passesSameOriginGate) accepted only `https://` for tailnet hosts; over plain-HTTP the Origin was `http://<host>` → 403 → module never executed → shell rendered without JS. Tests passed because curl and headless render never send a module Origin header; only a real browser over the tailnet does.
es-module-cors-http-origin.value:
Apply when implementing or auditing a same-origin gate for a dashboard or web server that may be accessed via HTTP (not just HTTPS) — Tailscale --http=80, local dev, internal proxies.

(1) ES modules fetch in CORS mode unconditionally: any `<script type='module'>` is fetched in CORS mode regardless of how it's requested. CORS mode means the browser ALWAYS attaches an `Origin` header. Classic scripts, stylesheets, and images do NOT trigger CORS mode and send no Origin — they pass origin gates that ES modules fail.

(2) The trap: a same-origin gate that accepts only `https://` for external/trusted hosts will silently 403 ES module fetches on any HTTP transport (`http://` Origin). The page renders a partial shell (CSS loads, no JS) with no console error surfaced to the user unless they open DevTools and look at network failures.

(3) Fix: in passesSameOriginGate (or equivalent), accept BOTH `http://` and `https://` when the host matches a trusted pattern:
  - For *.ts.net hosts: `origin === 'https://' + host || origin === 'http://' + host`
  - For AGENT_MESH_DASHBOARD_ALLOWED_HOSTS entries: same dual-scheme check.
  - Foreign origins (different host, any scheme): still rejected.

(4) Why 'tests passed' until real-browser-over-tailnet: curl never sends Origin. Localhost headless rendering (same-origin, no CORS) never sends Origin. Node-based integration tests send no Origin. Only a real browser fetching a cross-origin ES module emits the Origin header — and only when the transport is different enough to classify as 'not same-origin'. Test coverage gap: add explicit test cases `tailnet-module-Origin (http)` and `foreign-Origin (rejected)` to exercise both branches. PR #368 ships both.

(5) Defensive coding: log or annotate any same-origin gate that enforces scheme-matching with a comment naming the ES-module CORS exception. Future readers must not 'simplify' http-or-https to https-only — add a comment like `// ES module fetches send Origin even over http://; accept both schemes for trusted hosts`.

Provenance: PR #368 (2026-06-21): fix(dashboard): accept http Origin from tailnet hosts (fixes iPhone partial-load via Tailscale).

provenance.source: PR #368: fix(dashboard): accept http Origin from tailnet hosts (module 403 → phone partial-load)

ios-cookie-redirect-drop.l0: iOS Safari drops SameSite=Strict cookies set on redirect — serve 200 directly + use Lax + dual cookie/header auth.
ios-cookie-redirect-drop.l1: PR #363: iOS Safari dropped the SameSite=Strict cookie on the /m?t= 302 redirect → all CSS/JS subrequests 403'd → partial render. Fix (defense in depth): (1) /m?t= serves 200 directly, sets cookie on same response (no redirect hop); (2) SameSite=Strict → Lax (sent on top-level nav + same-site subrequests; CSRF unchanged because API always requires explicit token, never ambient cross-site POST); (3) /api/* accepts token via cookie OR X-Dashboard-Token header — PWA captures ?t= once and sends as header. Desktop / keeps its redirect.
ios-cookie-redirect-drop.value:
Apply when building a mobile PWA with token-in-URL authentication:

(1) NEVER set an auth cookie on a redirect response: iOS Safari drops Set-Cookie on 3xx in some SameSite+HTTPS combinations. Serve the token-handoff page directly on 200 with the Set-Cookie header on that SAME response. Pattern: GET /m?t=<token> → 200 + Set-Cookie; the desktop GET / 302 redirect path is unaffected.

(2) SameSite=Lax, not Strict: Strict cookies are not sent on same-site subresource requests after top-level navigation — CSS/JS/XHR all fail auth. Lax is correct when the cookie must be present on both the initial nav AND subsequent subresource requests. CSRF posture is unchanged when APIs always validate an explicit token (never rely on ambient cookies from cross-site POSTs).

(3) Dual-mode auth for APIs: accept the token via HTTP-only cookie OR X-Dashboard-Token header. The PWA captures ?t= once on first load and attaches it as a header on every API call — fully cookie-independent. This survives future cookie-quirk regressions without a second partial-load failure.

(4) Public shell, gated API: serve the static UI shell (HTML/CSS/JS) unauthenticated so the page always renders. Protect only /api/* data and action routes. Non-sensitive UI code is safe unauthenticated; all sensitive operations require the explicit token.

Related: mobile-tailscale-askonly-concierge (broader mobile architecture). Provenance: PR #363 (2026-06-21).

provenance.source: PR #363: fix(dashboard): mobile auth survives iOS cookie quirks (partial-load fix)

cross-session-history-server-side.l0: PWA chat continuity: persist history JSONL server-side (LRU cap), pre-load into context, suppress greeting on resume.
cross-session-history-server-side.l1: PR #366: a browser PWA can be closed, rebooted, or evicted — the client cannot own conversation state. Fix: persist history server-side as JSONL (LRU-trim to AGENT_MESH_CONCIERGE_HISTORY_MAX=200), expose GET /api/concierge/history?limit=N for UI re-hydration, and have message() pre-load the last 10 server-side turns into buildPrompt automatically — model sees context without the client resending. Suppress the greeting when history already exists; a welcome banner after prior turns is disorienting.
cross-session-history-server-side.value:
Apply when building any HTTP-served AI chat where the client is ephemeral (mobile PWA, browser tab, thin web client).

(1) SERVER-SIDE STORAGE: append each turn ({role, content, ts}) to an append-only JSONL file at a stable server-owned path (e.g. <meshRoot>/mesh/<service>/history.jsonl). LRU-trim on append: keep the last MAX_ENTRIES entries (env-configurable, default 200). The file never grows unboundedly; trim is an in-memory array slice before the write.

(2) HISTORY ENDPOINT: GET /api/<service>/history?limit=N returns the last N stored messages. Return [] on missing file (first-boot graceful). Never 500 — fail-open. Client calls this on load to re-hydrate the conversation UI.

(3) MODEL PRE-LOAD: in the server-side message(userText) handler, read the last M server-side turns (e.g. M=10) and prepend them to buildPrompt BEFORE spawning the model. The client does NOT need to resend history — the server owns state continuity. Client-provided history is supplementary context, not authoritative.

(4) GREETING SUPPRESSION: when the client loads and finds history.length > 0, skip the opening greeting. A welcome banner after an established conversation is disorienting. Pattern: if (historyFromServer.length === 0) showGreeting().

(5) GRACEFUL FALLBACK FOR OLD SERVERS: if /history is absent or errors, client falls back to empty state — no crash. Pattern: const hist = res.ok ? await res.json() : []; wrap in try/catch → [].

(6) MULTI-DEVICE SUPPORT: because history is server-side, any device calling GET /history sees the same conversation — cross-device continuity is automatic, no client-side sync protocol needed.

Provenance: PR #366 (2026-06-21), closing Issue #362. Related: mobile-tailscale-askonly-concierge (concierge architecture), mobile-activity-feed-endpoint-reuse (endpoint-reuse pattern).

provenance.source: PR #366: feat: server-side concierge history for cross-session continuity (#362)

alert-episode-set-diff-dedup.l0: Alert dedup: openAlerts[] + set-diff → open new-critical, close recovered. One issue per critical episode.
alert-episode-set-diff-dedup.l1: PR #367 (planHealthAlerts in health-alert.js): filing a new issue every CRITICAL sweep tick creates duplicates that flood the queue. Solution: carry openAlerts:[key] in a state file. Pure planner computes: opens = current-critical keys NOT in prevOpen (new episode); closes = prevOpen keys no longer critical (recovered); nextState.openAlerts = [...current-critical]. Planner inherits the health-model's honesty rule — only reacts to what the model already classified; never re-judges liveness.
alert-episode-set-diff-dedup.value:
Apply when any monitoring or health-sweep loop must open exactly one GitHub issue per critical episode and auto-close it on recovery.

(1) State file carries {generatedAt, openAlerts:[key]} — key = namespaced alert id per monitored unit (e.g. 'mesh-health-alert:agents/critical'). Persisted between sweep ticks.

(2) Pure planner({report, prev}) returns {opens, closes, state}:
  opens = current-critical keys NOT in prev.openAlerts → file a new issue
  closes = prev.openAlerts keys no longer critical → close the issue
  state.openAlerts = [...current-critical]

(3) Host shell applies the plan: gh issue create per open, gh issue close per close, then atomically writes new state. Planner has zero I/O — fully unit-testable with constructed inputs.

(4) Re-entry behavior: an organ staying critical across ticks remains in openAlerts — neither opens nor closes (no duplicate issues). Recovery then re-critical = a new open (new episode).

(5) Honesty inheritance: planner must NOT re-classify organ status. It only reacts to what the underlying model already classified, preserving the model's honesty invariants (e.g. never false-dead for idle on-demand agents).

Distinct from daemon-health-observer-pattern (count-based seenCount flap-suppression) and soft-launch-clean-clock-watchdog (wall-clock clean-clock). Provenance: PR #367 (2026-06-21), src/mesh-health/health-alert.js, closes Issue #361.

provenance.source: PR #367: idea: health vital signs → proactive needs-human alert when any organ transitions to critical/dead (#361)

render-nested-api-guard-leaf-scalar.l0: Never pass nested API objects to String() — type-guard then drill to leaf scalar; test with the real response shape.
render-nested-api-guard-leaf-scalar.l1: PR #373: mobile Status tab showed 'Tokens [object Object]' — summarizeStatus passed daily.tokens (an {total:{input,output,costUsd}} nested object) to String(). Fix: (1) check typeof x === 'object' before accessing nested props; (2) drill to leaf scalars (tot.costUsd, tot.input + tot.output) via a typed intermediate; (3) tolerate flat fallbacks only when typeof !== 'object'. Tests must use the REAL api shape — a stub with tokens:42 passes but misses the nested-object bug entirely.

provenance.source: PR #373: fix(dashboard): mobile Status renders real daily-report shape (no [object Object])

dev-mesh-agent-path-convention.l0: New dev-mesh agents live at dev-mesh/<name>/ directly — not dev-mesh/agents/<name>/; wrong path breaks doctor wiring.
dev-mesh-agent-path-convention.l1: PR #377: concierge was first placed at dev-mesh/agents/concierge/ then moved to dev-mesh/concierge/ to match mesh root convention. All agents are direct subdirs of dev-mesh/ (not an agents/ intermediate dir). doctor's add/validate/doctor commands enumerate direct subdirs as agents; intermediate nesting breaks conformance checks, registry.json absolute spawn paths, peer-bridge .mcp.json MESH_ROOT stamps, and board-notify hook paths.
dev-mesh-agent-path-convention.value:
Apply when adding any new dev-mesh agent role:

(1) Path: create at dev-mesh/<name>/ (direct subdir of mesh root), NOT dev-mesh/agents/<name>/. doctor treats each direct subdir of the mesh root as a candidate agent directory; any deeper nesting makes it invisible to validate/add/doctor commands.

(2) Implication: generated wiring files embed absolute paths including the agent root. An agent placed at dev-mesh/agents/concierge/ will have registry.json peer entries pointing to $MESH_ROOT/agents/concierge/ — not the canonical form. Re-running doctor --apply after moving fixes all generated wiring, but move rich content (AGENT.md, agent.json, prompts/) manually before running doctor so it does not overwrite.

(3) Detection: run agent-mesh validate dev-mesh → Conformance: OK when all agents are at the correct depth. A 'directory not found' or conformance failure on a newly-added agent almost always means path depth is wrong.

(4) Recovery: (a) cp -r dev-mesh/agents/<name> dev-mesh/<name>; (b) rm -rf dev-mesh/agents/ (or just the wrong subdir); (c) update mesh.json roster and wiring test paths; (d) re-run doctor --apply to regenerate absolute-path wiring.

(5) Convention: the directory name (e.g. concierge) becomes the agent's canonical peer name in registry.json, mesh.json, and board identity. Keep it lowercase, no separators — matches all 10 existing dev-mesh agents (tester, triager, analyst, maintainer, orchestrator, coder, reviewer, curator, security, concierge).

Related: new-agent-scaffold-completeness (scaffold dirs), doctor-apply-on-framework-upgrade (re-run doctor after framework changes). Provenance: PR #377 (2026-06-22) — fix commit: 'agent lives at dev-mesh/concierge (not dev-mesh/agents/) — match mesh root convention'.

provenance.source: PR #377: feat(concierge): promote the concierge to a first-class mesh agent — fix commit corrected path convention

concurrent-api-form-unify.l0: Merge of concurrent API forms: accept both DI+file-path shapes; return both field aliases; drop duplicate exports.
concurrent-api-form-unify.l1: PR #369 mergefix: HEAD added file-path constructor (historyPath/historyMax); main added injectable DI (appendHistory/loadHistory). If the merge picks one style, the other side's tests break or the client reads an empty key silently. Pattern: accept BOTH constructor shapes; expose both method aliases (getHistory/history); return BOTH API keys (history+turns); store entries under BOTH field names (text+reply); remove duplicate named exports at merge time. Applicable whenever two feature branches independently add I/O integration to the same module.
concurrent-api-form-unify.value:
Pattern: when two feature branches independently add I/O to the same module and land concurrently, unify by accepting both forms.

The failure mode:
- HEAD (PR #366) adds injectable DI to concierge.js: {appendHistory, loadHistory, contextTurns} constructor params for test-friendly injection.
- main already added direct file-path I/O: {historyPath, historyMax} constructor params for production persistence.
- A merge that keeps only one style silently breaks the other: tests that inject stubs fail if only file-path is accepted; the client reads data.history but the server returns data.turns (or vice versa).

Four-part unification pattern (PR #369 mergefix):

(1) DUAL CONSTRUCTOR: accept both input shapes in the same constructor. Check injectable form first; fall back to file-path:
  constructor({ appendHistory, loadHistory, contextTurns, historyPath, historyMax, ... })
  this._appendHistory = appendHistory || (turn => appendJsonl(historyPath, turn));
  this._loadHistory = loadHistory || (() => readJsonl(historyPath).slice(-historyMax));

(2) METHOD ALIASES: expose both getHistory() and history() pointing to the same implementation. Branch code calling either succeeds without modification.

(3) DUAL RESPONSE KEYS: the route handler returns BOTH field names:
  return { history: turns, turns };
Clients on each branch read their expected key; neither receives undefined silently.

(4) DUAL VALUE ALIASES: when entries have two field names for the same datum (e.g. text and reply for assistant response), store BOTH in the persisted record. Reader code expecting either field succeeds.

(5) DUPLICATE EXPORT REMOVAL: when both branches export a default constant of the same name and value (e.g. DEFAULT_CONCIERGE_HISTORY_MAX from config.js), the merged file has a duplicate named export. Remove one at merge time — a duplicate named export is a syntax error in ESM.

(6) CLIENT CONSUMPTION ALIGNMENT: check which response key the currently-deployed client reads (e.g. data.turns vs data.history) and ensure that key is authoritative. Return both for compat; note which is primary in a comment.

Test coverage for dual-form merges:
- Unit-test both constructor shapes independently (injectable and file-path) — not just the happy path.
- Assert both response keys are present in the route response.
- Assert the client reads the correct (authoritative) key.

Related: multi-bot-shared-file-conflicts (concurrent PRs conflicting on shared memory keys; mergefix resolves at the key level — this lesson applies when the conflict is a competing API design, not just overlapping data). Provenance: PR #369 (2026-06-21), mergefix commit 2681c9727d — 'Unified two competing history API designs from HEAD and main.'

provenance.source: PR #369: feat(concierge): server-side conversation history — mergefix unified two competing API designs

dashboard-pure-model-dual-surface.l0: New dashboard view: DOM-free pure model → route → view. One model serves desktop + phone; escape user content at view.
dashboard-pure-model-dual-surface.l1: PR #381 (A2A Task Board): `buildTaskBoard(tasks)` — DOM-free pure function grouping tasks into state columns, fully unit-tested with zero imports. Route `GET /api/board/tasks` reads store + auth-gates like every /api/*; returns `{ok,tasks}` with tolerant [] fallback. Desktop (`tasks-view.js`) and phone tab (`mobile/app.js`) both import the same model. Agent-authored task briefs are HTML-escaped at the view layer — never injected raw. Result: 3 separate test files, zero jsdom, full coverage. Read-only enforced by omission: no write route = no write possible.
dashboard-pure-model-dual-surface.value:
Pattern for adding a new read-only data-backed dashboard view:

1. DOM-free pure model (`src/dashboard/public/<noun>-model.js`): one export `buildXxx(rawData)` → groups/summaries. No fetch, no document, zero imports. Test with hand-computed expected values — no jsdom needed.

2. Route (`GET /api/<noun>/data`): reads storage, auth+host-gates like every `/api/*`, returns `{ok, data}` with tolerant empty fallback `[]`. Unit-tested with the server fake-auth pattern.

3. View file (`<noun>-view.js`): imports the model, renders via DOM or string concat. Escape all user-controlled content (agent briefs, external labels) — never `.innerHTML = untrustedString`. Phone tab reuses the same model via browser-only dynamic import.

4. Read-only safety by omission: no write route + no write button = no mutation possible. Prefer this over per-route write guards for display-only features.

5. Naming: `build<Noun>` → `<noun>-model.js`; `<noun>-view.js`; `GET /api/<area>/<noun>`. Consistent naming aids discovery.

Provenance: PR #381 (2026-06-22) — A2A Task Board kanban view (desktop + phone).

provenance.source: PR #381: feat(taskboard): A2A Task Board view — read-only ticket kanban (desktop + phone)

phone-board-orchestrator-team-lead.l0: Phone task → board ticket → orchestrator team-lead: daemon triggers, agent conducts team (ask-only fanOutToPeers).
phone-board-orchestrator-team-lead.l1: PR #383: When a phone message requests substantive work, concierge routes via assign_task to the orchestrator (not a specialist). The orchestrator is the team lead — owns its board ticket (assigned→done) and coordinates specialists via a conductor workflow + parallel fanOutToPeers (ask-only). Daemon schedules board-drive (every 10min) as the trigger-only mechanism; the agent executes the workflow. Status reuses the existing Task Board surface — desktop board2 refresh() + phone pickPoll (15s, visibility-aware) — no new HTTP routes.
phone-board-orchestrator-team-lead.value:
Apply when wiring phone-initiated substantive tasks into an autonomous agent team:

(1) Routing persona: update the concierge prompt to distinguish chat/status queries (answer inline) from substantive work/build/investigate requests. Substantive → assign_task to the orchestrator peer. Never route directly to specialists — the orchestrator is the coordination layer.

(2) Team-lead board pickup: the orchestrator has a daemon-scheduled board-drive delegate job (kind:delegate, every:10, in schedule.json). Its prompt lists its tasks (list_my_tasks), acknowledges, runs a staged conductor workflow (analyst/coder/tester/reviewer phases), fans out to specialists via fanOutToPeers (ask-only), synthesizes results, and advances its OWN ticket (only the `to` agent may advance).

(3) Daemon triggers, agent works: the scheduler fires board-drive on cadence (agent-driven autonomy); the agent decides what to do. This keeps the daemon thin — schedule.json contains only the firing cadence and prompt, not the logic.

(4) Status reuse: No new API route needed. Desktop Task Board (board2.js) already has a refresh() loop (SSE+30s). Phone (mobile/app.js) uses pickPoll — polls the active data tab at 15s, paused on document.hidden. Add the Tasks tab to the poll targets and the board updates live.

(5) Ask-only discipline: all specialist delegation is ask-only. do-mode for the orchestrator (running the team workflow) is valid; do-mode for the specialists is deferred. Board invariants unchanged: identity framework-set, only `to` advances, brief is data.

Related: task-board-interactive-handoff (board fundamentals), scheduler-builtin-for-native-jobs (kind:delegate vs kind:builtin). Provenance: PR #383 (2026-06-22).

provenance.source: PR #383: feat(board-pipeline): orchestrator team-lead — phone task → autonomous team workflow → live status

pwa-voice-swappable-adapter.l0: PWA voice: put STT+TTS behind a fixed adapter interface — swap cloud vs native without touching UI.
pwa-voice-swappable-adapter.l1: PR #388: `createVoiceEngine` wraps Web Speech API (STT+TTS) in a stable interface; a cloud engine (OpenAI/ElevenLabs) replaces only this module without UI changes. DOM-free + injectable → zero-dep L0 tests with fake SpeechRecognition/speechSynthesis. Voice-in → voice-out auto-read; typed turns stay silent. TTS language auto-inferred from content (CJK→zh-CN, else en-US); STT language is a manual toggle (native engines weak on mixed zh/en).
pwa-voice-swappable-adapter.value:
Apply when adding voice to a browser PWA or web app:

(1) Adapter interface: define `createVoiceEngine(opts)` returning `{ start(), stop(), sttSupported, onresult, onerror, onend }`. Back it with native Web Speech API on first ship. The stable interface means a cloud engine (OpenAI Whisper/TTS, ElevenLabs) can replace only this module later — UI, app.js, and tests never change.

(2) DOM-free + injectable: the adapter must not access DOM directly; inject any needed DOM refs as opts. This makes it testable with a fake: set `global.SpeechRecognition` / `global.speechSynthesis` in tests, covering support detection (incl. `webkitSpeechRecognition` for iOS Safari), single-utterance config, trimmed transcript, `onend` reset, stop, error surfacing, TTS cancel-then-speak, and language inference. Zero deps needed.

(3) Voice-in → voice-out only: only voice-initiated turns trigger TTS readback; typed turns stay silent. Prevents unwanted audio for users who type into a voice-enabled UI. Implement by flagging whether the last send was voice-triggered.

(4) Language handling: native STT engines are weak at mixed-language input (e.g. zh/en code-switching). Use a manual lang chip/toggle for STT. TTS can be auto-inferred from the reply text: if CJK characters present → `zh-CN`, else `en-US` (or primary locale). Keep inference as a pure function for easy unit testing.

(5) Feature gating: hide mic UI (button + lang chip) when `sttSupported` is false. Never hard-fail when speech APIs are absent — degrade gracefully to text-only.

(6) Auth path unchanged: voice fills the existing text input and sends via the existing message API. No new server permissions required beyond the browser's own mic prompt.

Provenance: PR #388 (2026-06-22) — feat(pwa): bidirectional voice for the mobile concierge.

provenance.source: PR #388: feat(pwa): bidirectional voice for the mobile concierge — mic STT + reply TTS

board-ticket-daemon-driver-only.l0: Only daemon-driver agents reliably pick up board tickets in headless — session-hook specialists stall at acknowledged.
board-ticket-daemon-driver-only.l1: PR #387: concierge's assign_task routed to 'analyst' — a session-hook-only specialist. The board-notify SessionStart hook fires only in interactive sessions; silent in headless deploy. Only the orchestrator has a daemon-scheduled board-drive job (every 10min in schedule.json). Ticket stuck ~3h at 'acknowledged'. Health Vitals flags this CRITICAL, but prevent-at-source beats detect-after-stall.
board-ticket-daemon-driver-only.value: Gate assign_task (dispatch.js) to board lead only (orchestrator/deps.boardLead). Reject other peers with an error naming ask_peer_rerun for synchronous one-off asks. Invariant: board tickets go only to agents with a daemon board-drive cadence in schedule.json — never session-hook-only agents. Tests: lead-accept, specialist-reject, configurable boardLead dep. Provenance: PR #387 (2026-06-22).

provenance.source: PR #387: fix(concierge): route durable board tickets to the team lead — stop driverless stalls

ios-tts-gesture-unlock.l0: iOS Safari TTS: unlock with a silent utterance in a tap before async speak() — post-fetch callbacks silently drop TTS.
ios-tts-gesture-unlock.l1: PR #391: concierge voice reply was silently dropped on iOS — `speechSynthesis.speak()` called in a post-`fetch` callback is outside a user gesture; iOS Safari discards it. Fix: `engine.unlock()` primes a silent (volume 0) utterance inside the mic tap, satisfying iOS's requirement. All subsequent async `speak()` calls work for the session. Idempotent — no-op where TTS is already unlocked. Also: a 🔊 'read replies aloud' toggle persists to localStorage, enabling voice-out on typed turns too. Spoken when `speakReplies || lastWasVoice`.

provenance.source: PR #391: fix(pwa): make the concierge reply actually speak — iOS TTS unlock + 🔊 readback toggle

claude-api-529-retry-resilience.l0: Claude API 529 overload → jitter sleep on re-run (composite action) + annotate infra_auth in assert-run-healthy.
claude-api-529-retry-resilience.l1: PR #393 / Issue #386: Claude API 529 'overloaded' burns 3-4 internal retries (~5min) then exits 1 — false-red on run history. Two fixes: (1) .github/actions/mesh-retry-backoff composite action sleeps 3-5min (180 + RANDOM%120 s) when github.run_attempt > 1; added before the claude step in all 11 dev-mesh workflows with matching if: guard. (2) assert-run-healthy.mjs detects '529'/'overloaded'/'overload_error' in error output and emits ::error title=infra_auth:: with a 'Re-run failed jobs' hint — Triager classifies as infra, not code bug.
claude-api-529-retry-resilience.value:
Two-part pattern for making Claude Code action workflows resilient to transient API 529 overload errors:

(1) Back-off on re-run — composite action:
Create .github/actions/mesh-retry-backoff/action.yml:
  runs:
    using: composite
    steps:
      - if: ${{ github.run_attempt > 1 }}
        shell: bash
        run: |
          secs=$(( 180 + RANDOM % 120 ))
          sleep "$secs"
Add as a single step immediately before the anthropics/claude-code-action step in every affected workflow. Carry the same if: condition guard as the claude step so the sleep fires only when claude would have run (e.g. conditional workflows like autofix/mergefix need the same guard or the sleep step always fires).

Why jitter: RANDOM%120 spreads retried runs across a 2-min window, avoiding re-collision on a still-overloaded slot. Fixed sleeps all hit the same point.

(2) Classify 529 in assert-run-healthy.mjs:
Detect before general error checks:
  const looksOverloaded = /529|overloaded|overload_error/i.test(output);
  if (isError && looksOverloaded) {
    core.error('Claude API overloaded (529). Re-run failed jobs.', { title: 'infra_auth' });
    process.exit(1);
  }
Emit ::error title=infra_auth:: so the Triager classifies the run as infrastructure failure, not a code bug. Run history shows a clear actionable reason instead of a generic exit-1.

(3) Scope: apply composite action + 529 detection to ALL workflows calling anthropics/claude-code-action that pass through assert-run-healthy.mjs — never just a subset. A 529 can hit any role.

(4) Max retries: 2 attempts × 3–5 min jitter, on top of the CLI's own 3–4 internal retries. Total recovery window per attempt: ~5–10 min.

(5) No auto-rerun: back-off fires only on manual 'Re-run failed jobs'. No actions:write permission change needed. The infra_auth annotation tells the operator exactly what action to take.

(6) Test coverage: assert 529/overloaded/overload_error in is_error output → infra_auth annotation + exit 1; existing errored/noop/blocked tests unchanged. Workflow structural test: assert mesh-retry-backoff step appears before the claude step in all 11 target workflows.

Provenance: PR #393 (2026-06-22), closes Issue #386.

provenance.source: PR #393: feat(dev-mesh): 529-retry resilience — composite back-off action + infra_auth annotation

flat-store-archive-sweep.l0: Flat-dir task stores accumulate done records — add an archive sweep or listTasks and health triage inflate unboundedly.
flat-store-archive-sweep.l1: PR #399 / #396: mesh/board/tasks/ grows forever — every task, every state. Weeks of daemon + concierge activity → hundreds of stale done files; listTasks() scans all, health triage inflates, Done column clutters. Fix: pure planner (select terminal tasks past age threshold → archive/YYYY-MM/) + thin applier (atomic write then remove from tasks/). ID-collision trap: the nextTaskId scan must also walk archive/**/ or archived IDs recycle. Backward compat: default listTasks() reads tasks/ only; { includeArchived: true } for forensic queries.
flat-store-archive-sweep.value:
Apply when any flat-directory store accumulates terminal records unboundedly:

1. **Pure planner + thin applier**
   Planner (pure fn): input = task list + age threshold + clock; output = [{ task, destPath }]. destPath = archive/YYYY-MM/ from completion month.
   Applier: atomicWrite(dest) then unlink(src). One file per task, atomic write-before-delete.

2. **Age gate** (default 7 days)
   Only terminal tasks older than threshold are moved. Config: AGENT_MESH_BOARD_ARCHIVE_AGE_MS (default 604800000 ms). Disable: AGENT_MESH_BOARD_ARCHIVE_DISABLED.

3. **ID-collision trap (critical)**
   The nextTaskId scan for a <from>-<to> pair walks active tasks/ only. Once a task is archived its ID disappears from that scan — the next task for the same pair recycles the counter. Fix: extend nextTaskId to also walk archive/**/ so archived IDs stay in the collision-avoidance set.

4. **Backward compatibility**
   listTasks({ includeArchived: false }) — default, reads tasks/ only. Existing consumers (Task Board, health-collect.js, triage_logs) see only the lean active set automatically.
   listTasks({ includeArchived: true }) — also reads archive/**/ for forensic queries.

5. **Crash safety / idempotency**
   Write-before-delete: a crash after write leaves a duplicate in tasks/. Re-run detects the archive file already exists and skips (no-op). Never a data loss.

6. **Test cases to cover**
   Aged done → archived; recent done → kept; in-flight never archived. Move integrity: byte-identical, present in archive/, absent from tasks/. nextTaskId no recycling: create → archive → create same pair → distinct counters. listTasks default excludes archive; includeArchived:true returns both. Idempotency: re-run after archive is a no-op.

Provenance: spec PR #399 (2026-06-22), closes Issue #396.

provenance.source: PR #399: spec: board task archive — done tasks accumulate forever, clutter active view (#396)

confirm-gated-dispatch-audit-log.l0: Confirm-gated dispatch actions need an off-path audit JSONL — they leave no persistent searchable record otherwise.
confirm-gated-dispatch-audit-log.l1: The concierge dispatch system (file_issue/assign_task/ask_peer_rerun) fires, returns, and leaves no persistent record. Fix: append-only JSONL at <mesh-root>/mesh/concierge-audit.jsonl. Four invariants: (1) off the response path — append failure → warn, not dispatch failure; (2) sanitize per-action — bounds on body/task/context fields + /token|secret|key|auth|pass/i stripping on all keys; (3) trim when max is set uses a file lock (best-effort: skip on lock failure); (4) read via token-gated GET /api/concierge/audit?limit=N, newest-first, tolerant of malformed lines. Provenance: PR #398 spec.
confirm-gated-dispatch-audit-log.value:
Apply when adding an audit log to any human-confirm-gated action dispatcher (concierge, MCP tool-with-side-effects, webhook relay).

(1) Off-response-path append: call appendEntry(entry) AFTER the action succeeds, fire-and-forget. An append failure must log a warning and NOT alter or fail the dispatch result. Pattern: dispatchAction(...).then(result => { appendEntry(entry).catch(warn); return result; }).

(2) Payload sanitizer — pure, table-testable: sanitize(action, payload) → sanitizedPayload. Per-action rules: bound large free-text fields (body: 2000 chars, task/context: 1000 chars, requirements/pointers: 500 chars). Backstop rule for ALL actions: any top-level key matching /token|secret|key|auth|pass/i → "[redacted]". The sanitizer bounds and redacts; it never strips identity fields (peer, title, labels).

(3) Trim with lock: when AGENT_MESH_CONCIERGE_AUDIT_MAX is set, trim read-drop-rewrite path must hold an exclusive file lock (sidecar .lock or O_EXCL). Lock failure → skip trim this cycle (best-effort, file grows past cap temporarily). A write error during trim → warn and leave original untouched. Regular appends use fs.appendFile (OS-atomic for single-writer); the lock protects only the rewrite path.

(4) Token-gated read endpoint GET /api/concierge/audit?limit=N: tail-read last N entries, skip malformed lines (tolerant), return newest-first. Default limit when absent. Auth gate identical to /api/board/tasks and /api/health. Never 500 — fail-open with [].

(5) Entry shape: {timestamp, action, payload (sanitized), result, session_id?}. session_id present when the dispatch carries a chat session id; absent otherwise — omit from JSON (not null).

(6) Required test matrix: append on success (each action → one correctly-shaped line); no log on failure/cancel; sanitization (secret key stripped, intent fields preserved); result capture (URL/task_id/summary per action type); append non-blocking (simulated failure → warn, result unchanged); GET history (last-N newest-first, limit honored); auth gate (unauthenticated → rejected); tolerant read (malformed line skipped, others returned); trim (past cap → drops oldest, holds lock).

Provenance: PR #398 spec (2026-06-22), closes Issue #397.

provenance.source: PR #398: spec: idea: concierge dispatch audit log — tap-gated actions have no persistent record (#397)

http-a2a-sse-streaming-opt-in.l0: HTTP A2A SSE streaming is opt-in via Accept: text/event-stream — batch path unchanged, stdio transport untouched.
http-a2a-sse-streaming-opt-in.l1: spec 2026-06-22 (#401 → PR #407): http-server.js optionally streams task progress as SSE when caller sends Accept: text/event-stream. Negotiation happens after auth. Emits in-progress → partial stdout chunks → heartbeat (AGENT_MESH_A2A_SSE_HEARTBEAT_MS) → terminal TaskCompleted. TaskCompleted payload is identical to the batch response. stdio-server.js stays batch-only; callers without the header are unaffected.
http-a2a-sse-streaming-opt-in.value:
Implement HTTP A2A SSE streaming in http-server.js:

(1) NEGOTIATION — after the existing auth gate, inspect Accept: if it includes 'text/event-stream' and SSE is not disabled (config flag), switch to SSE mode; else fall through to the unchanged batch path.

(2) RESPONSE FRAMING — set Content-Type: text/event-stream and flush headers immediately (before claude starts). Do not buffer the response start.

(3) EVENT SEQUENCE — in order:
  a. Emit TaskStatusUpdateEvent { state: 'in-progress' } when claude -p spawns.
  b. Forward stdout chunks as partial events while running.
  c. Emit heartbeat events every AGENT_MESH_A2A_SSE_HEARTBEAT_MS seconds regardless of output.
  d. On exit: emit TaskCompleted with content identical to the batch result → close stream.
  e. On failure/timeout: emit terminal error event → close stream. Never a silent hang; failure-as-data invariant applies.

(4) BATCH EQUIVALENCE — the TaskCompleted payload must equal what batch returns for the same task. Tests must assert this explicitly (same task → same content, different delivery).

(5) STDIO UNTOUCHED — stdio-server.js has no SSE code path and must not be given one.

(6) NON-GOALS — do not scope-creep: WebSocket transport; Last-Event-ID resumption; cross-hop streaming (each HTTP hop streams its own response independently); cancellation-on-disconnect semantics; dashboard/concierge UI rendering (server-side delivery only).

(7) REQUIRED TESTS: SSE vs batch negotiation (with/without Accept header); event ordering (in-progress → partials → terminal); result equivalence (TaskCompleted payload = batch); heartbeat fires before any output; failure emits terminal error event; auth gate rejects unauthenticated SSE; config disable flag forces batch even with Accept header; client-disconnect triggers cleanup; stdio-server has no SSE path.

Provenance: spec PR #407 (2026-06-22), idea issue #401.

provenance.source: PR #407: spec: idea: SSE streaming task progress for HTTP A2A peers (#401)

prompt-nudge-unit-test-gap.l0: Tool-description nudge: unit test proves the string exists, not the regression — close perf issues on eval re-run.
prompt-nudge-unit-test-gap.l1: PR #406 / #404: quality_per_1k_tokens -16.9% in 3x-disjoint cell — model broadcast fan_out_to_peers for single-owner queries. Fix: 4-line description nudge steering model to prefer delegate_to_peer. Unit test asserts /prefer delegate_to_peer/i — proves the lever (string) exists, not that the model now routes better. Real evidence: re-run the eval-matrix cell, read agentmesh/metrics quality_per_1k_tokens. Close perf issues on the eval re-run, not on green unit tests.
prompt-nudge-unit-test-gap.value:
Tool-description nudge pattern for model behavioral performance regressions:

(1) Identify the over-routing anti-pattern
When the model consistently uses a high-cost tool (fan_out_to_peers: spawns 1 worker per peer) for queries that a single targeted peer could answer, the result is N× token cost for no quality gain. Signal: drop in quality_per_1k_tokens in a disjoint routing eval cell.

(2) Minimal lever: add guidance to the tool description
A 3-5 sentence nudge in the tool's description string steers the model to the cheaper path without changing behavior, schema, or invariants. Example (PR #406): appended 'Prefer delegate_to_peer when ONE peer clearly owns the domain (see list_peers): fan-out spawns a worker per peer, so broadcasting a query only one peer can answer multiplies token cost for no quality gain. Reserve fan_out_to_peers for genuinely redundant/best-of-N, adversarial, or multi-domain queries where each peer adds value.'

(3) Test what you can, but be honest about what the test proves
Write a unit test asserting the nudge string is present (e.g., assert.match(tool.description, /prefer delegate_to_peer/i)). This proves the lever exists and prevents accidental removal — it does NOT prove the regression is reversed. A passing unit test closes nothing.

(4) Real evidence: eval-matrix re-run
Re-run the affected eval-matrix cell (e.g. 3x-disjoint) and read agentmesh/metrics quality_per_1k_tokens from the run record. Only a recovered metric justifies closing the perf issue.

(5) Issue lifecycle
Do NOT close the perf issue on green CI. Close it on the eval re-run confirming the metric recovered. If the nudge is insufficient, escalate to a behavioral mechanism (e.g. a pre-flight warning or cap).

Provenance: PR #406 (2026-06-22), closes #404 (quality_per_1k_tokens -16.9% in 3x-disjoint cell).

provenance.source: PR #406: [mesh-scan] perf-regression: quality_per_1k_tokens regressed (-16.9%) (#404)

board-task-dep-gate-at-pickup.l0: Board task dep ordering: gate-at-pickup only; blocked is not a state; blockedBy is derived; dependsOn immutable.
board-task-dep-gate-at-pickup.l1: PR #408 / Issue #400: orchestrator board-drive fans out tasks (analyze→implement→review) with no framework-enforced ordering. Fix: optional dependsOn:[taskId] stamped at creation, immutable (anti-spoof). Gate fires only at assigned→acknowledged: if any dep not done, acknowledge returns {error:blocked, blockedBy:[...]}, task stays assigned. Blocked is NOT a new state. blockedBy is derived at read time from live dep states, never stored. Backward-compat: dependsOn:[] = today.
board-task-dep-gate-at-pickup.value:
Apply when extending the board state machine with task ordering guarantees:

(1) GATE-AT-PICKUP only
Enforce ordering at the assigned→acknowledged transition exclusively. Gates at later transitions create false blocking after the dep resolves. Gates at creation cannot know future dep state. The pickup gate is the earliest and most useful enforcement point.

(2) Framework-stamp, immutable
dependsOn is set by create_task_for_peer and cannot be modified post-creation — same anti-spoof rule as from/to/id. Any attempt to update or clear dependsOn after creation has no effect.

(3) Blocked is NOT a state
The state machine stays assigned→acknowledged→in-progress→done. Blocked = assigned + unresolved deps. Do NOT add a blocked state — it creates invalid transitions and complicates the lifecycle. Visibility comes from the blockedBy badge, not from a new state.

(4) blockedBy is derived, never stored
Compute (task, resolveState) → [taskId] at read time from live dep states. Never persist a blockedBy array in the task record — it becomes stale and requires cache invalidation. Return it only from list_my_tasks as a computed read-only field.

(5) Failure-is-data
A blocked acknowledge returns { error: blocked, blockedBy: [...] } — not a throw. The task stays assigned, visible on the board badge, and eligible for stale-task detection (#219).

(6) Acyclicity at creation
On create_task_for_peer, validate: dep task exists on the same board, is not the task being created (no self-ref), and introducing this dep does not create a cycle. Any violation → structured error returned, task not created.

(7) done-only resolution
A dep is resolved only when its task reaches done. No partial, soft, or in-progress resolution in v1.

(8) Gate ordering: dep check precedes identity check
A blocked task reports blocked even when called by the wrong identity — check deps first. A dep-resolved but wrong-identity call still fails on the identity gate. This ordering is intentional: an agent should know its task is blocked before learning it is unauthorized.

(9) Backward-compat
Tasks with dependsOn:[] (or field absent) behave exactly as today. The gate is purely additive.

Provenance: spec PR #408 (2026-06-22), closes Issue #400.

provenance.source: PR #408: spec: idea: board task dependency sequencing — dependsOn gate for framework-enforced task ordering (#400)

routing-disjoint-fastpath-skip-deliberat.l0: Disjoint routing over-spends on LLM turns — score peers first, skip deliberation for clear winners (30s→0s, lossless).
routing-disjoint-fastpath-skip-deliberat.l1: PR #414 / #413: the 3x-disjoint cell burned ~30s/call ($0.1457) despite precision=recall=1.0 — the deliberative router paid a full LLM turn for a structurally obvious answer. Fix: score each peer by longest matching intent phrase length (case-insensitive substring). Fast-path predicate: leader score > 0 AND (leader − runner-up) ≥ margin (default = leader, so runner-up must be 0 — perfectly disjoint only). Falls through to deliberation on any ambiguity. Tag route_via: 'fastpath' | 'deliberation' in every run/metrics record.
routing-disjoint-fastpath-skip-deliberat.value:
Apply when adding a routing pre-pass before any LLM deliberation step:

(1) SCORING — scorePeers(task, peers): for each peer, find the longest intent phrase (from peer's declared intents) that is a case-insensitive substring of the task. Score = phrase.length, 0 if none match. Rank peers by score descending. Reuses the same includes() test as routeByRules — no new algorithm.

(2) PREDICATE — fastPathPeer(task, peers, margin): leader score > 0 AND (leader.score − runnerUp.score) ≥ margin. Default: margin = leader.score (runner-up must be 0 — perfectly disjoint). Conservative: fires only when structurally unambiguous. Configurable via AGENT_MESH_ROUTE_FASTPATH_MARGIN to widen to near-disjoint once the default is validated.

(3) INTEGRATION — in orchestrator.js, call fastPathPeer before spawning claude -p for deliberation. If it returns a target, construct the route decision immediately and skip the LLM turn. Tag route_via: 'fastpath' in metrics. Otherwise fall through unchanged and tag route_via: 'deliberation'.

(4) TELEMETRY — route_via must be recorded in the run/metrics record for every routing decision so the perf benchmark can attribute wins and detect drift (a confusable cell going fastpath = regression).

(5) REQUIRED TESTS — (a) disjoint fires: one clear match → fastpath selects that peer; (b) confusable does not fire: close scores → deliberation; (c) margin boundary: exact-at-margin semantics locked; (d) equivalence: fastpath selects the same peer deliberation would; (e) downstream identity: fastpath delegation produces same metrics shape as deliberative; (f) disable flag: AGENT_MESH_ROUTE_FASTPATH_DISABLED forces deliberation; (g) perf assertion: 3x-disjoint latency/cost drop, precision/recall unchanged.

(6) OUT OF SCOPE — no new model call for confidence scoring (defeats the purpose); no changes to the deliberative router itself; no multi-peer fan-out interaction; no dynamic threshold learning.

Provenance: PR #414 / spec Issue #413 (2026-06-22).

provenance.source: PR #414: spec: Add a fast-path for unambiguous (disjoint) routing — 3x-disjoint burns ~30s for a perfect route (#413)

perf-baseline-compare-before-update.l0: Baseline drift detection: compare against old baseline before rolling forward; cold-start → info, never warning.
perf-baseline-compare-before-update.l1: PR #416 fixes 9 false-positive perf-cell warnings (baseline: null on first run). Two invariants: (1) Cold-start gate — no prior baseline → emit info, establish baseline, no warning. (2) Compare-then-update — evaluate current run against old baseline THEN roll forward; a regressing run cannot self-mask. (3) Direction-aware: latency/cost increase adverse; quality decrease adverse; improvements never flag.

provenance.source: PR #416: spec: Persist a rolling perf baseline so mesh-improvement-report can actually detect drift (#412)

infra-classify-http-529-overloaded.l0: HTTP 529 / 'api overloaded' in CI log → add to INFRA_RE; else misclassified as !ranAnyTest not as infraError.
infra-classify-http-529-overloaded.l1: PR #419 (2026-06-22): INFRA_RE in src/dev-mesh/classify.js missed 'HTTP 529' and 'api overloaded'. When Claude API returns 529 (capacity overload), CI logs emit exactly these strings but neither matched INFRA_RE, so infraError stayed false. Classification fell through to the !ranAnyTest branch — technically the right label but via wrong code path, and without infraError=true the re-kick heuristic cannot trigger. Pattern: any new Claude API error string that appears verbatim in CI logs must have a corresponding INFRA_RE literal.
infra-classify-http-529-overloaded.value:
When the Claude API returns HTTP 529 (overloaded), CI logs contain exactly the strings 'HTTP 529' and/or 'Claude API overloaded' (which includes 'api overloaded' case-insensitively). These MUST be present as literals in INFRA_RE inside src/dev-mesh/classify.js so that `infraError` is set true.

Without this, the classification logic falls through to the `!ranAnyTest` branch — a label that is arguably still correct but routes incorrectly, and critically: the re-kick-on-infra heuristic never fires because infraError stays false.

Checklist when the Claude API introduces a new error response:
1. Find the exact string that appears in CI logs for that error.
2. Add it as a literal (or minimal regex) to INFRA_RE in src/dev-mesh/classify.js.
3. Add a regression test using that exact log line, asserting infraError=true.

Provenance: PR #419 (2026-06-22), closes #418. The two strings added were 'HTTP 529' and 'api overloaded'.

provenance.source: PR #419: fix(classify): add HTTP 529 / api-overloaded patterns to INFRA_RE (closes #418)

ci-prompt-forbid-unqualified-bash.l0: CI agent prompts: HARD RULE must forbid /fewer-permission-prompts + unqualified Bash on ephemeral runners.
ci-prompt-forbid-unqualified-bash.l1: On ephemeral CI runners, skills that shell out to read local state (e.g. /fewer-permission-prompts reads transcripts/settings via unqualified Bash) produce 8–12 permission denials per run when only Bash(gh:*)/Bash(git:*) is granted, and write a .claude/settings.json the runner discards. Accumulated denials fail the agent-postrun health gate (RED). Root: model reaches for the skill unprompted to reduce noise. Fix: HARD RULE in the prompt explicitly forbidding the skill and unqualified Bash, plus a YAML comment explaining why. Enforcement is prompt-level (soft). PR #422.
ci-prompt-forbid-unqualified-bash.value:
For any CI workflow agent (intake, review, backlog, etc.) operating with restricted Bash grants (Bash(gh:*) + Bash(git:*)): add a named HARD RULE to the prompt explicitly prohibiting (1) the /fewer-permission-prompts skill and (2) unqualified Bash invocations.

Include a YAML comment above the allowedTools line explaining why — e.g. 'the skill shells out to read settings/transcript files (Bash beyond the gh/git grants) and writes an allowlist into .claude/settings.json that the ephemeral CI runner discards — so it only burns turns and accrues denials until the gate fails.'

Pattern: comment sits above the claude_args: block; HARD RULE sits in the prompt: block near the tool-grant section.

This is soft enforcement (prompt-level) — the model can still disobey. If denials recur after adding the HARD RULE, escalate to removing the skill from the allowed tool surface entirely (tool-grant removal = hard guarantee).

Generalizes: any skill that writes local filesystem state on an ephemeral runner wastes turns and accrues denials — enumerate and forbid by name in the prompt.

Provenance: PR #422 (2026-06-22), fixing Issue #421 (8–12 denials per dev-mesh-intake run).

provenance.source: PR #422: bug: dev-mesh-intake failing with 8–12 Bash permission denials on every scheduled run

actions-self-cancel-relabel.l0: cancel-in-progress:true + agent relabels/edits own issue = self-cancel; fix: false + drop edited trigger.
actions-self-cancel-relabel.l1: dev-mesh-intake cancelled 34-45% of runs: Analyst relabeled the issue mid-work, firing another labeled event on the same concurrency group (dev-mesh-intake-${{ issue.number }}, cancel-in-progress:true) → killed the in-flight run. Also had issues:edited trigger; Analyst edits body as it works. Fix: cancel-in-progress:false (group still serializes — GitHub keeps ONE pending run max per group; bounded waste) + remove issues:edited. Evidence: cancelled pairs share identical timestamp. PR #428.
actions-self-cancel-relabel.value:
Concurrency self-cancel pattern: when a workflow's concurrency group is stable per-object (e.g. dev-mesh-intake-${{ issue.number }}) with cancel-in-progress:true, any agent side effect that re-fires the SAME trigger event mid-execution kills the in-flight run.

Common sources:
- relabeling the issue → labeled event → same group → cancel
- editing the issue body → edited event → same group → cancel
- commenting on the issue → issues: commented → same group → cancel

Fix pattern (two changes):
1. cancel-in-progress: false — serializes the burst WITHOUT killing live work. GitHub queues exactly ONE pending run per group, so the maximum redundant waste is one cold start, not unbounded.
2. Audit the workflow's own side effects vs its trigger list — remove any event the agent fires as a byproduct:
  - Drop issues: edited if the agent ever edits bodies.
  - Drop labeled if the agent relabels.
  - Drop issues: commented if the agent comments.

Diagnosis: look for cancelled runs that share identical timestamps and the same issue number.

Test coverage: assert cancel-in-progress: false in workflow lint for any intake-style workflow where the agent modifies the trigger object. Provenance: PR #428 (2026-06-23), ~18 wasted/cancelled runs per 60 recovered.

provenance.source: PR #428 (2026-06-23): fix(ci): stop dev-mesh-intake self-cancelling its own runs (~34-45% wasted + live API spend)

lifecycle-strip-all-on-terminal-close.l0: On terminal close, strip ALL lifecycle labels (not just trigger subset) — clears drift like spec:in-review+done.
lifecycle-strip-all-on-terminal-close.l1: PR #435 P7: lifecycle labels accumulate on issues because state transitions add without removing predecessors (e.g. spec:in-review + pr:in-review + done). A reconciler stripping only its trigger set (RECONCILE_STATES) leaves earlier labels intact. Fix: strip LIFECYCLE_STATES (the full ordered group) — removes every lifecycle label present, not just triggering ones. Latent footgun: RECONCILE_STATES ⊆ LIFECYCLE_STATES must hold; if a new trigger label falls outside the strip set, done stamps without stripping the trigger. Encode as an assertion or derive one set from the other.
lifecycle-strip-all-on-terminal-close.value:
Apply when a post-merge reconciler (or any terminal-state handler) adds a `done` label and closes an issue.

(1) Strip the FULL lifecycle group, not just the trigger subset:
  Define an ordered LIFECYCLE_STATES array covering all mutually-exclusive lifecycle labels:
    const LIFECYCLE_STATES = ['idea','discussing','spec:draft','spec:in-review','approved','in-progress','pr:in-review'];
  At reconcile time:
    removeLabels: LIFECYCLE_STATES.filter(l => issue.labels.includes(l))
  This collapses any accumulated combination (spec:in-review+in-progress+done) to a single clean `done`.

(2) Terminal/rejected labels are NOT part of the lifecycle group:
  `blocked` and `rejected` are terminal, not linear lifecycle steps — leave them untouched by the strip-all. Do not add them to LIFECYCLE_STATES.

(3) RECONCILE_STATES ⊆ LIFECYCLE_STATES is a load-bearing silent invariant:
  The strip-all works because every trigger label is in the strip set. If RECONCILE_STATES grows to include a label absent from LIFECYCLE_STATES, the reconciler stamps `done` without removing that trigger — re-introducing the exact drift it was meant to prevent.
  Encode at module load:
    for (const s of RECONCILE_STATES) {
      if (!LIFECYCLE_STATES.includes(s)) throw new Error(`${s} not in LIFECYCLE_STATES`);
    }
  Or derive one from the other to eliminate the hand-maintenance gap:
    const RECONCILE_STATES = LIFECYCLE_STATES.filter(l => IN_FLIGHT_SET.has(l) || l === 'approved');

(4) Ordering for deterministic tests: list removeLabels in LIFECYCLE_STATES order so gh command sequences are stable.

(5) Related: apply removeLabels + addLabel:done BEFORE gh issue close (see reconcile-approved-label-before-close) so the terminal-sweep backstop can catch a failed close.

Provenance: PR #435 (2026-06-23), Issue #430 P7 (label-state drift).

provenance.source: PR #435 (2026-06-23): Pipeline audit (2026-06-23) — P7 lifecycle label-state drift fix

external-client-not-mesh-agent.l0: Mesh UI front-doors (voice/phone/IDE) are external clients via ask-only console broker — never registered mesh agents.
external-client-not-mesh-agent.l1: PR #429 (voice console) is the second external front-door after /m Mobile Concierge. UI clients that only discuss and ask should NOT be registered peers: pollutes the roster, adds routing overhead, complicates ask-only enforcement. Pattern: an external server with an ask_mesh_agent tool calling the console broker A2A proxy + read-only tools (gh/REST). The AI brain lives outside the mesh — any model (Gemini, OpenAI) works. Console broker enforces ask-only so consulted agents answer but never do work.
external-client-not-mesh-agent.value: When adding a new UI front-door to the mesh (voice assistant, phone chat, IDE plugin, CLI wrapper): (1) Do NOT register it as a peer in registry.json — it is a client, not a collaborator. (2) Give it an ask_mesh_agent tool that POSTs to the console broker A2A endpoint — the broker enforces ask-only on entry. (3) Give it direct read tools (gh CLI, REST, file reads) for mesh state — these bypass the mesh for speed. (4) Its AI brain is independent of the mesh: any model/provider works, no AGENT_MESH_* env needed. (5) For TTS/STT in voice frontends: keep a persistent warm TTS worker (e.g. Kokoro) to avoid cold-start latency per reply; provide a hosted TTS fallback (e.g. Gemini TTS) for reliability. (6) Gemini-specific gotcha: force functionCallingConfig: {mode: "ANY"} — Gemini 2.5-flash sometimes emits function calls as plain text (leaked tool call) which breaks parsing; forcing the mode prevents it. Provenance: PR #429 (2026-06-23).

provenance.source: PR #429: feat(voice-demo): phone voice console — Gemini agent ↔ mesh (STT/TTS + ask-only agent proxy)

postrun-gate-inert-skill-carveout.l0: Postrun health-gate: use inert-skill allowlist to filter false-positive denials; unattributable denial always counts.
postrun-gate-inert-skill-carveout.l1: PR #422's HARD RULE forbids /fewer-permission-prompts on CI but is soft. PR #438's structural layer: each denial record carries skill provenance; the gate classifies against an explicit inert-skill allowlist (`AGENT_MESH_HEALTH_INERT_SKILLS`; default: `fewer-permission-prompts`). Denials from inert skills (effects discarded on ephemeral runner) are excluded from the unhealthy-block tally. Fail-safe: unattributable denial always counts. Core seam: pure classifier `(denial, allowlist) → {counts, reason}` — table-testable. PR #438 / #432 (2026-06-23).
postrun-gate-inert-skill-carveout.value:
Defense-in-depth pattern for CI agents that call inert skills despite HARD RULE prompts.

**Layer 1 (soft — prompt):** Already in `ci-prompt-forbid-unqualified-bash`. A named HARD RULE in the agent prompt forbids the skill. First line of defense; model can still disobey.

**Layer 2 (structural — postrun gate):** Added by PR #438:
1. Add provenance to each denial record: identifies its originating skill.
2. Pure denial classifier: `(denial, inertSkillAllowlist) → { counts: bool, reason }`
   - Inert skill source → `counts: false` (excluded from tally, logged for observability)
   - Any other source → `counts: true`
   - No provenance → `counts: true` (fail-safe: never silently ignore)
3. Gate compares COUNTED denials (not total) against the unhealthy-block threshold.
4. Config: `AGENT_MESH_HEALTH_INERT_SKILLS` (default: `fewer-permission-prompts`); explicit and minimal — only skills provably inert on the target environment.
5. Gate output distinguishes ignored-inert vs. counted denials (observability preserved).

**What makes a skill inert:** its ONLY observable effect on ephemeral runners is discarded (e.g., writing `.claude/settings.json` the runner throws away). Do NOT add conditionally-inert skills — only skills provably inert regardless of input.

**Testing the pure classifier:**
- All denials from inert skill → gate passes (0 counted)
- Mixed: inert + genuine → only genuine count; gate reds if counted ≥ threshold
- Genuine only → gate reds as before (carve-out did not weaken the gate)
- No provenance → always counts (fail-safe)
- Config-driven: adding/removing from allowlist changes behavior without code change
- Gate output names skipped skills (observability)

**Related:** `ci-prompt-forbid-unqualified-bash` (PR #422). Provenance: PR #438 (2026-06-23), closes #432.

provenance.source: PR #438 (2026-06-23): spec: postrun health-gate: don't fail runs on inert fewer-permission-prompts denials (#432)

autofiler-coalesce-gate-before-create.l0: Auto-filer: coalesce findings by key, gate at noise band, label-dedup update-or-file — never create then triage.
autofiler-coalesce-gate-before-create.l1: PR #437 spec: ~40% of perf issues rejected as noise or near-dups after filing; same-broken-intake filed per-symptom each run. Root causes: (1) noiseBandPct gated triage only, not creation; (2) each raw finding became a separate issue before grouping. Fix: coalesce by natural key (metric or workflow) before the planner; apply noiseBandPct as a creation gate; query existing open perf:<metric> / intake:<workflow> label to update vs. file; close on recovery.
autofiler-coalesce-gate-before-create.value:
Apply whenever an automated scanner files issues into a human-reviewed backlog.

Part A — perf regressions (same scan, N cells, same metric):
1. COALESCE: group per-cell findings by metric before the planner — one finding per (scan, metric) carrying all per-cell rows.
2. NOISE GATE: drop findings inside noiseBandPct and cold-start/null-baseline establishments at coalesce time — before reaching gh issue create.
3. LABEL-DEDUP: query open perf:<metric> issue; found → update (refresh rows); not found → file one.
4. RECOVER: metric returns within band → close the open issue.

Part B — intake health (same workflow, repeated symptoms/runs):
1. COALESCE: all same-window symptoms for a workflow → one intake:<workflow> issue accumulating them.
2. ROLLING WINDOW: AGENT_MESH_INTAKE_DEDUP_WINDOW_MS (default 24h); an issue aged out counts as absent.
3. UPDATE vs FILE: open issue within window → update; none → file once.
4. RECOVER: intake healthy → close.

Cross-cutting: label-based marker lookup (never read issue bodies); pure-planner/gh-applier pattern; labels ensured before the loop (see label-preflight-before-issue-create). Provenance: PR #437 (2026-06-23) spec, closing #433.

provenance.source: PR #437: spec: auto-filed issue dedup + noise-band gating (perf-regression + intake-health) (#433)

streaming-pipeline-latency-reduction.l0: Streaming pipeline: cut perceived latency by pipelining generation→segment→per-chunk synthesis→ordered queue.
streaming-pipeline-latency-reduction.l1: PR #443: Stage A voice was sequential (full reply → full TTS → play; ~4.5–6 s). Stage B pipelines it: stream generator → sentence segmenter → TTS per sentence → ordered client audio queue (plays as chunks arrive). Target: ≤3 s to first audio. Critical: (1) segment timeout prevents stalls on tool-call-heavy turns; (2) ordering enforced by queue index, not arrival time — TTS chunk N+1 may finish before N; (3) fallback to single-shot+batch on any stream/TTS error; (4) time-to-first-chunk instrumentation required from day 1.
streaming-pipeline-latency-reduction.value:
Apply when a sequential generate→synthesize→deliver pipeline is the perceived-latency bottleneck:

1. STREAM the generator so output begins arriving immediately (streamGenerateContent, SSE, streaming API).
2. SEGMENT the stream into delivery units (sentences, frames, tiles) — flush on a short timeout (~300ms) so tool-call-heavy turns or boundary-free text never stall the segmenter.
3. SYNTHESIZE each unit immediately on arrival (TTS, encode, render) while later units are still generating.
4. DELIVER in order via a client queue indexed by unit position, NOT by arrival time — chunk N+1 may synthesize faster than N; hold it until N is delivered.
5. BEGIN playback/display on the first unit — do not wait for the full batch.
6. INSTRUMENT time-to-first-unit per turn from day 1. Without a recorded metric, improvement is unverifiable.
7. FALLBACK: any stream or synthesis error → degrade to single-shot (full output then batch synthesis); the turn still completes.

Gotchas:
- Segment timeout is mandatory: a boundary-free turn (all tool calls, then a one-word answer) must eventually flush or the user waits for the whole turn.
- Queue ordering by index prevents audio/display ordering from depending on per-chunk timing variance.
- Test each fallback path with forced injection of stream errors and synthesis errors — not just the happy path.
- Inline tool calls that modify state (e.g. file_mesh_task within a streamed turn) must still fire correctly; test explicitly.

Provenance: PR #443 (2026-06-23) — Voice Console Stage B spec, resolving issue #426.

provenance.source: PR #443 (2026-06-23): spec: Voice console — Stage B: streaming low-latency voice (stream reply + chunked sentence TTS) (#426)

attempt-cap-circuit-breaker-durable-stat.l0: Retry loops need durable-state attempt caps — read from issue comments/state file, not ephemeral runner vars.
attempt-cap-circuit-breaker-durable-stat.l1: PR #439 (spec for #431): issue #405 re-authored 12× over ~15h because spec-push retry had no attempt cap. Root cause: counter lived only in ephemeral runner state, reset on each re-trigger. Fix: read prior 'Spec ready attempt N' comment markers (or a state file) to count attempts across runner instances. After N=3 failed pushes: add `needs-human` label, leave ONE deduped escalation comment (check for marker before posting), STOP. Reset counter on green push.
attempt-cap-circuit-breaker-durable-stat.value:
Apply whenever a pipeline agent can fail and be re-triggered (spec-push, code-gen, auto-PR).

DURABLE STATE COUNTER: Never use ephemeral runner env/vars for attempt counts — the count resets each re-trigger. Instead:
  Option A: scan issue comments for a per-attempt marker string (e.g. 'Spec ready attempt N') and count them.
  Option B: use a committed or external state file keyed to the issue/workflow.

CIRCUIT-BREAK LOGIC (after N=3 failures):
  1. Add `needs-human` label to the source issue.
  2. Post ONE deduped escalation comment: check for an existing sentinel-marker comment before posting — re-running at cap must NOT add a second comment.
  3. STOP — exit without re-triggering.

COUNTER RESET: On an observed GREEN outcome (e.g. successful push), reset the counter. A later failure starts fresh at 1.

SCOPE: The circuit breaker stops the loop; it does NOT diagnose the root cause. `needs-human` is the hand-off — a human investigates why N pushes failed.

RELATED: pair with upfront credential preflight (see `label-preflight-before-issue-create`) to fail fast before the loop starts, and bounded 529 retry (see `claude-api-529-retry-resilience`) so transient errors don't burn attempt budget. Provenance: PR #439 (2026-06-23), issue #431.

provenance.source: PR #439 (2026-06-23): spec: intake runtime reliability: circuit breaker + DEV_MESH_PAT preflight + 529 retry (#431)

health-gate-denial-filter-envelope-form.l0: Denial filter: match the envelope form production emits — array vs count path mismatch = silent dead code.
health-gate-denial-filter-envelope-form.l1: PR #448 filtered `fewer-permission-prompts` denials from the health gate, targeting the `permission_denials` ARRAY form. But real runs emit `permission_denials_count` (a NUMBER) — a different path. Tests against the array form pass while the fix is dead code in production. Also: JSON.stringify(.includes('.claude')) over-matches any denial record with a `.claude` path anywhere, silently disabling the gate. Fix: grep a real failing envelope first to confirm the form, then filter specific fields (tool_name, tool_input.*), not whole-object substrings.
health-gate-denial-filter-envelope-form.value:
Two-layer trap from PR #448 (2026-06-23): filtering health-gate signals requires knowing which envelope variant production actually emits.
