1. ENVELOPE FORM MISMATCH: `classifyRunHealth` filters the `permission_denials` ARRAY (show_full_output form). Production `claude-execution-output.json` (read by assert-run-healthy.mjs) emits `permission_denials_count` (a NUMBER). Two different code paths — array-form tests pass green while the count path is untouched. Action: before adding a denial filter, open a real failing run's output file and confirm which key is actually populated.

2. WHOLE-OBJECT SUBSTRING MATCH: `isInertDenial` used `JSON.stringify(denial).toLowerCase().includes('.claude')`. This matches `.claude` anywhere in the record — including cwd, transcript_path, session_path. A genuine denial whose record includes any `.claude` context path is silently laundered. Always filter specific fields: `tool_input.command`, `tool_input.file_path`, `tool_name === 'Skill'`.

3. TEST COVERAGE BLINDSPOT: fixtures for denial filtering that only supply `{tool_name, tool_input}` leave field-level over-matching latent. Include the full real denial record shape (with context fields) to surface it.

Pattern for any new denial/signal exception:
- Grep a real failing artifact first to confirm the exact key path
- Filter specific named fields, never whole-object serialization
- Write tests with full real record shapes, not minimal stubs

provenance.source: PR #448: postrun health-gate: don't fail runs on inert fewer-permission-prompts denials (#432)

implicit-set-subset-assert-or-derive.l0: Silent A⊆B set dependency: derive one from the other or assert at module load — never hand-maintained comments.
implicit-set-subset-assert-or-derive.l1: PR #435 fixed label drift by stripping LIFECYCLE_STATES on terminal close, but noted a footgun: RECONCILE_STATES ⊆ LIFECYCLE_STATES is hand-maintained. Adding a trigger label to RECONCILE_STATES without updating LIFECYCLE_STATES causes the reconciler to stamp `done` without stripping the trigger — re-introducing drift. PR #445 spec (Issue #442) fixes this: (A) module-load assertion — throw if any entry in RECONCILE_STATES is absent from LIFECYCLE_STATES; (B) derive RECONCILE_STATES from LIFECYCLE_STATES by filter — subset by construction. Hermetic test gates CI.

provenance.source: PR #445 (2026-06-23): spec: fix(dev-society): assert RECONCILE_STATES ⊆ LIFECYCLE_STATES at module load (#442)

per-metric-noise-band-llm-variance.l0: LLM-dominated metrics need per-metric noise bands — a global band false-positives on real-model latency jitter.
per-metric-noise-band-llm-variance.l1: Wall-clock `latency_ms` (and `cost_usd`) are dominated by real-model call time whose run-to-run jitter routinely exceeds a 10% global band — so normal LLM noise looks like a code regression. Add `noiseBandPct` to METRICS entries for high-variance metrics. `isRegression` resolves it with `METRICS[name]?.noiseBandPct ?? bandPct` — `??` (not `||`) is load-bearing: `||` would treat a zero band as absent and fall back to the global default.
per-metric-noise-band-llm-variance.value:
In the metric registry (e.g. `src/mesh-improvement/metrics.js`), add `noiseBandPct: N` to each METRICS entry dominated by external non-code factors (LLM latency, API cost). `isRegression` applies it: `const band = METRICS[name]?.noiseBandPct ?? bandPct`. The `??` is critical — `||` treats a zero band as falsy and falls back, breaking intentional tight bands.

Derive the band value from `computeStats`/cv on historical runs; `computeStats` already exists in metrics.js for this purpose. A hand-picked constant (e.g. 30) risks two failure modes: too tight = false positives persist; too wide = genuine code regressions masked. The cv-derived value anchors the band to observed variance.

Reviewer notes from PR #461: (1) `Math.max(bandPct, noiseBandPct)` would better express 'at least this wide' if the intent is a floor — plain `??` lets a low noiseBandPct override a wider global band (opposite direction); choose semantics deliberately. (2) `cost_usd` has the same LLM-dominated variance class and likely needs the same treatment. PR #461 (2026-06-23) introduced `latency_ms: { noiseBandPct: 30 }` (≈2× the observed 15.9% swing), closing false-positive issue #459.

provenance.source: PR #461 (2026-06-23): [mesh-scan] perf-regression: latency_ms regressed (-15.9%) (#459)

per-peer-model-tier-spawn-flag.l0: Per-peer model tier: `model` in registry.json peer entry → `--model` at spawn; never from tool args.
per-peer-model-tier-spawn-flag.l1: Set `model: "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"` on a registry.json peer entry to run that peer on a cheaper tier. The registry is the sole authoritative source — a delegating model's tool-arg `model` field has no effect (anti-spoof invariant). Absent `model` → binary default unchanged. Bad model ID → `status:error` result, not exception. BAMAS/AgentDropout research: 30–97% cost savings via tiering (arXiv 2511.21572, 2503.18891).
per-peer-model-tier-spawn-flag.value: Threading path: `normalizePeer` preserves the field (undefined when absent) → `peer-bridge.js` passes it to `delegateTask` options → `delegate.js` threads to `buildClaudeInvocation` → appends `--model <id>` to `claude -p` argv iff set. Regression lock: no `model` field → argv byte-identical to today; present → flag threaded end-to-end across all four layers. Design scope: v1 is static/operator-configured per peer (no dynamic tier selection, no per-task override, no model-ID validation — bad IDs degrade to `status:error`). Spec: 2026-06-23 design doc (PR #467 / Issue #457).

provenance.source: PR #467 (2026-06-23): spec: idea: per-peer model-tier selection — run Haiku workers, Opus orchestrator (#457)

eval-corpus-anti-gaming-design.l0: L5 eval corpus: keywords = real verifiable framework terms, non-reconstructable from task alone; min_hits < total.
eval-corpus-anti-gaming-design.l1: PR #469 / Issue #454: mesh-bench.json shipped empty → vacuous L5 scorecard every run. Corpus rules: (1) keywords = real framework terms (component names, env vars, exit codes) verifiable in codebase — not extractable from task text; (2) min_keyword_hits < len(expected_keywords) for partial-correct scoring; (3) ≥5 concern areas × ≥2 tasks each; (4) cross-concern tasks validate multi-peer advantage. Tests: schema+threshold sanity, ≥10 tasks load, anti-gaming review (answer not reconstructable from task+keywords).
eval-corpus-anti-gaming-design.value:
Apply when populating any eval task corpus for a harness that uses keyword-hit scoring.

(1) Keywords must be REAL and VERIFIABLE: choose actual component names, env var names, function signatures, or exit-code semantics from the codebase. A keyword that appears verbatim in the task description is gameable — a model can extract it without understanding. Verify each keyword appears in the actual codebase or spec.

(2) min_keyword_hits < len(expected_keywords): require N out of M keywords where N < M. A threshold of N=M is brittle — one synonym or phrasing variation fails the task. N < M allows correct-but-differently-worded answers to score.

(3) Anti-gaming review (manual gate per task): confirm the correct answer is NOT reconstructable from task text + expected_keywords alone. A reviewer seeing only those two fields should not be able to write a passing answer without understanding the framework.

(4) Coverage breadth: ensure all major system areas have ≥2 tasks each (e.g. routing, security, board, health, protocol). Single-area concentration inflates apparent coverage while missing large surfaces.

(5) Cross-concern tasks: include tasks spanning two system areas. These validate whether multi-peer meshes outperform single agents on compound questions — the core L5 hypothesis.

(6) End-to-end smoke: after populating, run the eval script and confirm it produces a non-vacuous scorecard (>0 tasks scored, >0 keywords evaluated). An empty corpus produces silently vacuous results with no error.

Provenance: PR #469 spec (2026-06-23), Issue #454.

provenance.source: PR #469: spec: idea: populate mesh-bench task corpus — L5 eval harness runs with empty task list (#454)

multi-component-path-eval-coverage.l0: New multi-component paths need an L2 eval scenario at merge — uncovered gaps are invisible until live test.
multi-component-path-eval-coverage.l1: Orchestrator-board pipeline (spec 2026-06-22) wired 5 components: `create_task_for_peer → board/store.js → board/task-state.js → board/identity.js → peer-bridge.js` — but shipped with no L2 eval scenario. Issue #455 (Analyst-detected) surfaced the gap; PR #468 specifies scenario 13: two-turn, deterministic (no daemon scheduling), real store (no mocks), includes identity enforcement as required negative probe (board security invariant: only `to` agent may advance). Out-of-scope: fan-out, full `assigned→done` walk, production code changes.
multi-component-path-eval-coverage.value:
When a feature wires ≥3 framework components end-to-end, treat an L2 eval scenario as a required deliverable alongside the spec — not a follow-up. Pattern for designing the scenario:

(1) Two-turn deterministic: avoid daemon scheduling dependencies. Drive the pipeline explicitly (turn 1: create, turn 2: advance). Scheduler behavior is a separate concern.

(2) Real store, no mocks: if the framework uses a file-based store, the scenario reads/writes it. A mock substitution would be a defect — the eval must catch persistence failures, not paper over them.

(3) Identity enforcement is a required negative probe: if the path has an identity invariant (e.g. 'only the `to` agent may advance'), include a wrong-identity case with `refusedWith`. This is a security property per the board invariant; the L2 tier must gate it.

(4) Name out-of-scope explicitly: state which behaviors the scenario does NOT cover (daemon scheduling, full state-machine walk, production code changes, fan-out reasoning). This prevents scope creep and makes the scenario's coverage boundary clear.

(5) Analyst is the natural gap detector: the `analyst-daily-review` daemon reads MIR + daily-report and is well-positioned to identify eval coverage gaps. File the gap as a `generated:analyst` `idea` issue so it enters the backlog and is trackable.

Provenance: PR #468 / Issue #455 (2026-06-23).

provenance.source: PR #468 (2026-06-23): spec: behavior eval scenario for orchestrator-board pipeline (#455)

reviewer-concern-follow-on-pr.l0: Reviewer-deferred concerns: file immediately + close in a focused follow-on PR — never defer-and-forget.
reviewer-concern-follow-on-pr.l1: PR #461 reviewer flagged `cost_usd` needed the same `??`→`Math.max` fix as `latency_ms` but was scoped out. Filed as issue #462; completed by PR #475 (2 files: `isRegression` operator fix + regression test 'wider global band never narrowed'). The `Math.max(perMetricBand, globalBand)` invariant now guards all high-variance metrics proactively before a live bug. Both semantic fix and regression test deferred and completed together.
reviewer-concern-follow-on-pr.value:
Pattern: reviewer-deferred concern → tracked issue → focused follow-on PR.

(1) When a reviewer says 'this is correct today but will break if X changes', immediately file an issue with the concern, the fix, and the invariant. Don't rely on PR comments — they decay.

(2) The follow-on PR should be minimal: only the flagged concern. PR #475 changed one operator and added one test. 2 files, ~10 lines of diff.

(3) Include a regression test that names the invariant, not the implementation: 'wider global band is never narrowed by a smaller per-metric override'. This gates future regressions regardless of how the code evolves.

(4) Thread the audit trail in the PR description: cite the original PR's reviewer comment + the filed issue. This makes the fix traceable without reading comments.

The Math.max vs ?? invariant (the specific case):
- `Math.max(perMetricBand, globalBand)`: widen-only — per-metric band acts as a floor, never a ceiling.
- `perMetricBand ?? globalBand`: replace-semantics — if global later exceeds override, per-metric becomes MORE sensitive than global, inverting 'high-variance = wider tolerance'.
- Today global=10, overrides=20 — no live bug when deferred. Semantics were wrong by construction; fix when footprint is small.

Apply whenever a reviewer flags: 'correct for current values but breaks if Y changes'. That is a broken invariant awaiting the right trigger — encode it in code + test now.

Provenance: PR #475 (2026-06-23) closes issue #462, filed from PR #461 review. See also [[regression-band-override-semantics]] for the Math.max semantics.

provenance.source: PR #475 (2026-06-23): fix: use Math.max for isRegression band so wider global band is never narrowed

do-mode-branch-isolation.l0: AGENT_MESH_DO_BRANCH=1: do-mode writes land on agentmesh/<runId>; dirty→refused, non-git skip, error-path cleanup.
do-mode-branch-isolation.l1: PR #474: AGENT_MESH_DO_BRANCH=1 puts do-mode writes on a scratch branch `agentmesh/<runId>` for human review before merge. Dirty working tree → status:refused/reason:dirty_worktree (clean precondition must hold). Non-git folders skip silently. On error or timeout the scratch branch is cleaned up (framework restores original branch, deletes scratch) — 'failure is data' still holds. Five hermetic tests: success, dirty-tree refusal, ask-mode no-op, non-git skip, error-path cleanup.
do-mode-branch-isolation.value:
Apply when adding an opt-in human-review gate for do-mode writes via a scratch branch:

(1) Opt-in flag: AGENT_MESH_DO_BRANCH=1 activates branch isolation. Default OFF — integrations without the flag are unaffected. Add to RESERVED_BRIDGE_ENV so peer.env cannot set it (anti-spoof invariant).

(2) Clean precondition: if the working tree is dirty, return {status:'refused', reason:'dirty_worktree'} before creating any branch. A mid-flight tree cannot safely host do-mode output.

(3) Branch naming: agentmesh/<runId> — framework-generated, collision-resistant. Never accept a branch name from tool args (same anti-spoof principle as recursion call-path).

(4) Non-git folder: skip silently. No error, no isolation — same behavior as without the flag.

(5) Error-path cleanup: on any non-done outcome (error, timeout), restore the original branch and delete the scratch branch. 'Failure is data' still holds — a status:timeout or status:error result is returned regardless; the cleanup prevents stale agentmesh/* branches from accumulating.

(6) Ask-mode no-op: the flag is ignored when mode='ask'. Branch isolation is only meaningful for do-mode writes.

(7) Test coverage (5 required cases): success (branch created, committed, original restored); dirty-tree refusal (refused before branching); ask-mode no-op (no branch, same output as no-flag); non-git skip (no error, no branch); error-path cleanup (branch deleted after error/timeout).

Human-review flow: after a successful run the scratch branch holds the do-mode changes; human inspects and merges at their discretion. The framework never auto-merges the scratch branch.

Provenance: PR #474 (2026-06-23), Issue #458.

provenance.source: PR #474: feat: branch-isolated do mode (AGENT_MESH_DO_BRANCH=1)

voice-proxy-via-dashboard-not-inprocess.l0: Voice/tool ask_mesh_agent: proxy via deployed dashboard /api/agent/<name>/message — in-process broker is invisible.
voice-proxy-via-dashboard-not-inprocess.l1: PR #476 follow-up to #429: the in-process broker in voice-demo spawned agents against the working-copy mesh, making consults invisible to the dashboard activity feed and routing to the wrong mesh. Fix: POST to the deployed dashboard's console API (/api/agent/<name>/message, mode:'ask'). Requires VOICE_DASHBOARD_URL / VOICE_DASHBOARD_TOKEN[_FILE] / VOICE_MESH_DIR (agent allowlist from deploy mesh.json). Dashboard must start with --enable-chat (ask-only, still token-gated).
voice-proxy-via-dashboard-not-inprocess.value:
Apply when building any external tool (voice assistant, CLI, IDE extension, phone chat) that calls mesh agents via ask_mesh_agent or equivalent:

(1) WRONG: in-process broker
Spawning an in-process A2A broker within the tool's own process connects to whichever mesh the tool's working-copy points at — typically the dev checkout, not the production deployment. Consults are invisible to the dashboard (no activity log entry, no session trace), so the owner cannot observe them or distinguish tool traffic from live-user sessions.

(2) RIGHT: POST to deployed dashboard's console API
Routing via `POST /api/agent/<name>/message` (the dashboard's ask-only console, the same endpoint the browser UI uses) brokers the spawn through the DEPLOYED mesh the dashboard fronts. Result: (a) the consult appears as live dashboard activity (visible to owner); (b) the responding agent is the production peer, not the dev-checkout instance; (c) ask-only is enforced at the broker layer — the consulted agent cannot do work.

(3) CONFIG
  VOICE_DASHBOARD_URL — URL of the deployed dashboard (e.g. http://localhost:7077 or tailnet URL)
  VOICE_DASHBOARD_TOKEN / VOICE_DASHBOARD_TOKEN_FILE — auth token for the dashboard's token gate
  VOICE_MESH_DIR — path to deploy mesh.json for the allowed-agent roster (fallback: working-copy mesh.json)

(4) DASHBOARD REQUIREMENT
The dashboard MUST start with --enable-chat. Without it, /api/agent/<name>/message is disabled. This is a one-time launchd plist change; add the flag to the deploy install script so it persists across reinstalls. Verify: POST /api/agent/<name>/message returns 200 (not 404/403) before shipping the tool.

(5) ACTIVITY VISIBILITY
With routing via dashboard, consults appear in the deploy activity-log and the dashboard's live feed. Log line: '[ask] → <agentName> (via dashboard)'. Without it, there is no record.

(6) GENERALIZES
This applies to any process that embeds mesh agent calls — CI scripts, cron jobs, IDE extensions, background workers. Route through the deployed dashboard instead of an in-process broker for the same observability and mesh-targeting reasons.

Related: external-client-not-mesh-agent (PR #429, broader external-client pattern); mobile-tailscale-askonly-concierge (Tailscale-served dashboard setup). Provenance: PR #476 (2026-06-23).

provenance.source: PR #476 (2026-06-23): feat(voice-demo): route ask_mesh_agent via dashboard console (visible activity)

analyst-codebase-demonstrated-vs-claimed.l0: Codebase assess: Demonstrated (code-cited) vs. Claimed (docs-only); adversarially test novelty; report coverage limits.
analyst-codebase-demonstrated-vs-claimed.l1: Analyst codebase-mode deep-research (PR #489/#487): standard web research misses source evidence. Protocol: (1) enumerate load-bearing files, read chunked; (2) tag each claim Demonstrated (code-cited) or Claimed (docs-only); (3) adversarially search to DISPROVE each novelty candidate; (4) report exact coverage (read vs. unread). Confidence-calibrated verdicts only — no bare novelty assertions. Runtime, private repos, JS-rendered targets: marked unobservable, never hallucinated.
analyst-codebase-demonstrated-vs-claimed.value:
When doing any technical assessment of a codebase, documentation corpus, or external library, apply the Demonstrated/Claimed evidence protocol:

1. **Read source, not just docs.** Enumerate load-bearing files (entry points, core modules, tests); read them in full (chunked for large files) rather than stopping at README/landing page. The README may overstate or understate what the code actually does.

2. **Tag every claim:** Demonstrated = supported by a code excerpt with file:line citation. Claimed = stated in documentation only, no code backing. A claim cannot be upgraded from Claimed to Demonstrated without a concrete code reference. If the code contradicts the docs, Demonstrated wins.

3. **Adversarial novelty testing.** For each innovation candidate, fan out web searches *to disprove* — not to confirm. Return a calibrated verdict: `actually-common` (prior art found), `plausible-novel` (no prior art found, but limited search), or `novel-within-scope` (absence of prior art corroborated across multiple searches). Never echo the project's self-assessment as fact.

4. **Honest coverage.** Explicitly report (a) which files were read, (b) which were skipped and why (too large, inaccessible, binary), (c) what categories of evidence are unobservable (runtime performance, private repo internals, dynamic/JS-rendered content). An unobservable claim is labelled as such, never hallucinated.

5. **Non-regression.** Standard web-only research paths (fact-finding, summarisation) are unaffected — codebase mode is a routing specialisation activated by repo/architecture/innovation-assessment requests.

Applies to: Analyst deep-research skill, security reviews, architecture assessments, third-party library evaluations, any mesh task producing a technical judgment from source evidence.

Provenance: PR #489 (2026-06-24), closing issue #487 — Analyst spec for codebase-mode deep-research.

provenance.source: PR #489 (2026-06-24): spec: Analyst codebase-mode deep-research with Demonstrated/Claimed evidence separation

mir-noise-band-reversion.l0: Ghost issue + blocked review merged: PR #477 reverted isRegression Math.max→?? despite blocking verdict.
mir-noise-band-reversion.l1: Issue #462 was already closed by PR #475 (Math.max fix). PR #477 re-implemented it with ?? semantics, regressing the fix. Reviewer gave a blocking verdict correctly flagging semantic regression but described diff direction backwards ('??→Math.max' when code went 'Math.max→??'). PR was merged anyway. regression-band-override-semantics (Math.max) is now expired; current code uses ?? override semantics.
mir-noise-band-reversion.value:
Three-layer failure pattern from PR #477 — each preventable:

(1) Coder claimed a closed issue
Before implementing, verify `gh issue view N --json state` returns OPEN. If CLOSED, check whether the target line was already changed in a recent PR: `git log --oneline -S 'isRegression' -- src/mesh-improvement/metrics.js`. A closed issue means the fix exists; re-implementing it often reverses the existing fix with opposite semantics.

(2) Blocking review merged anyway
The A2A reviewer gave an explicit blocking verdict. The merge happened anyway. A blocking verdict is data, not an enforcement gate — the society has no automated stop mechanism beyond human judgment. When a blocking review is overridden, the Curator must immediately scan the lesson store for any lessons the merged code now contradicts and expire them. Expired entry: [[regression-band-override-semantics]] (Math.max semantics, set valid_to 2026-06-23).

(3) Reviewer described diff direction backwards
The reviewer wrote 'changes from override (??) to wider-of-two (Math.max)' — the actual diff went the other way (Math.max removed, ?? added). Reading '+'/'-' lines directly prevents this: '-' = old code (removed), '+' = new code (added). A misdescribed direction does NOT invalidate the semantic concern — the reviewer was correct that the merged change was a regression; they just named the operators in the wrong order. Never dismiss a blocking review solely because its described direction seems reversed; verify the diff yourself.

(4) Current code state post-PR #477
`isRegression` now uses `METRICS[name]?.noiseBandPct ?? bandPct` — per-metric overrides global. If the global band is later raised above 20%, latency_ms and cost_usd become MORE sensitive than global (counter-intent: high-variance metrics should be wider, not tighter). The Math.max fix from PR #475 should be restored: `Math.max(METRICS[name]?.noiseBandPct ?? 0, bandPct)`.

Provenance: PR #477 (2026-06-23), ghost-implementing closed issue #462; see expired [[regression-band-override-semantics]] and [[reviewer-concern-follow-on-pr]] (PR #475 fix that was reverted).
