gitignore-inline-comment-silently-expose.l0: Git .gitignore: inline # comments extend the pattern—they don't comment it, leaving the file silently un-ignored.
gitignore-inline-comment-silently-expose.l1: PR #505 (2026-06-24): .voice-token and .voice-env had trailing inline # comments in .gitignore (e.g. `.voice-env # key loader`). Git supports ONLY full-line comments (line starting with #)—no inline comments. The # and all text after it become part of the filename pattern, so it matches nothing. Both credential files were silently un-ignored. Fix: move comments to a preceding line. Verify: `git check-ignore -v <file>` before assuming a sensitive file is safely ignored.
gitignore-inline-comment-silently-expose.value:
Pattern: inline # text in .gitignore is part of the filename pattern, not a comment.

WRONG (silently un-ignores the file):
  .voice-env # key loader file
  .voice-token # oauth token

RIGHT:
  # key loader file
  .voice-env
  # oauth token
  .voice-token

Git .gitignore syntax (per git-scm.com/docs/gitignore):
- A '#' on a line whose first non-whitespace character is '#' = comment (line ignored).
- A '#' anywhere else is a literal character in the pattern.
- Blank lines are also ignored.
- No inline comment syntax exists.

Consequences of the mistake:
- The file is NOT ignored — `git status` shows it as untracked.
- A `git add .` or `git add -A` can silently commit secrets, tokens, or env files.
- The bug is invisible at write time: `.gitignore` editors don't warn, `git add` doesn't warn.

Checklist when adding sensitive files to .gitignore:
1. Use only full-line comments (# on its own line above the pattern).
2. After adding, verify with `git check-ignore -v <file>`. A missing line in the output means the file is NOT ignored.
3. For files already staged/committed accidentally: `git rm --cached <file>`, then re-commit.

Provenance: PR #505 (2026-06-24) — fix(voice): durable key loading + Gemini-TTS 429 cooldown + Kokoro pre-warm.

provenance.source: PR #505 (2026-06-24): fix(voice): durable key loading + Gemini-TTS 429 cooldown + Kokoro pre-warm

voice-text-test-tier-split.l0: Voice+logic agents: separate test tiers — text/logic (default, zero voice tokens) + voice (opt-in, logic stubbed).
voice-text-test-tier-split.l1: PR #513 spec: agents bundling STT/TTS + reasoning run every logic test through the voice pipeline, burning unnecessary tokens. Fix: a text tier (text-in→text-out, voice module never instantiated, zero STT/TTS tokens, deterministic) + a voice tier (audio fixtures, logic layer stubbed, no reasoning tokens). A test-tier selector (file pattern, flag, or tag) runs text by default; voice requires an explicit opt-in. CI: text tier on every change, voice tier on a narrower trigger (voice-surface changes / scheduled / manual).
voice-text-test-tier-split.value:
Apply when an agent bundles two concerns: a reasoning/logic layer and an audio layer (STT/TTS).

(1) VOICE/LOGIC SEAM: expose the agent's text-in→text-out logic independently of the audio wrapper. This seam is the enabling abstraction — everything else depends on it. Test this layer first; absence means no isolation is possible.

(2) TEXT TIER (default): test files drive the logic layer with text inputs, assert text outputs, and never import or instantiate the voice module. Zero STT/TTS tokens. Deterministic and fast. All logic/reasoning/routing/tool assertions belong here — no coverage loss from the split.

(3) VOICE TIER (opt-in): test files exercise STT/TTS transforms and audio round-trip wiring. The logic layer is stubbed/fixture-driven; live audio uses pre-recorded fixtures — no reasoning tokens and deterministic by default. One explicitly-gated live e2e case is acceptable.

(4) TIER SELECTOR: file pattern (e.g. test/text/ vs test/voice/), flag, or tag. Default test command → text tier only. Explicit opt-in → voice tier (or both). The mechanism is test-framework-agnostic; uses existing test tooling.

(5) CI WIRING: text tier runs on every PR/change (fast, cheap). Voice tier runs only on a narrower trigger: voice-surface changes, scheduled, or manual dispatch.

(6) KEY ASSERTIONS:
  - Logic tests invoke no voice module: a text-tier run instantiates no STT/TTS and records zero voice token usage.
  - Default excludes voice: the default command runs text tier only; voice requires explicit opt-in.
  - Voice tier runs independently: exercises STT/TTS with logic stubbed, no full-reasoning tokens.
  - Seam isolation: logic layer is fully testable text-in→text-out with voice module absent.
  - Determinism: text-tier is deterministic; voice-tier fixture tests are deterministic (except the explicit gated e2e).

(7) OUT OF SCOPE: changing agent logic, the voice pipeline, or its UX; optimizing voice latency; removing voice tests; new token-accounting infrastructure. The split restructures HOW agents are tested, not what they do.

Provenance: spec PR #513 (2026-06-24), closes Issue #512.

provenance.source: PR #513: spec: Separate voice and text module testing for agents (#512)

stream-json-tao-trajectory-tradeoff.l0: stream-json gives per-turn TAO events; terminal type:result line has same result/usage as json envelope.
stream-json-tao-trajectory-tradeoff.l1: PR #519 spec (Issue #517): `--output-format json` emits only the terminal envelope (one-shot). `--output-format stream-json` emits newline-delimited events per turn: thought, tool_use, tool_result, run_end. The terminal line with type:"result" carries the same result and usage fields as the json envelope — parseResultEnvelope adapts to scan for it instead of parsing the whole stdout. Cost capture (summary, tokens, cost_usd, num_turns) is byte-identical. Disable via AGENT_MESH_TRAJECTORY_DISABLED=1 to revert to the simpler json parse path.
stream-json-tao-trajectory-tradeoff.value:
Pattern for adding per-turn observability (TAO trajectories) to delegated claude -p runs without changing cost capture.

(1) Switch the worker spawn from `--output-format json` to `--output-format stream-json`. The stream is newline-delimited JSON; each line is one event.

(2) Classify each line by type: thought / tool_use / tool_result / run_end. Append classified events to `<run-id>-trajectory.jsonl` in AGENT_MESH_LOG_DIR — streaming, so partial runs leave a partial but valid file.

(3) Adapt parseResultEnvelope: scan the NDJSON stream for the last line with `type: "result"`. This carries the same `result` and `usage` fields (summary, total_cost_usd, num_turns, api_ms) as the json envelope. Aggregate log and cost capture write unchanged.

(4) Disable path: AGENT_MESH_TRAJECTORY_DISABLED=1 reverts to `--output-format json` and the original single-object parse. Behavior is byte-identical to pre-change.

(5) Partial trajectory on failure: a timeout or crash leaves a valid partial JSONL without a `run_end` event — diagnosable because it shows exactly where the run stopped.

(6) Sanitize and cap: secret-bearing tool results are redacted; oversized payloads are truncated with `truncated:true`. Per-event sanitization at the stream consumer, not at parse time.

(7) Tradeoff origin: the 2026-06-13 cost-capture spec chose `json` over `stream-json` because 'the worker is one-shot; we want the terminal envelope.' TAO trajectory capture is the first reason to prefer `stream-json` — the terminal `type:result` line makes the switch reversible: adapt the parser, preserve all downstream behavior unchanged.

Provenance: PR #519 / Issue #517 (2026-06-25).

provenance.source: PR #519: spec: delegation trajectory capture — TAO JSONL from delegated runs (Issue #517)

pre-do-planning-pass.l0: Gate do-mode delegation with plan_peer_task — ask-mode dry-run returning PlanResult before any writes.
pre-do-planning-pass.l1: `plan_peer_task({peer, task})` on agentmesh_peerbridge force-spawns ask mode (caller cannot override) with `<planning-pass>` suffix. Returns `PlanResult {files_to_change, risks, ambiguities, confidence, summary, usage}`. Routing: `confidence:"low"` or non-empty `ambiguities` → human escalation. Failure-as-data: malformed output → `status:error`, never a throw. Three layers: plan (pre-do) → do-mode → post-do validation (#509). Spec 2026-06-25 / PR #521 / Issue #518.
pre-do-planning-pass.value:
Apply when an orchestrator or concierge is authorizing a do-mode delegation and you want a structured approval gate before any writes occur.

Verb: `plan_peer_task({peer, task})` on the agentmesh_peerbridge MCP server.
- Framework force-spawns ask mode regardless of caller intent (anti-spoof invariant).
- Framework appends `<planning-pass>` suffix to the task text; caller's task is treated as data.
- Returns PlanResult schema: `{ status, plan: { files_to_change, risks, ambiguities, confidence: "high"|"medium"|"low", summary, usage } }`.

Routing rules:
- `confidence: "low"` OR non-empty `ambiguities` → escalate to human (do not auto-execute).
- `confidence: "high"` + acceptable risk → auto-proceed if config policy permits.
- Default: surface plan for explicit confirm on task board / concierge.

Decision outcomes:
- Approve → `delegate_to_peer` in do-mode with the same task. Post-do validation (#509) and branch isolation (#458) still apply.
- Reject → close task; zero writes occurred at any point.

Failure-as-data: malformed/non-JSON worker output → `{ status: "error" }` or `confidence: "low"` with raw preserved. Never a throw.

Three-layer composition (additive, independent):
1. plan_peer_task — pre-do ask-mode probe; surfaces scope before writes.
2. delegate_to_peer do-mode — executes writes after approval.
3. post-do validation (#509) — declarative validators run after do-mode.

Cost rationale: ask-mode planning pass is materially cheaper than a failed or misdirected do-mode run; a planning pass that surfaces a misunderstood task short-circuits before any file writes occur.

Registry gate: same marker-validated registry.json gate as delegate_to_peer — unknown peer rejected before any spawn.

Spec: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-25-idea-pre-do-planning-pass-ask-mode-dry-run-that-returns-a-st-design.md. PR #521 / Issue #518 (2026-06-25).

provenance.source: PR #521: spec: idea: pre-do planning pass — ask-mode dry-run that returns a structured impact plan before any writes (#518)

mir-null-passrate-no-data-alert.l0: null/absent eval passRate = no-data hard finding, not silent pass; blocks all-green rollup (PR #528)
mir-null-passrate-no-data-alert.l1: MIR aggregate.js: if behavior.passRate is null/absent, emit hardFinding({ id: 'behavior:overall:no-data' }) + summary.behavior.status = 'no-data'. Healthy rollup requires all required tiers to have a real passing measurement. Renderer shows ⚠ no-data (not —). Mirrors SRE absent() / Grafana no-data-is-alert convention. Applies to all tiered passRates. Distinct from fail: missing run ≠ measured failure.
mir-null-passrate-no-data-alert.value:
Spec PR #528 (2026-06-25): When the behavioral eval tier produces no measurement (behavior.passRate === null), the MIR must surface an explicit no-data signal — a hard finding plus a distinct status field — rather than silently equating absence of data with a pass.

Implementation pattern:
(1) aggregate.js: guard typeof behavior?.aggregate?.passRate === 'number'; if false, emit hardFinding({ id: 'behavior:overall:no-data', cluster: 'behavior-no-data', evidence: { trace: 'behavior scorecard absent or passRate missing' } }).
(2) summary.behavior.status = typeof passRate === 'number' ? 'ok' : 'no-data'. Additive field; backward-compatible.
(3) render.js: show ⚠ no-data for status==='no-data', not —.
(4) No change to policy.js, baseline.js, issues.js, collect.js — ledger handles the new hard finding automatically.

Why hard (not soft): no measurement is epistemically equivalent to unknown health, not a pass. A behavioral regression during an outage of the eval harness would be invisible if null = pass.

Risk: meshes that never run L2 evals get a daily no-data issue — this is the correct signal. Ledger dedup ensures one open issue per cluster at a time.

The pattern generalizes: any required tier with null passRate should follow the same classifier (no-data state). v1 targets behavior tier; perf/adversarial extension is a separate issue.

provenance.source: PR #528 (2026-06-25): spec: MIR: treat null behavior passRate as a no-data alert, not a silent pass (#526)

eval-scaffold-sensitivity-attribution.l0: Eval pass-rate drops may be scaffold noise — run named variants before attributing as genuine regression.
eval-scaffold-sensitivity-attribution.l1: SWE-bench 2026: same model, 5 harnesses → 50.2%–55.4% (5.2pp scaffold variance; harness design dominates model choice). For agent_mesh: genuine-regression (all variants drop) vs scaffold-sensitive (one variant drops) vs healthy (stable). v1 variants: baseline (default), no-roster (AGENT_MESH_EVAL_NO_ROSTER=1), terse-task (first sentence only). --scaffold-variants flag (default: baseline only — no change to existing usage). Cross-variant classifier: all-drop → genuine-regression; one-drop → scaffold-sensitive; stable → healthy. PR #532.
eval-scaffold-sensitivity-attribution.value:
Pattern for attributing eval pass-rate movements to scaffold vs. genuine regressions:

(1) Define a named scaffold-variant catalog (eval/scaffold-variants.mjs). v1 variants:
  - baseline: current default (regression-locked); full roster + full task text.
  - no-roster: suppress peer roster injection (AGENT_MESH_EVAL_NO_ROSTER=1); full task.
  - terse-task: full roster; task truncated to first sentence only.
Each variant specifies env overrides and a task transform; probes/assertions are byte-identical across variants.

(2) Expose a --scaffold-variants <names> flag (default: 'baseline' only — no change to existing invocations). Passing 'baseline,no-roster,terse-task' runs each scenario under all three variants.

(3) Cross-variant classifier (pure function):
  - all variants drop → genuine-regression (warrants framework investigation)
  - only one variant drops (others stable) → scaffold-sensitive (framing issue, not a real regression)
  - stable across all variants → healthy
  Apply per-scenario.

(4) Scorecard renderer: emit per-variant column + aggregate + classification tag. The MIR daily review shows 'scaffold-sensitive' vs 'genuine-regression' tags so a reviewer can attribute before investigating.

(5) Default unchanged: no --scaffold-variants flag → only baseline runs; existing usage is identical.

Attribution rule: a drop classified 'scaffold-sensitive' does NOT warrant a framework investigation — the fix target is the scenario framing, not the code. A 'genuine-regression' drop warrants investigation.

Why this matters: a 5.2pp pass-rate variance from scaffold alone was measured in SWE-bench 2026 (same model, five harnesses; digitalapplied.com, June 2026). Without attribution, a reviewer cannot distinguish a real regression from scaffold noise and wastes investigation time.

Existing seam: AGENT_MESH_EVAL_NO_ROSTER is now the 'no-roster' catalog entry — no longer ad-hoc.

Testing: (a) default unchanged (no flag → baseline only); (b) variant application verifiable against fixtures; (c) probes invariant across variants; (d) classifier: scaffold-sensitive / genuine-regression / healthy; (e) catalog pure (same input → same output); (f) scorecard columns + tag present; (g) unknown variant name rejected with clear message.

Provenance: PR #532 / Issue #531 (2026-06-26), Analyst-authored spec.

provenance.source: PR #532: spec: idea: eval scaffold variant seams — harness sensitivity instrumentation (#531)

mir-one-sided-classification.l0: MIR: reserve `*-regression` for unfavorable moves (dPct<0) — favorable wins classify as `improvement`.
mir-one-sided-classification.l1: PR #536 added `classify()` in `applyBaseline`: gates on `tier==='soft' && /-regression$/.test(cluster) && dPct>0` → relabels to `improvement`. Without this, direction-correct wins (latency −17%) appeared in `perf-regression` cluster, polluting agent triage of `mir-*.json`. Hard findings (tier!=='soft') are never reclassified. `/-regression$/` also covers `behavior-regression` (passRate) — broader than perf only. The fileable gate fires only on `dPct<0`; this fix targets the raw MIR JSON surface, not filed issues.
mir-one-sided-classification.value: For any eval pipeline using `deltaPct` (signed toward 'better'): (1) ONE-SIDED GATE: `dPct>0` → favorable; `dPct<0` → unfavorable. Reserve `*-regression` cluster for unfavorable moves only — favorable wins label as `improvement`. Never funnel both directions through `perf-regression`. Mirrors Bencher one-sided upper-boundary alerts and Criterion's Improved/Regressed/No-change distinction. (2) TIER GUARD: only `tier==='soft'` findings reclassified — hard findings (`test-failure`, `security-invariant`, `delegation-failure`) never change cluster regardless of delta sign. (3) PATTERN SCOPE: `/-regression$/` covers BOTH `perf-regression` AND `behavior-regression` (passRate) — a point fix for perf alone silently misses the behavior cluster. (4) INSERTION POINT: `classify(f, dPct)` belongs inside `applyBaseline` AFTER `deltaPct` is computed and BEFORE the finding is returned — pure function, no side effects. (5) TESTS NEEDED: (a) favorable move must NOT appear under `perf-regression`; (b) unfavorable stays in regression cluster; (c) hard findings never reclassified even when `dPct>0`; (d) no-baseline (`dPct=null`) left unchanged. (6) LATENT RISK: `cluster` is now polysemous (regression OR improvement). If an improvement were ever fileable, issue title templates emit contradictions — guard: improvements stay non-fileable since `dPct>0` never satisfies `dPct<0`. Provenance: PR #536 (2026-06-26), closing Issue #515.

provenance.source: PR #536: MIR: classify direction-correct metric moves as improvements, not perf-regression warnings (#515)

per-peer-thinking-effort-spawn-flag.l0: Per-peer effort budget: `thinking_effort` in registry.json → `--effort` at spawn; same anti-spoof path as `model`.
per-peer-thinking-effort-spawn-flag.l1: PR #537: Set `thinking_effort: "low"|"medium"|"high"|"xhigh"|"max"` on a registry.json peer entry to control extended thinking for that peer. Registry-gate + anti-spoof: calling model cannot inject effort via tool args — only operator's registry value reaches `--effort`. Invalid value → `status:error`, no spawn. Absent → no `--effort` flag (regression lock). Parallels `per-peer-model-tier-spawn-flag` (PR #467): second operator spawn flag on the same 5-layer threading path.
per-peer-thinking-effort-spawn-flag.value:
Threading path (5 layers): (1) `normalizePeer` preserves field (undefined when absent); (2) `peer-bridge.js` sets `agentmesh/thinking_effort` in A2A metadata; (3) `stdio-server` extracts from metadata; (4) `delegateTask` validates against allowed set {low,medium,high,xhigh,max}; (5) `buildClaudeInvocation` appends `--effort <level>` to `claude -p` argv iff set.

Design: v1 is static/operator-configured per peer — no dynamic per-task override, no model-facing injection. Invalid value returns `status:error` before any spawn (failure-as-data, no spawn). Absent → no flag (backward-compatible, argv byte-identical).

Test coverage: absent → no `--effort` (regression lock); each valid level (max, xhigh, low) → correct argv; invalid value ("ultra") → `status:error`; peer-bridge metadata carries field iff set; normalizePeer preserves field.

Pattern: two operator-controlled spawn flags (`--model` PR #467 and `--effort` PR #537) follow the same 5-layer path. Adding a third (e.g. `--max-turns`) requires the same threading: normalizePeer → A2A metadata field → stdio-server extraction → delegateTask validation → buildClaudeInvocation flag injection. The invariants are identical: registry-only source (anti-spoof), invalid → status:error, absent → no flag (regression lock). Provenance: PR #537 (2026-06-26), closes Issue #530.

provenance.source: PR #537: feat(delegate): per-peer thinking effort budget (issue #530)

registry-gate-spawn-flag-template.l0: Per-peer spawn flag template: registry.json field → 4-layer thread; absent=no-op; invalid=error; never model-facing.
registry-gate-spawn-flag-template.l1: Confirmed by two independent flags: `--model` (PR #467/2026-06-23) and `--effort` (PR #533/2026-06-26). New per-peer spawn flags follow the same template: (1) `normalizePeer` preserves the field (undefined when absent), (2) `peer-bridge.js` passes to `delegateTask` options, (3) `delegate.js` threads to `buildClaudeInvocation`, (4) flag appended to argv iff defined; absent → argv unchanged (regression lock). Invalid value → `status:error`, not throw. Field never in model-facing tool description or tool args — registry is sole authority. Multiple flags on the same peer compose independently.
registry-gate-spawn-flag-template.value:
Apply when adding any new per-peer spawn-time flag (e.g. --model, --effort, future --max-turns, --parallel-tool-calls). The four-layer threading path is always identical:

(1) src/a2a/registry.js normalizePeer: accept the optional field, default undefined when absent. If the flag must not be overridable by peer.env, add it to RESERVED_BRIDGE_ENV (anti-spoof invariant).

(2) src/a2a/peer-bridge.js: look up peer.<field> from the normalized registry entry and pass it into delegateTask's options argument.

(3) src/delegate.js: destructure the option from opts and thread it to buildClaudeInvocation.

(4) src/delegate-invocation.js buildClaudeInvocation: when the option is defined, push ['--flag', value] onto the claude -p argv; when undefined, leave argv unchanged. Absent = byte-identical to today (regression lock).

Validation (pure): accept only a known enum set or validated format; any other value → { error } (failure-as-data); the validation helper is the hermetic test seam for invalid inputs.

Error handling: an invalid value causes status:error result, not a throw — consistent with how the mesh treats any spawn failure.

Anti-spoof: the field never surfaces in the model-facing tool description or any tool argument. A delegating model cannot influence the flag value — only the operator-authored registry entry can.

Composition: multiple independent flags on the same peer entry (e.g. model + thinking_effort) thread through the four layers independently without coupling.

Test matrix for each new flag:
- Absent field → no argv change (regression lock)
- Valid value → flag threaded to argv
- Invalid value → status:error (not throw)
- End-to-end: registry.json → normalizePeer → peer-bridge → delegate → buildClaudeInvocation carries value unbroken
- Anti-spoof: tool-arg attempt to set/raise value has no effect
- Composition: peer with two flags threads both correctly and independently
- Run-record: per-hop metrics reflect the new flag's effect (e.g. higher token use for --effort max)

Provenance: --model (PR #467, 2026-06-23), --effort (PR #533, 2026-06-26). Pattern crystallized after two independent instantiations following identical structure.

provenance.source: PR #533 (2026-06-26): spec: per-peer extended thinking budget — registry thinking_effort field threads --effort to delegated workers (#530)

fs-rm-git-repo-enotempty-retry.l0: fs.rm tmp git repo: add maxRetries+retryDelay against ENOTEMPTY races from git background pack writers (Node 20).
fs-rm-git-repo-enotempty-retry.l1: Node 20's fs.rm races with git background pack writers (post-index-pack, gc --auto) that keep .git/objects/pack file handles open after the foreground command resolves. { recursive:true, force:true } alone is insufficient — force only swallows ENOENT, not ENOTEMPTY/EBUSY/EPERM. Adding maxRetries:3, retryDelay:50 gives a 50/100/150ms linear backoff that clears the race for small seed repos. Belt-and-suspenders: disable gc at repo creation with git -c gc.auto=0; this prevents the background worker from spawning entirely.
fs-rm-git-repo-enotempty-retry.value: Define a shared RM_OPTS = { recursive: true, force: true, maxRetries: 3, retryDelay: 50 } constant and apply it to EVERY rm call site in the harness teardown — error path, success path, AND the .claude/projects/<enc> transcript cleanup — a missed site can still flake. Apply uniformly (a shared constant makes coverage trivial to audit). Optionally add git -c gc.auto=0 at commit/init time to make the fix deterministic rather than probabilistic for seed repos large enough to hit the gc threshold. Note: retries are probabilistic — if a background gc worker runs longer than ~300ms (3×100ms total) the ENOTEMPTY can recur. In practice, tiny one-commit seed repos don't cross the loose-object threshold triggering gc --auto, so retries are very likely sufficient. Provenance: PR #543 / Issue #542 (2026-06-26, adversarial-harness ENOTEMPTY on ubuntu/node-20).

provenance.source: PR #543 / Issue #542: flake: adversarial-harness ENOTEMPTY race on tmp/.git/objects/pack cleanup

mir-policy-direction-before-magnitude.l0: MIR policy: deltaPct>0 (signed toward better) → perf-improvement; never check magnitude until direction is unfavorable.
mir-policy-direction-before-magnitude.l1: deltaPct() in metrics.js is signed toward 'better': positive = improvement (latency lower, precision higher), negative = regression. Before PR #535, policy.gate() ignored direction — every soft finding with a baseline was classified perf-regression/warning. Issue #515 caught latency −17%, cost −7%, precision +3.4% filed as regression warnings. Fix: gate direction first. deltaPct>0 → perf-improvement (info, fileable:false). deltaPct≤0 within noise band → null severity, fileable:false. deltaPct≤0 past noise floor → perf-regression (warning, fileable:true). No baseline → null severity.
mir-policy-direction-before-magnitude.value:
Apply when reading or modifying MIR policy classification in src/mesh-improvement/policy.js.

The MIR deltaPct convention: deltaPct() in src/mesh-improvement/metrics.js returns a signed-toward-better value. Positive = metric improved in its own direction (latency went down, precision went up). Negative = regression. The sign already encodes direction — no separate direction field needed at the gate layer.

The four-state direction-aware model (post-PR #535):

(1) No baseline — cold-start:
  Condition: baseline == null
  Result: { fileable: false, severity: null }
  No historical reference point; cannot classify.

(2) Favorable direction:
  Condition: deltaPct > 0
  Result: { cluster: 'perf-improvement', fileable: false, severity: 'info' }
  The metric moved toward better — NEVER a perf-regression, regardless of magnitude.
  Short-circuit BEFORE calling isRegression() — isRegression only handles the unfavorable case.
  Rendered in the '## Improvements this run' section in MIR markdown.

(3) Unfavorable, within noise band:
  Condition: deltaPct ≤ 0 AND !isRegression(name, deltaPct, noiseBandPct)
  Result: { fileable: false, severity: null }
  Moved in the wrong direction but below the noise floor. Statistically insignificant.

(4) Unfavorable, past noise floor:
  Condition: deltaPct ≤ 0 AND isRegression(name, deltaPct, noiseBandPct)
  Result: { cluster: 'perf-regression', fileable: true, severity: 'warning' }
  The ONLY state that files a regression issue. isRegression() checks |dPct| > noiseBand.

Anti-pattern to avoid: calling isRegression() on ALL findings with a baseline (the pre-fix behavior). This conflates improvements with regressions and pollutes the regression signal reviewers triage.

Related: [[mir-noise-band-reversion]] (Math.max override semantics in isRegression), [[mir-null-passrate-no-data-alert]] (null passRate = no-data, not pass).

Provenance: PR #535 (2026-06-26), closing Issue #515.

provenance.source: PR #535: fix(mir): classify direction-correct metric moves as improvements

eval-zero-cases-tristate.l0: Zero eval cases: passRate:null not 0 + exit 2 (zeroIsError) + hard MIR finding — no-measurement ≠ 0% pass rate.
eval-zero-cases-tristate.l1: PR #549 fixed a silent failure mode: zero eval cases returned passRate:0 (looks like all-failed) rather than null (nothing ran). Four-part fix: (1) casesExecuted===0 → passRate:null; (2) exitCode({zeroIsError:true}) returns 2 for zero cases so CI fails loudly; (3) aggregate.js emits hard finding behavior:overall:zero-cases (cluster behavior-coverage) when scorecard present but casesExecuted===0; (4) MIR render uses tristate: numeric passRate | ⚠ zero-cases (0 trials scored) | — (no scorecard data). casesExecuted field added to scorecard aggregate. PR #549 / Issue #548 (2026-06-26).
eval-zero-cases-tristate.value:
Apply when writing or reviewing any eval/scorecard harness.

Three-state model for eval pass rate (post-PR #549):

(1) No scorecard / tool not run:
  Condition: scorecard absent
  passRate: null; casesExecuted: absent
  Render: — (no-data)
  No finding generated.

(2) Scorecard present, zero cases executed:
  Condition: casesExecuted === 0
  passRate: null (NOT 0 — 0 means measured-and-all-failed)
  exitCode({zeroIsError:true}): 2 → fails CI
  Hard finding: behavior:overall:zero-cases (cluster behavior-coverage)
  Render: ⚠ zero-cases (0 trials scored)
  Use: scorecard ran but produced nothing — a coverage gap, not a pass/fail result.

(3) Scorecard present, cases executed:
  Condition: casesExecuted > 0
  passRate: number 0.0–1.0
  exitCode: 0 pass / 1 fail (existing behavior)
  Render: numeric e.g. 83%

Anti-pattern: passRate:0 for zero cases misleads callers into thinking all tests failed when nothing ran — a pipeline gating on passRate===1 fails for the wrong reason with no diagnostic signal.

Field casesExecuted must be emitted in the aggregate output so the MIR aggregator can read it without re-running the eval. Backward-compat: read ?? trials when old scorecards lack the field.

The {zeroIsError:true} opt-in on exitCode() keeps the gate explicit: callers that test zero-trial behavior omit it and get exit 0 for a valid empty run.

Same pattern applies to any metric where no-data is qualitatively different from 0: test coverage %, latency baselines, budget utilization.

Related: [[mir-null-passrate-no-data-alert]] (null passRate = no-data at MIR layer).

Provenance: PR #549 (2026-06-26), Issue #548 (auto-closed on merge).

provenance.source: PR #549: feat(#548): behavior eval coverage gate — casesExecuted field and zero-case exit

agent-cap-prompt-over-shell-step.l0: Agent retry caps: implement as prompt instruction (agent counts own comments) — not a shell step needing jq/gh-api.
agent-cap-prompt-over-shell-step.l1: PR #550 (#508): prior circuit-breaker spec (#439) used a shell step with jq/gh api to count comments — hit test failures and env fragility. Fix: move cap logic into agent prompt ("count prior Spec-ready-attempt-N comments; if ≥3, escalate, stop"). Agent reads own thread naturally; no jq/gh-api dep. Simpler shell = fewer failure modes. Generalizes: any durable-state cap derived from issue comments belongs in the prompt, not a pre-run shell step. See also: attempt-cap-circuit-breaker-durable-state (the what).
agent-cap-prompt-over-shell-step.value:
When implementing a circuit-breaker or retry cap that reads from issue comments in an agent workflow:

PREFERRED — prompt instruction:
  Add to the agent YAML prompt (after the task description):
  "Before re-authoring: count all prior Spec-ready-attempt-N comments on this issue. If count >=3: (1) add needs-human label, (2) post ONE deduped escalation comment containing CIRCUIT BREAKER sentinel — check for that marker first to avoid duplicates, (3) stop without re-triggering. Reset on observed green push."
  The model reads gh issue comments as part of its normal context-gathering; no extra tooling.

AVOID — shell preflight step:
  A step that does: count=$(gh api /repos/.../issues/N/comments | jq "select comment contains marker | length")
  Fragile: jq must be installed, gh api must be authorized, JSON parsing must match exact body format. Env differences break it silently.

WHY IT MATTERS:
  PR #439 spec described the durable-state pattern correctly but left implementation to a shell step. PR #550 found the shell version had test failures and env fragility; switching to prompt instruction resolved both without adding complexity.

SECONDARY PATTERN (same PR — PAT preflight via curl):
  Use: curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" -H "Authorization: token $PAT" URL
  HTTP status only, no jq. Missing/invalid PAT -> immediate exit 1 before spending API budget.

Provenance: PR #550 (2026-06-26), closes #508.

provenance.source: PR #550 (2026-06-26): fix(intake): circuit breaker, DEV_MESH_PAT preflight, and 529 soft-exit (#508)

cross-network-sync-multi-transport.l0: Cross-network sync runbook: ≥3 topologies (tailnet, LAN, mac-pull) — topology is a deploy-time config, not code.
cross-network-sync-multi-transport.l1: PR #595 productionized PR #592's outbox/syncer by adding: (1) serve-capture CLI (agent-mesh serve-capture) — auth-gated (MAC_CAPTURE_TOKEN), binds 127.0.0.1, CAPTURE_HOST widens to LAN/tailnet; (2) syncer_daemon.py — reconcile-on-start then interval-drain; (3) docs/voice-capture-deploy.md — three transport options for box→Mac sync. Live proof: 131 ideas drained; 3 captured offline → all pending → synced on reconnect, none lost.
cross-network-sync-multi-transport.value:
When shipping any edge-to-home or device-to-desktop sync feature:

(1) SERVE-CAPTURE ENDPOINT PATTERN
  - Bind the receiving server to 127.0.0.1 by default; widen only via CAPTURE_HOST.
  - Require an auth token (MAC_CAPTURE_TOKEN) on every request — even on LAN.
  - Factor as `buildCaptureServer(args, env)` so all config comes from injected args/env, making the server hermetically testable without a real tailnet.
  - Require idempotency on the receiver: the sender may POST the same ULID multiple times on retry; the receiver deduplicates by id.

(2) SYNCER DAEMON PATTERN
  - Reconcile-on-start: at daemon boot, reset any record stuck in 'in-flight' to 'pending' (a prior crash may have halted mid-POST).
  - Interval-drain: on every tick, send all pending records to CAPTURE_URL. Transient failures (network down) keep the record 'pending' and retry next tick. Permanent failures (4xx) move to 'dead'. Never silently drop.
  - Background / decoupled: never block the capture turn loop on outbound sync.

(3) MULTI-TOPOLOGY RUNBOOK
  Always document ≥3 network paths in the same runbook. Operators pick at install time based on their network:
  - Tailscale serve: `tailscale serve --bg --http=80 <port>`. NAT-piercing, WireGuard-encrypted. Set CAPTURE_URL to the MagicDNS http:// URL. Zero firewall config needed.
  - Direct LAN: set CAPTURE_HOST to the Mac's LAN IP; set CAPTURE_URL to http://<LAN-IP>:<port>. Fast, simple, works only on same subnet.
  - Mac-pull / reverse-pull: firewalled box → add a read-only export endpoint on the box; Mac polls it on a cron. Useful when the box is behind CG-NAT with no inbound connection possible.
  The server code is topology-agnostic — CAPTURE_URL is the only env var that selects the topology.

(4) LIVE PROOF REQUIRED
  Verify Acceptance B before shipping: capture ≥3 ideas while the Mac is offline → all 'pending' → sync when Mac returns → none lost. Documents the offline-durability guarantee independent of topology.

Related: [[capture-first-voice-pipeline]] (PR #592 — the outbox/syncer/agent core that this productionizes). Provenance: PR #595 (2026-06-28).

provenance.source: PR #595: Voice demo → product: serve-capture CLI + syncer daemon (ideas reach the mesh)

multi-brain-run-record-parity.l0: New AI brain runtimes: run records must match delegate.js field names — health-model silently ignores mismatched agents.
multi-brain-run-record-parity.l1: PR #620 fix: gemini-agent.js initially omitted started_at/finished_at/route/root from its START and FINAL records. health-model.js bumpSeen() and activity-stats filters key on these exact field names — Gemini runs were silently dropped from health views and activity dashboards. The Important finding in the final review wave caught it; adding the missing fields made Gemini runs visible alongside Claude runs with no other change.
multi-brain-run-record-parity.value:
When integrating a new AI brain runtime into the mesh (Gemini, or any future provider):

(1) Audit your run records against delegate.js's schema. The START record must include: started_at, route, root. FINAL records (both done and timeout paths) must include: finished_at, route, root in addition to the standard result fields.

(2) The health monitoring stack keys on these EXACT field names. health-model.js bumpSeen() and the activity-stats filters use them for: showing the agent in the Vital Signs view, counting recent runs, inferring idle/dead state, computing cognition metrics. A mismatch — even a minor rename like start_time vs started_at — causes complete silent exclusion.

(3) The failure mode is silent: no error, no warning, no partial data. Health views simply show no activity for the agent. Particularly deceptive when a Gemini brain routes alongside Claude: Claude runs appear correctly while Gemini runs vanish.

(4) Test coverage: add assertions for the exact field names in your brain agent test, not just presence of a record:
  - START record: assert record.started_at, record.route, record.root
  - FINAL record: assert record.finished_at, record.route, record.root
  PR #620 had zero tests for run-record field names — the gap was caught only by a code review finding.

(5) Scope: applies each time a new monitoring consumer is added too. If health-model gains a new field it reads (e.g., a latency field), existing brain runtimes silently omit it — re-audit each new field against all registered brain implementations.

Provenance: PR #620 (2026-06-28), Important finding in the final review wave — zero test coverage for run-record field names at the time.

provenance.source: PR #620: feat(voice): logic in the mesh — Gemini-brained concierge agent + logic-free voice ingress

brain-runtime-failure-as-data.l0: New brain runtimes: wrap the brain-loop in try/catch — an unhandled rejection crashes the Node A2A server process.
brain-runtime-failure-as-data.l1: PR #622: runGeminiAgent's brain-loop lacked try/catch. A transient Gemini API error propagated as an unhandled rejection — Node exits on these, crashing the A2A server (one bad turn killed the voice concierge until supervisor restart). Fix: wrap the brain-loop in try/catch → return status:'error' Task (failure-is-data invariant). Also: brain usage was silently dropped by runBrainLoop — null in all records, breaking observability parity with the Claude path. The voice ingress already handles non-COMPLETED Tasks with a fixed fallback, so no ingress change needed.

provenance.source: PR #622: fix(brains): gemini agent failure-as-data (don't crash the A2A server) + usage threading

worker-env-explicit-allowlist.l0: Worker env: replace `...process.env` spread with an explicit allowlist — OS basics, auth, framework, extension.
worker-env-explicit-allowlist.l1: PR #588: `...process.env` spread silently forwarded every parent-process credential to delegated workers. Fix: explicit allowlist — OS basics (PATH, HOME, USER, SHELL, LANG, TZ, TMPDIR), Claude auth/config (CLAUDE_*, ANTHROPIC_*), framework (AGENT_MESH_*), operator extension (AGENT_MESH_WORKER_ENV_ALLOWLIST). Unset vars omitted; extension vars forwarded verbatim. Companion: MAX_CHILD_OUTPUT_CHARS bounds spawnFile() stdout/stderr; keeps the tail; records truncated:true — prevents OOM before timeout fires on noisy/adversarial workers.
worker-env-explicit-allowlist.value:
Apply when any subprocess, delegate, or worker env is constructed from the spawning process's env.

(1) Replace `...process.env` with an explicit allowlist helper. Categories to include:
  - OS basics: PATH, HOME, USER, SHELL, LANG, LC_ALL, TZ, TMPDIR, XDG_RUNTIME_DIR
  - Claude/Anthropic auth+config: any key starting with CLAUDE_ or ANTHROPIC_
  - Framework vars: any key starting with AGENT_MESH_ (except those in RESERVED_BRIDGE_ENV)
  - Operator extension: parse AGENT_MESH_WORKER_ENV_ALLOWLIST (comma-separated) and forward those specific keys. Validate names against a safe-identifier regex before forwarding.
  Omit all other vars — unset keys are not included in the child env.

(2) Anti-pattern (before fix): `{ ...process.env, AGENT_MESH_ROOT: path, ... }` — silently includes every parent-process secret. A single DATABASE_URL, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, or GITHUB_TOKEN in the parent's env reaches every delegated worker and can be exfiltrated via ask-mode web tools or logged in error output.

(3) Defense in depth: the worker is already confined by path-guard, ask-mode read-only tools, and the single-writable-root invariant. The env allowlist adds an additional layer: credentials absent from the child's env cannot be exfiltrated or leaked regardless of what the worker does.

(4) Companion pattern — bound subprocess output:
  Apply MAX_CHILD_OUTPUT_CHARS to any spawnFile() / child_process call whose stdout/stderr is buffered before timeout:
  - Keep the TAIL of oversized output (most recent content is most diagnostic).
  - Record { stdout_truncated: true } / { stderr_truncated: true } on the result object.
  - Prevents OOM when a noisy or adversarial child floods stdout before the timeout fires.
  - Without this: a child emitting 100MB before timing out exhausts process memory with no diagnostic benefit.

(5) Required tests:
  - Allowlisted vars (PATH, CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN, AGENT_MESH_DEPTH) present in child env.
  - Non-allowlisted vars (DATABASE_URL, MY_SECRET) absent.
  - AGENT_MESH_WORKER_ENV_ALLOWLIST='MY_SECRET' forwards MY_SECRET to child.
  - Extension var names validated (names with shell metacharacters rejected).
  - MAX_CHILD_OUTPUT_CHARS truncation: oversize stdout → tail preserved + truncated:true flag.

Provenance: PR #588 (2026-06-27), fix(security): harden delegated worker environment and output capture.

provenance.source: PR #588: fix(security): harden delegated worker environment and output capture

stt-hallucination-ingress-gate.l0: STT hallucination is an ingress problem — add an energy+duration audio gate before any transcriber, not a bigger model.
stt-hallucination-ingress-gate.l1: PR #624: recognizers (whisper/Gemini) cannot return empty on short/quiet/noisy clips — they invent plausible text. Three fixes: (1) energy+duration audio gate before any transcriber (returns '' on rejection — biggest impact); (2) `condition_on_previous_text=False` for whisper (single biggest lever; stops cross-chunk drift); (3) whisper-large-v3 over turbo for Mandarin. A bigger model (flash→pro, turbo→large) does NOT fix hallucination on genuine silence/noise — the gate does.
stt-hallucination-ingress-gate.value:
Apply when building any voice pipeline with STT (whisper, Gemini, Deepgram, etc.).

**Root cause**: Recognizers cannot return empty — on short/quiet/noisy clips they generate plausible-sounding text (coin-counting, stray phrases). This is a model constraint, not a model quality problem. A bigger model produces the same hallucination.

**Three-layer fix (priority order):**

(1) PRE-TRANSCRIPTION AUDIO GATE — highest-value fix:
  `has_speech(chunk) → False → return ''` before calling any STT.
  Gate criteria:
  - Frame energy (RMS) below noise floor → reject
  - Voiced duration < min_speech_ms → reject
  Implement as a PURE, unit-tested function (numpy-optional stdlib fallback).
  Five test cases: too-short, silence, quiet-hiss, loud-voiced-accepted, tiny-blip-rejected.
  Apply to EVERY transcriber path (whisper AND gemini) — the gate centralizes protection.
  The ingress already skips empty transcripts, so '' is a safe return.

(2) `condition_on_previous_text=False` for whisper — single biggest lever:
  Without it, whisper conditions each decode chunk on the prior chunk's text, so an initial hallucination propagates ("cross-chunk drift"). Setting False makes each chunk decode independently.
  Full anti-hallucination param set: `beam_size=1, temperature=0, no_speech_threshold=0.6, log_prob_threshold=-1.0, compression_ratio_threshold=2.4` + tuned `vad_parameters` (Silero VAD).

(3) Model choice for non-English:
  whisper-large-v3 over whisper-turbo for Mandarin — turbo is measurably weaker on Chinese.
  Cloud code-switch engines (Deepgram, Chirp) lack Mandarin; on-box whisper is correct.
  large-v3 handles zh+en code-switching via its single multilingual decoder.

**Gemini STT note**: flash-lite and flash are identical on clean audio — both hallucinate on noise. A `?stt=gemini` override is acceptable as a manual A/B path; whisper+gate is the primary.

**Required test coverage**: `test_audio_gate.py` — must be stdlib-only (numpy-optional) to run in every CI environment. Five cases minimum.

Provenance: PR #624 (2026-06-28).

provenance.source: PR #624 (2026-06-28): fix(voice): kill STT hallucination — whisper-large-v3 primary + VAD/energy gate + anti-hallucination params

whisper-initial-prompt-multilingual.l0: Whisper STT: set initial_prompt for domain/multilingual audio — prevents subtitle hallucination and loanword garbling.
whisper-initial-prompt-multilingual.l1: PR #626: real phone audio (zh+en) exposed two failures synth tests missed: (1) subtitle hallucination on unclear clips (Whisper subtitle-training → '歡迎收看…'); (2) Mandarin-locked decoding garbles English loanwords (mesh→Mess, agent→安). Fix: initial_prompt with domain hint kills both. float16 over int8 lifts accuracy when VRAM allows. Synth clips pass without the fix — always test on real saved clips.
whisper-initial-prompt-multilingual.value:
Apply when deploying Whisper for real-world audio — especially multilingual (zh+en code-switching), technical domain, or unclear input.

(1) ROOT CAUSE: Whisper is trained on subtitle/transcript data. Without context, it pattern-matches unclear audio to subtitle phrases. In multilingual decoding it can lock to one language and transliterate loanwords phonetically (mesh→Mess, agent→安). Synth (pure-zh, pure-en) test clips PASS without the fix — failures emerge only on real-user, real-mic, unclear audio.

(2) initial_prompt — primary fix:
  Set the `initial_prompt` arg on every `model.transcribe()` call. Include:
  - The primary spoken language (e.g. '普通话')
  - Domain-specific loanwords the user regularly speaks (e.g. 'mesh/agent/PR/issue 等英文词')
  Keep it concise — it seeds the decoder's context window, not a full prior transcript.
  Without it: subtitle hallucination + loanword garbling. With it: both disappear on the same clips.

(3) PRECISION — secondary gain:
  Prefer float16 over int8 when GPU VRAM allows. int8 quantization trades accuracy for speed; float16 catches phonetic nuance the quantized model misses.

(4) VERIFY ON REAL AUDIO:
  Save a representative real-mic clip (e.g. last_in.wav). Run A/B before and after:
  - No prompt → observe failure mode (hallucination or garbling)
  - With prompt → verify correct output
  Synth clips are insufficient — they lack the spectral irregularities that trigger the failure.

(5) REMAINING LIMITS: homophones (e.g. 胎压→泰亚) are inherent ASR limits, not addressable by initial_prompt. Document as accepted trade-offs.

Related: [[stt-hallucination-ingress-gate]] (PR #624 — audio gate + anti-hallucination params, the complementary pre-transcription fix).

Provenance: PR #626 (2026-06-28) — fix(voice): whisper float16 + domain initial_prompt (kill subtitle hallucination + keep loanwords).

provenance.source: PR #626: fix(voice): whisper float16 + domain initial_prompt — kill subtitle hallucination + keep loanwords

stt-audio-preprocess-before-engine-swap.l0: Gain-normalize audio before swapping STT engines — clean-benchmark MER doesn't transfer to real degraded phone audio.
stt-audio-preprocess-before-engine-swap.l1: PR #628: SenseVoice-Small scored 6.71% MER on ASCEND benchmark — better than Whisper on paper — but was WORSE on real user phone audio (dropped content, event/emotion tags). Gemini looped catastrophically. Root cause: a real turn was 25.8s for a ~3s phrase, ~half noise (low SNR). A single RMS gain-normalization of the same clip lifted Whisper from garbled to coherent output. Fix: preprocess_audio() (RMS normalize to 0.15 + light energy trim) in serve_turn.py before STT call; raw audio still saved for diagnosis. Rolling clip-saver (last 24 turns) enabled the decisive real-data A/B.
stt-audio-preprocess-before-engine-swap.value:
Apply when a voice pipeline is underperforming and you're considering an STT model or engine swap.

(1) PREPROCESS AUDIO FIRST — cheaper and often more effective than a model swap:
RMS gain-normalization + light energy trim on the incoming WAV:
  rms = sqrt(mean(pcm^2)) + 1e-9
  scale = min(TARGET_RMS / rms, MAX_GAIN)  # target ~0.15; cap gain at 6x
  pcm_out = clip(pcm * scale, -1, 1)
Light energy trim: find voiced frames (RMS > noise_floor ~0.015, window ~20ms), trim to first/last voiced + 5-frame margin.
Always save raw audio (pre-preprocess) for diagnosis — never overwrite the raw with the cleaned copy. If preprocess fails, fall back to raw silently.

(2) BENCHMARK MER DOES NOT TRANSFER TO REAL DEGRADED AUDIO:
Clean-benchmark scores (ASCEND, LibriSpeech, etc.) measure noise-free or near-field audio. Real phone PTT audio can have 25s of hold time for 3s of speech — half the clip is ambient noise. A model scoring better on a benchmark can be worse on your specific noise profile.
Decision rule: before committing to a model swap, run the candidate engine on your own saved real clips and compare transcripts side by side.

(3) CLIP-SAVER IS THE DIAGNOSTIC FOUNDATION:
Without saved real clips you cannot A/B-test or prove a fix:
  - last_in.wav: always overwrite with the most recent raw turn
  - clips/<timestamp_ms>.wav: rolling buffer, keep last N=24 turns (~2MB total)
  - Save raw audio (pre-preprocess), not the cleaned copy — raw is ground truth
A real-clip A/B (same clip, old path vs. new path, compare transcripts) is the only trustworthy gate before shipping a change. Synth clips pass fixes that real clips fail.

(4) RELATED GATES (complement, don't replace):
  [[stt-hallucination-ingress-gate]] (PR #624): energy+duration gate before transcription — rejects clips with no speech (silence/noise-only). Different problem from low-SNR valid speech.
  [[whisper-initial-prompt-multilingual]] (PR #626): initial_prompt — prevents subtitle hallucination and loanword garbling.
  Gain-normalize handles valid speech that is too quiet or variably loud. The gate handles clips with no speech. The prompt handles decoder context bias.

(5) WHEN TO ESCALATE TO ENGINE SWAP:
If after gain-normalization + gate + initial_prompt the recognizer still produces garbled output on representative saved clips, benchmark alternative engines — always validating on your own saved clips, not just the benchmark dataset. Beware: Gemini may loop catastrophically on real noisy audio (observed: 25 repetitions of a filler phrase) even when it performs well on clean benchmarks.

Provenance: PR #628 (2026-06-28) — fix(voice): audio gain-normalize + trim before STT.

provenance.source: PR #628: fix(voice): audio gain-normalize + trim before STT (real-clip testing says keep whisper, fix the audio)

voice-stt-language-default.l0: STT language default: use the language reliable on worst-case real hardware; surface others via per-session toggle.
voice-stt-language-default.l1: PR #630: Chinese STT on the in-car phone path was unreliable (acoustic noise, degraded audio) despite working in lab; English STT on the same pipeline was flawless. Fix: default lang='en' across all three layers (PWA app.js, agent_livekit DEFAULT_LANG, serve_turn handler). The 中 toggle still switches per session. Pending noise-cancellation (LiveKit+Krisp) may restore Chinese as default later.
voice-stt-language-default.value:
Apply when a voice feature supports multiple STT languages deployed to a real-world environment (car, factory, outdoor, degraded phone audio):

(1) TEST ON THE ACTUAL DEPLOYMENT HARDWARE — not a laptop mic or quiet room. Lab-passing STT is not production STT. In-car phone audio has acoustic noise, gain issues, and compression that break non-English phoneme detection while English survives.

(2) DEFAULT = RELIABLE LANGUAGE. Set the STT default to the language verified end-to-end on the worst-case real hardware. Never default to the majority language, preferred locale, or most-requested feature when it fails on the primary deployment path.

(3) PER-SESSION TOGGLE, not persistent override. Users who need a non-default language switch in-session via a chip/button; the toggle writes to session state, not localStorage as a permanent override — one new session restores the default.

(4) THREE-LAYER DEFAULT: wire the default at ALL layers — (a) PWA JS initial value, (b) server-side DEFAULT_LANG, (c) request-handler lang fallback. A missing layer means different clients see different languages unexpectedly.

(5) DEFER NON-DEFAULT LANGUAGE UNTIL FIXED. If a language's STT is unreliable and the fix is known (noise-cancellation upgrade), ship with the reliable default and file an issue referencing the fix. Do not hold the product on a broken default pending a future upgrade.

Provenance: PR #630 (2026-06-28).

provenance.source: PR #630 (2026-06-28): fix(voice): default to English interaction (Chinese STT unreliable on degraded phone audio)

cross-machine-a2a-url-peer.l0: url-peer in registry.json = cross-machine A2A; macOS→reverse-SSH for inbound; headless claude token in launchd env.
cross-machine-a2a-url-peer.l1: PR #632: cross-machine A2A (box→Mac) needed zero new framework code — registry.json url field + HttpClientSession (PR #151) already handle HTTP peers. Two live prerequisites: (1) Network path: macOS firewall+Tailscale block inbound → Mac initiates reverse-SSH tunnel (`ssh -R <box-P>:127.0.0.1:<mac-Q> box`). (2) Headless auth: CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN must be in launchd EnvironmentVariables on the target machine — SSH session env doesn't persist to daemon spawns. Transport proven live; auth was the only remaining blocker. Test: localhost tunnel-equivalent, hermetic.
cross-machine-a2a-url-peer.value:
Apply when wiring cross-machine agent delegation in an A2A mesh.

(1) NO NEW CODE: Check for existing HTTP peer support first. If registry.json normalizeRegistry already handles url: peers and HttpClientSession already exists, cross-machine A2A is WIRING only — not a new framework feature. The framework-level work is done; operational work remains.

(2) NETWORK PATH — macOS inbound-blocked pattern:
  macOS application firewall blocks inbound. Userspace Tailscale (non-kernel extension) does NOT route inbound on macOS. If the target is a Mac:
  - Mac INITIATES a reverse SSH tunnel: `ssh -f -N -R <box-P>:127.0.0.1:<mac-Q> <box-host>`
  - The box WSL/Linux client reaches the Mac agent at `127.0.0.1:<box-P>`
  - Registry entry: `{ "<agent>": { "url": "http://127.0.0.1:<box-P>/rpc" } }`
  This is the only reliable inbound path from box to Mac given macOS restrictions.

(3) HEADLESS AUTH — launchd env, not SSH session:
  `claude -p` in a daemon/launchd service does NOT inherit SSH session env. `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` set in an SSH session is NOT visible to processes started by launchd. Fix: add the token to the launchd job's EnvironmentVariables plist key (or systemd Environment directive), not the shell env.
  Recovery: `claude setup-token` → set CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN in the target machine's OS service config.

(4) HERMETIC TEST = PRODUCTION TRANSPORT:
  A test using localhost for both caller and server is transport-equivalent to the real reverse-SSH tunnel (both bind 127.0.0.1). The hermetic test fully validates the A2A HTTP path; the tunnel is pure network plumbing.

(5) EXISTING SECURITY INVARIANTS INHERITED:
  - ask-only: peer-bridge refuses non-ask before any HTTP send
  - recursion + depth: threaded as X-AgentMesh-Path/Depth headers (see a2a-http-dual-transport-dispatch)
  - failure-as-data: unreachable peer → ok:false Task, never throws
  - marker-validated registry: x-agentmesh-generated:true required

Provenance: PR #632 (2026-06-29).

provenance.source: PR #632: feat(a2a): cross-machine A2A bridge — concierge delegates to remote agents over HTTP peers (tested)

brain-loop-relay-on-hop-exhaustion.l0: Brain loop on maxHops: force one tool-free final turn to relay — empty-returning loop silently drops all gathered data.
brain-loop-relay-on-hop-exhaustion.l1: In src/brains/loop.js `runBrainLoop`, when the model exhausts `maxHops` by looping on tool calls, the prior code returned ''  — silently discarding everything gathered. The brain (gemini-2.5-flash in the voice concierge) had collected a peer answer via `ask_peer` but never relayed it as text. Fix: on exhaustion, run ONE final turn with tools=[]. A model that still won't produce text stays gracefully empty (no throw). Paired with a concierge prompt update: 'call at most one tool, then answer; relay ask_peer's result right away'. PR #634 (2026-06-29).
brain-loop-relay-on-hop-exhaustion.value: When building a multi-turn brain loop that calls tools (e.g. ask_peer, web_search), the loop must not silently return ''  on hop exhaustion — it must force one final tool-free turn so the model produces a relay response. Pattern: detect hops >= maxHops, strip the tools list, and make ONE final model call. The model has already gathered context across prior turns; a tool-free prompt compels it to produce a summary/relay instead of another tool call. Still-looping models on the forced turn stay gracefully empty (no throw — returning ''  is a valid fallback for callers rendering partial results). Pairing: update the brain's system prompt to converge early ('call at most one tool, then answer; relay the peer's result right away') so maxHops is rarely reached. Implementation: src/brains/loop.js runBrainLoop + test/brains-loop.test.js. PR #634 (2026-06-29), fixing voice-concierge empty-reply where gemini-2.5-flash looped on tools across all 4 hops without relaying the ask_peer answer.

provenance.source: PR #634: fix(brains): relay peer answers on hop exhaustion + concierge prompt

cross-machine-bridge-as-scripts.l0: Commit cross-machine bridge setup as idempotent scripts — hand-built machine state is unreproducible from a fresh clone.
cross-machine-bridge-as-scripts.l1: PR #636: the Mac-mesh/box-concierge A2A bridge lived only on two machines — hand-built launchd jobs, tunnel.sh, registry.json, supervisor env patches. A fresh clone could not recreate it. Fix: committed mac-bridge-setup.sh + box-bridge-setup.sh as idempotent generators plus a README. Idempotency verified: supervisor patch uses grep-before-append; emitted scripts pass bash -n. See [[cross-machine-a2a-url-peer]] for the wiring protocol; this covers persisting the operational glue.
cross-machine-bridge-as-scripts.value:
Apply when any cross-machine A2A bridge setup (tunnels, serve scripts, registries, supervisor patches) has been hand-built on individual machines.

(1) COMMIT AS IDEMPOTENT SCRIPTS:
  Generators overwrite their output files from current parameters — no residual state.
  Pattern from PR #636 (scripts/bridge/):
  - mac-bridge-setup.sh: generates serve-agents.sh (serve N agents over serve-a2a-http, self-restarting) + tunnel.sh (reverse ssh -R per agent) + loads KeepAlive launchd jobs.
  - box-bridge-setup.sh: writes registry.json (trust marker + HTTP url peers) + patches supervisor to export MESH_ROOT/MESH_CEILING, then restarts.

(2) IDEMPOTENCY CHECKLIST:
  - File generators: overwrite from params on each run (naturally idempotent).
  - Patching steps (e.g. supervisor env): use grep-before-append guard: grep -q EXPORT_VAR file || echo export X=Y >> file.
  - Verify: run script twice, diff output — no duplicate exports, no new files.

(3) VALIDATION:
  - Emitted scripts: pass bash -n syntax check.
  - Emitted registry.json: valid JSON with x-agentmesh-generated:true marker (required by readManagedRegistry).

(4) README MUST COVER:
  Topology diagram, why reverse tunnel (macOS firewall blocks inbound), port map, headless-claude token step, verify commands, add/remove-an-agent procedure, caveats.

(5) COMPLEMENT:
  [[cross-machine-a2a-url-peer]] (PR #632) covers the framework wiring — url: peer in registry.json, HTTP transport, CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN in launchd env. This lesson covers PERSISTING that wiring as reproducible scripts.

Provenance: PR #636 (2026-06-29).

provenance.source: PR #636: feat(bridge): reproducible cross-machine A2A bridge setup (Mac + box scripts + docs)

dep-injection-default-consistency.l0: DI tool defaults must wire the SAME source as sibling tools — empty default creates visible tool-set disagreement.
dep-injection-default-consistency.l1: `list_mesh_agents` in `src/brains/tools.js` defaulted `listAgents` to `async () => []`. `gemini-agent.js` passed bare `{}` deps so no real backend was wired. `ask_peer` already defaulted to the marker-validated registry — the two tools diverged and the voice brain reported "no agents" even though the registry and agents were correct. Fix: default `listAgents` to `createBridge().listPeers()`, the same source as `ask_peer`, degrading to `[]` on failure.
dep-injection-default-consistency.value:
When a module exposes multiple DI-injectable tools over the same data source (e.g. list-peers and ask-peer in a voice brain), ALL tool defaults must wire to the same canonical reader. A silent `async () => []` default on the list op while the ask op already reads the real source creates user-visible inconsistency: the brain truthfully reports an empty list while send operations work fine, appearing as a config or mesh problem.

Fix pattern (brains/tools.js):
  const listAgents = deps.listAgents || (() => createBridge().listPeers().catch(() => []));
Same source as `ask_peer`, degrades to [] on failure — never throws into the brain loop.

Test lesson: tests that always inject the dependency via `deps` leave the DEFAULT path uncovered. For every DI function with a production default, add at least: (1) a test where the dep is NOT injected but the backend (marked registry) works — the tool must return real data; (2) a test where the dep is NOT injected and the backend is absent (markerless/stale registry) — must return []. PR #638 added exactly these two cases; full L0 suite 280/280.

Provenance: PR #638 (2026-06-29).

provenance.source: PR #638: fix(concierge): list_mesh_agents reads the real registry (was always empty)

stt-entity-roster-bias.l0: STT initial_prompt: roster names beat homophones — generic mesh vocab (mesh/agent/PR) lets 'coder' transcribe as 'road'.
stt-entity-roster-bias.l1: PR #640: whisper only had generic terms (mesh/agent/PR/issue) — no entity names. Fix: stt_prompt(lang) in stt_bias.py includes the live agent roster; STT_BIAS_WORDS env extends at runtime (registry never drifts). Agent layer: concierge system.md now calls list_mesh_agents to fuzzy-resolve unknown peer names before claiming a peer is absent. Pattern: every new mesh entity (agent, code-name, team handle) must be added to STT_BIAS_WORDS.
stt-entity-roster-bias.value:
PR #640 root cause: stt_bias.py initial_prompt had only generic mesh vocabulary (mesh, agent, PR, issue). Whisper has no phoneme-level constraint — it picks the most plausible word, so coder → road/Corder. Two-layer fix:

(1) Ingress (stt_bias.py): stt_prompt(lang) returns fixed mesh terms PLUS the live agent roster. STT_BIAS_WORDS env var (comma-separated) extends at runtime without a code change — supervisor injects live registry names here. serve_turn.py calls stt_prompt(lang) instead of an inline dict.

(2) Agent (concierge/prompts/system.md): brain now calls list_mesh_agents on any unknown name and fuzzy-resolves to closest peer (one-word confirm if ambiguous); never claims no agents without checking first.

Fix generalizes: any domain entity that phonetically collides with common words (agent names, project codes, team handles) must appear in the STT bias vocabulary.

provenance.source: PR #640 (2026-06-29): fix(voice): bias STT + concierge toward the real agent roster

pure-routing-helper-no-infra-imports.l0: Extract routing logic as a pure helper — no infra imports — so it's unit-testable without GPU, cloud, or LiveKit.
pure-routing-helper-no-infra-imports.l1: PR #642: turn_route.build_turn_url(base, backend, lang) (no livekit import) made STT backend selection (Gemini vs. GPU) testable with 5 pytest cases — no hardware required. Before: routing logic lived inside agent_livekit.py, entangled with LiveKit room objects and GPU references. After: pure module, primitives in/out. Edge cases in pure code: strip ?query so UI choice wins; unknown backend → gemini; invalid lang → drop.
pure-routing-helper-no-infra-imports.value:
Apply whenever a routing function selects between infra-backed services (cloud API vs. GPU, model A vs. B).

(1) SEAM RULE: if the routing decision is f(primitives) → primitive, extract it as a pure module. Zero imports from infra code (SDKs, sockets, GPU drivers, cloud clients).

(2) SIGNATURE PATTERN (from turn_route.build_turn_url):
  - Accepts: base_url, backend ('gemini'|'local'), lang (str|None)
  - Returns: complete URL
  - Edge cases in pure code:
    * Strip pre-existing ?query — caller's explicit choice always wins
    * Unknown backend → safe default, not a throw
    * Invalid/None input → omit, not a throw

(3) TEST VECTORS (5 from PR #642): strip trailing slash; unknown backend → default; invalid lang dropped; existing ?query stripped; valid inputs produce correct URL.

(4) CALLERS STAY THIN: infra-entangled callers import the pure module and pass data. Only test the pure module.

(5) GENERALIZES: microservices endpoint selection, model routing, feature-flag dispatch. If the routing key is user data (UI toggle, env var), a pure function is the right layer.

Provenance: PR #642 (2026-06-28) — feat(voice): in-PWA STT model selector, default Gemini.

provenance.source: PR #642 (2026-06-28): feat(voice): in-PWA STT model selector, default Gemini

gh-retry-transient-backoff.l0: Daemon `gh` calls: classify-then-retry transient net failures (1s/5s/15s, N=3); auth/4xx/unknown → fail fast.
gh-retry-transient-backoff.l1: PR #647: 3 consecutive 'error connecting to api.github.com' crashes crashed label-repair-sweep → hard failure → heartbeat escalation → false-positive needs-human issue. No retry layer meant one blip = one failure. PR #652 (spec for #649) defines: wrap gh calls in a pure classify-then-retry wrapper: transient signals (DNS blip, timeout, connection refused) → retry up to N=3 with fixed 1s/5s/15s backoff; auth/4xx/validation → fail immediately; unrecognized/ambiguous → non-retryable (conservative default). N=3 hard-failure escalation unchanged — retry absorbs blips, not outages.
gh-retry-transient-backoff.value:
Three pure components for daemon gh call resilience (PR #652 spec pattern):

(1) Error classifier (pure, table-testable) — `(ghError) → { retryable: bool }`: transient network signals ('error connecting', DNS, timeout, connection refused) → retryable:true; auth / 4xx / validation → retryable:false; unrecognized/ambiguous → retryable:false (conservative default — fails fast, not retried).

(2) Backoff schedule — fixed delays: attempt 1→1s, attempt 2→5s, attempt 3→15s. Bounded at N=3. No jitter in v1.

(3) Retry wrapper — wraps every gh invocation: attempt → on failure, classify → if retryable: wait + retry up to N; else: fail immediately. On exhaustion: throw original error (unchanged for the heartbeat). Succeeds silently if any attempt succeeds.

Call-site adoption: route ALL daemon/builtin gh calls through the wrapper (label-repair-sweep, issue-sweep, and any other daemon builtin calling `gh issue list/edit/create`).

Config: N=3 / [1s,5s,15s] defaults; optional disable flag → single-attempt behavior.

Key invariants:
- Escalation preserved: N=3 consecutive hard failures still escalate the heartbeat exactly as before.
- Original-error fidelity: the error surfaced after retry exhaustion is the original gh error.
- gh list/edit/create are safe to retry on connection failure (request likely never reached the API).

Test coverage:
- Transient then success: network error on attempt 1, success on attempt 2 → result returned, no escalation.
- Persistent transient: all N attempts fail → original error thrown; escalation proceeds.
- Auth not retried: gh auth failure → immediate fail, zero retries.
- 4xx not retried: 404/403/422 → immediate fail.
- Unrecognized not retried: ambiguous error → immediate fail (conservative).
- Backoff schedule: delays follow 1s/5s/15s exactly.
- Disable flag: single-attempt behavior when set.

Provenance: PR #652 (2026-06-29), spec for Issue #649.

provenance.source: PR #652 (2026-06-29): spec: daemon sweep: add retry/backoff around gh API calls to handle transient network failures (#649)

prompt-depth-fan-out-latency.l0: Prompt-deepening PRs multiply cold-start latency across parallel fan-outs — run 3x-disjoint perf cell before/after.
prompt-depth-fan-out-latency.l1: PR #620 deepened each agent's --append-system-prompt (Gemini brain layer). In a 3-peer disjoint fan-out all three peers cold-start concurrently, compounding the penalty: 28,634ms→31,372ms (+9.6%). Recovery: Anthropic prompt caching (cache_control: ephemeral on the static prefix), but the claude CLI's --append-system-prompt is plain text — cache_control is an API message-block property. Phase 0 feasibility gate: confirm whether the CLI exposes a cache_control breakpoint mechanism before implementing. Spec: PR #661 / issue #659 (2026-06-29).
prompt-depth-fan-out-latency.value:
Pattern: prompt-depth latency tax in parallel fan-outs.

Any PR that deepens agent system prompts (brain layer, framed memory, injected roster) extends the static prefix on every claude -p spawn. In a parallel fan-out (e.g. 3x-disjoint — three peers cold-started concurrently), the cold-start penalty multiplies: PR #620 caused +9.6% on 3x-disjoint latency (28,634ms→31,372ms). Routing precision/recall were unchanged; zero wasted hops — pure cold-start cost.

Recovery: cache_control: {"type": "ephemeral"} on the shared static prefix. In a 3-peer disjoint fan-out all peers share the same static prefix; warm-cache reads cut ~90% input-token cost and lower TTFT.

Feasibility gate (Phase 0): the claude CLI passes system prompt as plain text via --append-system-prompt. cache_control is an Anthropic API message-block property — not a text concept. Before implementing: confirm whether the CLI exposes a cache_control breakpoint mechanism (structured file, annotation, env var). If not, approach must switch to direct Anthropic API calls or rely on CLI-internal TTL caching.

Pre-warm: a pre-warm call writes the prefix cache before the fan-out fires, converting cold writes to reads — best-effort only, never blocks the fan-out (failure-is-data).

Detection: run node scripts/eval-perf.mjs on the 3x-disjoint cell before AND after any prompt-deepening PR.

Provenance: PR #661, issue #659 (2026-06-29).

provenance.source: PR #661: spec: perf: enable Anthropic prompt caching on static agent system prompts (#659)

headless-claude-omit-allowedtools.l0: Omitting --allowedTools in headless claude -p → model attempts full tool set → ≥5 denials → health gate RED.
headless-claude-omit-allowedtools.l1: PR #664 / Issue #663: dogfood claude -p had no --allowedTools; model reached for Bash/Write in an observability-only run. 12 auto-denials exceeded BLOCKED_DENIALS_THRESHOLD (5) → classifyRunHealth flagged the run "blocked" (RED) despite a valid result. Fix: --allowedTools "Read,Grep,Glob" matches the stated read-only intent. Structural lint in test/dev-mesh-workflow.test.js asserts dogfood declares an explicit allowlist. Related to but distinct from [[bash-tool-wildcard]] (wrong Bash arg form vs. no list at all).
headless-claude-omit-allowedtools.value:
Pattern: every headless 'claude -p' invocation MUST declare --allowedTools scoped to what the task actually needs.

(1) DENY MECHANISM: in headless -p mode, the model receives the FULL default tool set unless restricted. If the prompt describes a read-only task but the model reaches for Bash/Write, those calls auto-deny. Denials accumulate. classifyRunHealth fails the run as 'blocked' (RED) at ≥ BLOCKED_DENIALS_THRESHOLD (5) — even if the run finishes without error and produces valid output.

(2) MINIMUM SCOPE RULE: set --allowedTools to the minimum the task needs:
  - Observability/read-only: "Read,Grep,Glob"
  - With node dry-run: "Read,Grep,Glob,Bash(node:*)"
  - Do-worker: "Read,Grep,Glob,Edit,Write,Bash(git:*),Bash(gh:*)" etc.
  Never omit --allowedTools from any automated claude -p invocation.

(3) STRUCTURAL GUARD: add a test assertion that each automation workflow declares --allowedTools. Without it, accidental omission is invisible until the health monitor reddens. Pattern (test/dev-mesh-workflow.test.js):
    assert.match(dogfood, /--allowedTools/, 'dogfood must declare an explicit tool allowlist');
  Pair with an exact workflow-count guard so adding a new workflow forces a deliberate update.

(4) REVIEWER NOTE (PR #664 review): --allowedTools fixes READ denials but cannot fix a missing --mcp-config. The peer bridge (agentmesh_peerbridge) requires --mcp-config to load; delegation gaps are a separate failure mode not addressed by this fix.

(5) DISTINCT FROM [[bash-tool-wildcard]]: that lesson covers using Bash(cmd:*) vs. Bash(cmd) (bare form blocks all args). This lesson covers the orthogonal case: OMITTING --allowedTools gives the model an unconstrained surface where it guesses what to reach for.

Provenance: PR #664 (2026-06-29), Issue #663.

provenance.source: PR #664 (2026-06-29): bug: dogfood claude -p has no --allowedTools, causing 12 permission denials that red the health monitor
